seasons.ncaaTournament: Sweet Sixteen (#1 seed East, lost to Florida 87-78)
seasons.tagline: The Exodus Reboot. Brand, Avery, Maggette, and Langdon all gone — seven freshmen arrive, Carrawell becomes ACC Player of the Year, and Duke finishes #1 anyway.
seasons.overview:
Nobody expected this. After the heartbreak of the 1999 national championship game loss to UConn, Duke suffered the most devastating talent drain in program history. Elton Brand, William Avery, and Corey Maggette became the first Blue Devil underclassmen to leave early for the NBA Draft. Trajan Langdon graduated. Chris Burgess transferred. Only 34.2% of minutes played and 26.0% of scoring returned from the 37-2 juggernaut.

The roster was rebuilt overnight. Seven freshmen arrived — the most on any Duke team in school history — headlined by point guard Jason Williams from Plainfield, New Jersey, power forward Carlos Boozer from Juneau, Alaska, and wing Mike Dunleavy Jr. from Portland, Oregon. They joined returning junior Shane Battier, who moved from defensive specialist to two-way star, and senior Chris Carrawell, the St. Louis kid who'd been waiting four years for his moment.

The season opened with a disaster. Duke dropped back-to-back games at Madison Square Garden in the Coaches vs. Cancer Classic — an 80-79 overtime loss to Stanford where the Blue Devils shot a K-era worst 28.2% from the field, followed by a 71-66 loss to defending national champion UConn. Duke fell to 0-2 for the first time since 1958-59. Krzyzewski joked about hoping his athletic director wasn't a fair-weather fan.

Then the Blue Devils caught fire. They rattled off 18 consecutive victories, a streak that revealed the character of the team. Carrawell was magnificent, posting 13 20-point games and becoming the ninth Duke player to win ACC Player of the Year. Battier earned his second consecutive National Defensive Player of the Year award (shared with Cincinnati's Kenyon Martin), averaging 17.4 points while leading the team with 70 blocks and 20 charges taken. He became Duke's first men's basketball Academic All-American since Quin Snyder in 1989.

The freshmen were extraordinary. Williams led the team in assists at 5.4 per game, scoring 14.5 points while starting every game. Boozer powered his way to 13.0 points and 6.3 rebounds, earning ACC All-Freshman honors. Dunleavy averaged 10.1 off the bench. As a class, the freshmen accounted for 46% of Duke's scoring, 44% of the rebounding, and 56% of the assists. Duke led the nation in scoring at 88.0 points per game for the second consecutive season.

All five starters averaged double figures — a hallmark of K's best teams and something no previous Coach K team had achieved. A sixth player, Dunleavy, came off the bench averaging double digits too. Nate James, the junior tri-captain, doubled his scoring from the prior year to 11.0 points per game and anchored the lineup late in the season.

Duke rolled to its fourth consecutive outright ACC regular season championship at 15-1, its second consecutive ACC Tournament title with Jason Williams earning tournament MVP as a freshman, and the #1 ranking in the final AP poll. In the NCAA Tournament, the Blue Devils survived a brutal second-round test against Kansas — Battier blocked eight shots (a career-high and Kansas opponent record), Boozer grabbed 13 rebounds, and Carrawell's clutch free throws sealed a 69-64 win despite Duke shooting 2-of-17 from three. But the run ended in the Sweet Sixteen at the Carrier Dome in Syracuse, where fifth-seeded Florida overwhelmed Duke 87-78. Duke committed 22 turnovers, Williams went 6-of-20, and Boozer fouled out with 11 points. Florida would go on to reach the national championship game.

The loss was a bitter end to Carrawell's career — 116 victories, the second-most by any player in ACC history. He became just the second Duke player (after Grant Hill) to record 1,000 points, 600 rebounds, 100 blocks, and 300 assists. But the season had accomplished something remarkable: it proved Duke could lose four starters to the NBA and still finish #1 in America. The freshmen who arrived in 1999 — Williams, Boozer, Dunleavy, Casey Sanders — would be the foundation of the 2001 national championship team. The exodus didn't break Duke. It rebuilt it.

unc.story: Bill Guthridge's Tar Heels were struggling at 13-8, but Chapel Hill is always dangerous. Duke raced to a stunning 41-24 halftime lead, dominating in every phase. Then Carolina stormed back. Joseph Forte hit a three near the buzzer to force overtime and the Dean Dome erupted. But this team had character — Duke had already won three overtime games this season. Carrawell scored 23, Battier had 25 points and 10 rebounds, and freshman Boozer scored seven of Duke's overtime points. The 90-86 win extended Duke's ACC record to 30 consecutive regular season victories. Carrawell captured the team's identity perfectly: 'Last year's team was great, but when it got tough we lost. This year's team is building some character in overtime.' It was UNC's fourth home loss of the season — the most at the Smith Center since 1951-52.

Carrawell's final game against North Carolina, and he and Battier made it a masterpiece. Duke stormed to a 42-27 halftime lead and never let Carolina back in. Battier erupted for 30 points — his highest total against UNC — while Carrawell added 21. Freshman Joe Forte scored 24 for the Tar Heels but it wasn't enough against Duke's suffocating defense. The 90-76 victory was Duke's fifth win in the last six meetings with Carolina, and it gave Carrawell a fitting sendoff at Cameron: the ACC Player of the Year, standing at center court, having won four consecutive outright ACC regular season championships.

theGame.title: Gator Aid: Florida Ends the Run
theGame.headline: Fifth-seeded Florida overwhelms top-ranked Duke in the Sweet Sixteen, ending Chris Carrawell’s career and exposing the limits of the rebuilt roster.
theGame.story:
It was supposed to be the year Duke exorcised its tournament demons — the year the #1-ranked Blue Devils, who'd lost the national title game to UConn just twelve months earlier, would complete the journey. Instead, it ended in Syracuse against a Florida team nobody outside Gainesville believed in.

The Gators, seeded fifth in the East, were led by Teddy Dupay, Donnell Harvey, Udonis Haslem, and Brett Nelson — a roster built on depth and athleticism rather than star power. They'd already upset fourth-seeded Illinois 93-76 in the previous round, and they came to the Carrier Dome ready to run.

Duke fell behind 40-33 at halftime, struggling with turnovers and cold shooting. Williams, the brilliant freshman point guard, went 6-of-20 from the field and 1-of-9 from three-point range while committing five turnovers. Boozer fouled out with just 11 points. Florida had 21 assists to Duke's 12, moving the ball with a purpose that exposed Duke's youth.

Battier was magnificent in defeat — 20 points, 9 rebounds, 4 blocks — and Carrawell contributed 16 points and 3 steals in his final college game. But the turnovers were lethal: 22 for the game, leading to easy Florida baskets in transition. Brett Nelson scored 15 points with three triples, and the Gators shot 46.9% from the floor.

The 87-78 final ended Chris Carrawell's Duke career at 116 victories — tied for third-most in ACC history at the time — and snapped Duke's 26-game NCAA Tournament East Region winning streak. Florida would go on to beat Oklahoma State and North Carolina before falling to Michigan State in the national championship game.

For Duke, the loss stung. But the freshmen who struggled that night in Syracuse — Williams, Boozer, Dunleavy, Sanders — would return the next season and, led by senior Battier, would win the 2001 national championship. The Florida loss wasn't the end. It was the last lesson before the coronation.

accTourney.story: Duke opened ACC Tournament play in Charlotte with a win over NC State, a team they'd already beaten in a wild 92-88 overtime thriller during the regular season. The Blue Devils were the #1 seed for the fourth consecutive year.
A semifinal win over Wake Forest set up the championship game against Maryland. Duke was rolling, with all five starters and Dunleavy contributing scoring from every angle.

Duke claimed its second consecutive ACC Tournament title and 11th overall with a dominant 81-68 victory over Maryland. Boozer scored 21 points in the title game, Carrawell and Battier were named first-team All-Tournament for the second straight year, and freshman Jason Williams earned the Everett Case Award as tournament MVP — becoming just one of four freshmen in ACC history to win the honor. Six Blue Devils earned All-Tournament recognition, a testament to the team's remarkable depth.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke opened its NCAA Tournament run in Winston-Salem with a comfortable 27-point win over Lamar. The Blue Devils' defense was smothering, and the victory was never in doubt. A warm-up for what lay ahead.

A brutal, physical game that showcased everything great about this Duke team — and everything that still needed polishing. Duke shot just 37% from the floor and went 2-of-17 from three, while committing a season-high 23 turnovers. But the defense was extraordinary: Battier blocked eight Kansas shots, a career high and a Kansas opponent record. Duke fell behind 13-4 early but clawed back to lead 35-35 at halftime. Kansas tied it at 59 with a 9-0 run, but Boozer made the play of the game — a crucial steal that led to two Carrawell free throws for a 67-64 lead. Williams iced it from the line. Boozer's double-double (15 points, 13 rebounds) carried Duke through a night when nothing came easy.

The end of the road. Florida's depth and athleticism overwhelmed a Duke team that committed 22 turnovers and shot 3-of-19 from three. Williams went 1-of-9 from deep, Boozer fouled out, and the Gators' 21 assists exposed Duke's youth. Battier (20 pts, 9 reb, 4 blk) and Carrawell (16 pts, 3 stl) were superb in defeat, but it wasn't enough. Florida would reach the national title game. For Duke, it was the last lesson before the 2001 championship.

seasons.ncaaTournament: National Champions (#1 seed East; beat Monmouth, Missouri, UCLA, USC, Maryland, Arizona)
seasons.tagline: The Third Title. Battier’s masterpiece. Williams’ explosion. A broken foot, a 22-point comeback, and the greatest season a senior has ever played at Duke.
seasons.overview:
This was the season everything came together — and almost fell apart.

Shane Battier entered his senior year as the last remaining member of the heralded 1997 recruiting class that had included Elton Brand and William Avery. Brand and Avery, along with Corey Maggette, had become the first Duke underclassmen to leave early for the NBA Draft two years earlier. Battier had stayed. He had watched the 1999 championship slip away against UConn. He had anchored the 2000 team through the post-exodus rebuild. Now, as a senior, he wanted the one thing that had eluded him: a title.

The supporting cast was extraordinary. Sophomore Jason Williams had evolved from a talented freshman into one of the most electrifying players in college basketball, a fearless scorer who would set the Duke single-season scoring record with 841 points (breaking Dick Groat’s 50-year-old mark). Sophomore Carlos Boozer was a bruising interior force. Sophomore Mike Dunleavy Jr. was a versatile wing who could shoot, pass, and rebound. Senior tri-captain Nate James was the glue. And freshman Chris Duhon arrived as the point guard who would unlock everything — a defensive specialist whose entry into the starting lineup in March would prove to be the masterstroke that won the championship.

The season began with a statement. Duke won the Preseason NIT, and on November 17, 2000, Coach K earned his 500th victory at Duke with a win over Villanova. After the game, the Cameron Indoor Stadium court was officially renamed Coach K Court in a ceremony that acknowledged what everyone already knew: this was the house that Krzyzewski built.

The Blue Devils were ranked #1 for most of the season and looked unstoppable. In a 93-68 win at Temple, Williams went 8-of-10 from three-point range. Five players could hit threes on any given possession — Williams, Duhon, Battier, Dunleavy, and James — making Duke the most prolific three-point shooting team in ACC history, the only team to average 10+ made threes per game.

Then came the Maryland wars. On January 27, #2 Duke played at #8 Maryland in what became one of the most famous regular-season games in ACC history. The Terrapins, led by Juan Dixon and Steve Blake, built a 10-point lead with 54 seconds remaining in regulation. It was over. Except it wasn’t. Williams scored eight points in the final 54 seconds, including two three-pointers in a 13-second span. Nate James hit two free throws to force overtime. In OT, Battier took over, scoring Duke’s last six points and blocking Dixon’s potential game-tying layup at the baseline with four seconds left. Duke won 98-96 in what would be called the “Miracle Minute.”

But the euphoria didn’t last. In the very next game, at home against #4 North Carolina, the Blue Devils lost 85-83 when Brendan Haywood — a 48% free throw shooter — calmly sank two from the line with 1.2 seconds left after Battier fouled him trying to get a steal. Dunleavy had tied the game with a three with 9.3 seconds remaining, and Chris Duhon’s desperation heave at the buzzer hit the back of the rim. Duke had missed 14 free throws. It was the first ACC loss of the season and a devastating emotional crash after the Maryland miracle.

The season’s darkest moment came on February 25 — Shane Battier’s Senior Night at Cameron. Maryland came to Durham and won 91-80, and worse, Carlos Boozer broke his foot during the game. The team’s starting center was done for the foreseeable future. Pundits wrote Duke off. The national championship dream appeared to be over.

Krzyzewski responded with one of the great strategic adjustments of his career. He inserted freshman Duhon into the starting lineup at point guard and moved Williams to shooting guard, creating a smaller, faster, more dynamic backcourt. Casey Sanders stepped in at center. The new lineup was untested and undersized. It was also devastating. Duke won all six games without Boozer, including the pivotal March 4 trip to Chapel Hill.

That game at North Carolina produced the single most iconic defensive play of the Battier era. With Duke leading, Joseph Forte stripped Mike Dunleavy at the top of the key and took off for what he assumed would be an uncontested breakaway dunk. Nobody told Battier. The senior came streaking from behind, timed his leap perfectly, and blocked Forte’s dunk attempt from behind. The Dean Dome went silent. Duke won 95-81, clinching a share of the ACC regular season title without its starting center. It was Battier at his absolute best — the play that defined a career built on effort, intelligence, and refusing to concede anything.

Duke won the ACC Tournament for the third consecutive year, beating Maryland 84-82 when Nate James tipped in the game-winner with 1.3 seconds left, then destroying UNC 79-53 in the championship game — a 19-1 first-half run that buried the Tar Heels. UNC shot 29% and didn’t make a single three-pointer. It was the most dominant ACC Tournament final in years, accomplished without Boozer.

Boozer returned for the Sweet Sixteen, coming off the bench as the NCAA Tournament began. Duke cruised through the East Regional: 95-52 over Monmouth, 94-81 over Missouri, 76-63 over UCLA, and 79-69 over USC. The Blue Devils traveled the same path they’d taken in 1992 — from Greensboro to Philadelphia to Minneapolis — and arrived at the Final Four to face Maryland for the fourth time.

What happened next was the greatest comeback in Final Four history.

Maryland, in its first-ever Final Four, came out with fury. The Terrapins built a 22-point lead in the first half. Duke trailed 39-17. Battier air-balled two shots. Williams was lost. The Maryland cheering section, 5,000 strong in red “Garyland” sweatshirts, smelled blood. During a timeout, Krzyzewski told his players: “You’re losing by so much, you can’t play any worse. So what are you worried about, losing by 40?” Then he did something extraordinary: he stopped calling plays. He told them to just play basketball.

They did. Duke outscored Maryland 31-15 to start the second half, cutting the deficit to four. Williams, who’d been dreadful in the first half, caught fire. His three-pointer gave Duke its first lead, 73-72, with 6:52 remaining. Maryland’s last lead came at 77-76 on a Terence Morris free throw with five minutes left. After that, it was all Duke. Battier finished with 25 points. Williams had 23. Duhon contributed 10 points and six assists. The 95-84 final — an 11-point win after trailing by 22 — was the largest comeback in Final Four history, a record that stood until Kansas overcame a 15-point halftime deficit against North Carolina in the 2022 championship game.

Two nights later, Duke faced Arizona for the national championship. Lute Olson’s Wildcats, led by Gilbert Arenas and Richard Jefferson, had reached the title game on an emotion-filled run after Olson’s wife Bobbi had died of cancer earlier in the season. The first half was tight, with Duke leading 35-33. In the second half, Duke pulled away. Dunleavy led with 21 points, including five three-pointers. Battier played all 40 minutes: 18 points, 11 rebounds, six assists. Boozer recorded 12 points and 12 rebounds off the bench. The 82-72 victory made Duke the first #1-ranked team to win the national championship since UCLA in 1995, and made Krzyzewski just the third coach to win three titles, tying his mentor Bob Knight.

Battier was named NCAA Tournament Most Outstanding Player. He and Williams swept every major National Player of the Year award — the first time in college basketball history that two teammates from the same school won separate major POY honors. Battier took the AP, Wooden, Naismith, Oscar Robertson, and Adolph Rupp awards; Williams won the NABC honor. Battier won his third consecutive NABC Defensive Player of the Year award and was Academic All-American for the second straight year. Both players’ jerseys were later retired by Duke.

The numbers tell the story of dominance: Duke set an NCAA record with 133 wins over a four-year period. Battier tied the NCAA record for career victories (131). Duke was the first team seeded #1 in four consecutive NCAA Tournaments. They finished #1 in the final AP poll for the third consecutive season — only John Wooden’s UCLA had ever done that.

“I thoroughly loved coaching these kids,” Krzyzewski said after the championship. “They’ve been so good. They’ve given me their hearts, their minds, and not only that, they’ve given it to each other. I get real close to the guys on my team. That’s the most rewarding thing about what I do.”

unc.story: The cruelest loss of the season. Coming off the “Miracle Minute” win at Maryland four days earlier, Duke carried a false confidence into this game against Matt Doherty’s Tar Heels. Joseph Forte scored 24 points and grabbed a career-high 16 rebounds. Boozer was held to just 4 points. Duke missed 14 free throws. With the score tied at 80, Dunleavy hit a three with 9.3 seconds left to put Duke up 83-80. But Battier, trying to force a steal, fouled Brendan Haywood — a 48% free throw shooter — with 1.2 seconds remaining. Haywood calmly sank both. Duhon’s desperation 50-foot heave hit the back of the rim. Carolina 85, Duke 83. It was Duke’s first ACC loss of the year and a devastating emotional crash. The 14 missed free throws haunted the Blue Devils.

The Battier Block Game. Playing without Carlos Boozer (broken foot), Duke went to Chapel Hill with K’s new small lineup — Duhon at point, Williams at shooting guard, Battier unleashed. The defining moment: Forte stripped Dunleavy at the top of the key and took off for what he assumed would be an uncontested breakaway dunk. Battier came streaking from behind, timed his leap perfectly, and blocked the shot. The Dean Dome went silent. It was the single most iconic defensive play of Battier’s career — the play that defined effort, intelligence, and refusing to concede anything. Duke won 95-81, clinching a share of the regular season title without its starting center. K’s smaller, faster lineup had proven itself for the biggest game on the schedule.

The ACC Tournament championship — and a demolition. Still without Boozer, Duke started Battier, Dunleavy, Casey Sanders, Duhon, and Williams against a UNC team featuring Forte, Jason Capel, Kris Lang, Brendan Haywood, and Ronald Curry (with Julius Peppers, the future NFL star, coming off the bench). It looked competitive for about ten minutes. Then Duke went on a 19-1 run and the game was over. The Tar Heels shot 29% and didn’t make a single three-pointer. Duke scored 19 points in transition to UNC’s four. The 79-53 final was Duke’s third straight ACC Tournament title and the most lopsided championship game in recent memory. The tournament was a coronation. The NCAA Tournament was next.

theGame.title: The 22-Point Comeback: Duke-Maryland, Final Four
theGame.headline: Down 22 in the first half, Coach K stops calling plays. Duke stages the greatest comeback in Final Four history to reach the national championship game.
theGame.story:
They had played three times already that season. Duke had won twice by a single basket. Maryland had won once by eleven. Now they met in the Final Four — the fourth collision between two teams that genuinely despised each other’s success.

Maryland came out with fury. The Terrapins, in their first-ever Final Four, had nothing to lose and played like it. They built a 22-point lead at 39-17 with 6:57 left in the first half. Battier air-balled two early shots. Williams couldn’t find the range. Duke missed its first eight three-point attempts. Maryland made five of its first six. Five thousand Maryland fans in red “Garyland” sweatshirts smelled blood.

During a timeout, Krzyzewski looked at his shell-shocked players and said: “You’re losing by so much, you can’t play any worse. So what are you worried about, losing by 40?” Then he made one of the most remarkable coaching decisions in tournament history: he stopped calling plays. He told his team to just play basketball. No sets. No clipboard. Just compete.

They did. Duke clawed back to trail 49-38 at halftime — an 11-point deficit that was still the largest halftime hole any team had ever overcome in a Final Four game. Then the second half began, and Duke outscored Maryland 31-15 in the first ten minutes. The Terrapins’ bench began to fracture. Gary Williams screamed at officials. Players argued with each other. Duke’s relentless pressure — the thing K had been building for twenty years — was suffocating them.

Williams, who had been invisible in the first half, caught fire. His three-pointer gave Duke its first lead of the entire game, 73-72, with 6:52 remaining. Maryland took its last lead at 77-76 on a Terence Morris free throw with five minutes left. After that, it was all Duke. Battier finished with 25 points. Williams had 23. Freshman Duhon contributed 10 points and six assists.

The final score was 95-84 — an 11-point victory after trailing by 22. It was the largest comeback in Final Four history, a record that stood for 21 years until Kansas erased a 15-point halftime deficit against North Carolina in the 2022 championship game. Krzyzewski had coached 20 seasons, won two titles, and been to nine Final Fours. Nothing he had ever done compared to what happened in the Metrodome that night.

Two days later, Duke beat Arizona 82-72 for the national championship. But ask anyone who was there — the Maryland game was the title. Everything after that was a coronation.

accTourney.story: The third Duke-Maryland game of the season, and another classic. Maryland rallied from a 14-point second-half deficit, but Nate James tipped in the game-winner with 1.3 seconds remaining. James, the senior tri-captain who had doubled his scoring from the previous year, delivered in the biggest moment of his career. Duke advanced to the ACC championship game for the third straight year.

A demolition. Without Boozer, Duke went on a 19-1 first-half run and buried UNC. The Tar Heels shot 29% and made zero three-pointers. Duke’s defense was historically good. Third consecutive ACC Tournament championship. Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as the #1 seed in the East.

ncaaTourney.story: A 43-point annihilation. Duke’s defense was suffocating and its offense ran in waves. The Blue Devils were angry, focused, and playing with the kind of purpose that only comes from a team that knows it has unfinished business. Monmouth never had a chance.

Missouri, coached by former Duke assistant and player Quin Snyder, put up a fight but couldn’t match Duke’s firepower. Williams was brilliant, and the Blue Devils advanced to the Sweet Sixteen in Philadelphia.

Boozer returned to the lineup for the first time since breaking his foot on February 25. He came off the bench — Casey Sanders kept the starting spot — and Duke’s frontcourt was suddenly whole again. The Blue Devils controlled the game from start to finish, holding UCLA to 63 points. Duke was rolling.

Duke earned its trip to the Final Four with a workmanlike 10-point win over the Trojans in Philadelphia. Battier was dominant on both ends. The Blue Devils had traveled the same path — Greensboro to Philadelphia to Minneapolis — that they’d taken nine years earlier on the way to the 1992 championship. History was repeating itself.

The greatest comeback in Final Four history. Down 22 in the first half, K stopped calling plays. Williams’ three gave Duke the lead 73-72. Battier had 25. Duke outscored Maryland 57-35 in the second half. The record stood for 21 years.

The coronation. Arizona, led by Gilbert Arenas and Richard Jefferson and coached by Lute Olson — who had lost his wife Bobbi to cancer earlier in the season — pushed Duke in the first half (35-33 at the break). But the Blue Devils pulled away in the second half behind Dunleavy’s 21 points (five threes), Battier’s all-everything 18 points, 11 rebounds, and 6 assists over 40 minutes, and Boozer’s 12-and-12 off the bench. Duke became the first #1-ranked team to win the title since UCLA in 1995. Every NCAA Tournament game was won by 10+ points — the first Duke team to accomplish that. Battier was named Tournament MOP. K won his third championship, tying Bob Knight. “I thoroughly loved coaching these kids,” he said. “They’ve given me their hearts, their minds, and not only that, they’ve given it to each other.”

seasons.accTournament: Champions (4th straight; record; beat NC State 93-64 in final)
seasons.ncaaTournament: Sweet Sixteen (#1 seed South; lost to Indiana 74-73)
seasons.tagline: The Big Three’s last ride. Williams, Boozer, and Dunleavy dominate the regular season, win a record fourth straight ACC Tournament — then Indiana ends everything on a missed free throw.
seasons.overview:
Expectations had never been higher. Duke returned three future NBA lottery picks — junior Jason Williams, junior Carlos Boozer, and junior Mike Dunleavy Jr. — from the 2001 championship team, along with sophomore point guard Chris Duhon and a roster loaded with talent including Dahntay Jones, Daniel Ewing, and Casey Sanders. The only significant losses were Shane Battier and Nate James to graduation. With 66.4% of minutes and 63.0% of scoring returning, Duke was the consensus preseason #1 team in America.

The Blue Devils lived up to the billing for most of the season. Williams was transcendent, becoming just the seventh repeat winner of National Player of the Year honors in college basketball history when he won the Naismith Award for the second consecutive year. He averaged 21.3 points, 5.3 assists, and 3.5 rebounds per game, breaking his own Duke single-season scoring record. Boozer averaged 18.2 points and 8.4 rebounds while shooting 60.7% from the field. Dunleavy contributed 17.3 points and 7.2 rebounds. All three earned All-America honors — the first time three teammates from the same school had been named All-Americans since 1957.

The season began with Duke winning the Preseason NIT, followed by a narrow 78-77 victory over Illinois in Greensboro and a 93-68 demolition of Temple in Philadelphia where Williams went 8-of-10 from three-point range. The Blue Devils looked invincible. But cracks appeared in December when Casey Jacobsen banked in a go-ahead shot with 3.6 seconds remaining to give Stanford an 84-83 win — Duke’s first loss after leading by 13 at halftime and 11 with four minutes to play.

The ACC season was another tour of dominance: Duke went 13-3, winning its sixth consecutive outright regular season championship. But the three losses all came on the road — at Florida State, at Maryland, and at Virginia — suggesting that without Battier’s defensive anchor, this team could be vulnerable in hostile environments.

The Doherty-era collapse at North Carolina provided the starkest contrast in the rivalry’s history. Matt Doherty’s Tar Heels were plummeting toward an 8-20 season — the worst in program history and the first losing record since Dean Smith took over in 1962. Duke swept UNC with ease. On January 31, the Blue Devils destroyed the Tar Heels 87-58 at the Dean Smith Center, the worst loss in the building’s history. Williams scored 30 and Duke led by 20 in the first half against a team that shot 28% from the field. On March 3, the regular season finale at Cameron, Duke won 93-68 with Williams scoring 32 — one shy of his career high. It was Carolina’s 11th ACC loss by double digits in a nightmare season.

Duke’s ACC Tournament in Charlotte was a coronation. The Blue Devils won all three games by double digits, including a 93-64 annihilation of NC State in the championship game — a 30-point rout that concluded a record fourth consecutive ACC Tournament title. No team in conference history had ever won four straight. Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as the #1 seed in the South and was considered the overwhelming favorite to repeat as national champions.

The first two rounds were routine: 84-37 over Winthrop and 84-77 over Notre Dame. Then came Lexington, Kentucky, and the fifth-seeded Indiana Hoosiers.

The game went according to script for 20 minutes. Duke led 42-29 at halftime, and the 13-point spread was exactly what Vegas had predicted. There was no reason to believe anything would change. Everything changed.

Indiana, led by sophomore Jared Jeffries and seniors Dane Fife and Tom Coverdale, came out of the locker room possessed. The Hoosiers chipped away, and then A.J. Moye blocked a Carlos Boozer shot that sent the Rupp Arena crowd — heavily tilted toward the underdog, since Bloomington was only three hours away — into a frenzy. Duke fought back and led by six with 2:45 remaining after a Dunleavy three-pointer. But Indiana wouldn’t die. Coverdale tied the game at 70 with two free throws, then gave Indiana its first lead of the game on a baseline jumper that rattled around the rim three times before falling.

With 11 seconds left, Moye hit two free throws to put Indiana up 74-70. Williams launched a desperate three and was fouled by Dane Fife. The three went in. The crowd held its breath. Williams stepped to the line needing one free throw to tie. He missed. Boozer tipped at the rebound but couldn’t convert. Indiana 74, Duke 73. The largest upset (by point spread) in NCAA Tournament history for a game in the Sweet Sixteen or later.

It was the last college game for Williams, Boozer, and Dunleavy. All three declared for the 2002 NBA Draft. Williams was taken second overall by the Chicago Bulls, Dunleavy third by the Golden State Warriors — making them just the second set of teammates drafted in the top three picks (after UCLA’s Lew Alcindor and Lucius Allen went one and three in 1969). Boozer was selected 35th overall by the Cleveland Cavaliers and went on to have the longest and most productive NBA career of the three.

The season’s final numbers were staggering: 31-4, #1 in the final AP poll for the fourth consecutive year (an NCAA first), #1 seed for a record fifth straight NCAA Tournament, a record fourth consecutive ACC Tournament title. Duke had finished with both a top-3 offense and defense per KenPom for the second straight year — a feat accomplished by just six teams in the metric’s history, four of them K’s Duke squads.

But the Indiana loss haunted Durham. One missed free throw. One blocked shot. One baseline jumper that rattled three times before deciding to fall in. The defending champions, who had been #1 from wire to wire for the fourth straight season, went home in the Sweet Sixteen. Indiana, improbably, would ride that win all the way to the national championship game before falling to Maryland — the same Maryland team that Duke had beaten four times the previous season.

Williams would suffer a devastating motorcycle accident six days after being drafted, ending what should have been a Hall of Fame NBA career. Boozer would become a two-time All-Star. Dunleavy would play 14 solid NBA seasons. Duhon would stay two more years and carry Duke through a difficult transitional period. But the Big Three’s last ride ended on a missed free throw in Lexington, and the what-ifs of the 2002 season remain among the most painful in the Brotherhood’s history.

unc.story: The worst loss in Dean Smith Center history. Matt Doherty’s Tar Heels were in freefall toward an 8-20 season — the worst in program history. Duke showed no mercy. Williams scored 30 points and the Blue Devils led by 20 in the first half against a UNC team that shot 28% from the field. The 87-58 final was a 29-point annihilation in Chapel Hill, a margin that would have been unthinkable in any previous era of the rivalry. The Tar Heels had no answer for Duke’s three-headed attack, and the Cameron Crazies didn’t even need to be present — the Dean Dome was practically a road game for Carolina. It was a snapshot of two programs at polar extremes: Duke at its absolute peak, UNC at its rock bottom.

The regular season finale at Cameron, and another rout. Williams scored 32 points — one shy of his career high — as Duke blasted UNC 93-68. It was Carolina’s 11th ACC loss by double digits in a season that would end 8-20, the most losses in school history. For Duke, it was Senior Night for Casey Sanders and the last home game for Williams, Boozer, and Dunleavy, though nobody knew it yet. The rivalry was so lopsided that it barely felt like a rivalry. Duke had won seven of the last eight meetings. Doherty would be forced to resign the following spring, and Roy Williams would finally come home from Kansas to rebuild. But on this night, in this building, it was Duke’s world.

theGame.title: The Missed Free Throw: Indiana Ends the Dynasty
theGame.headline: Jay Williams hits a three-pointer and is fouled with 4 seconds left — but misses the free throw that would have tied it. Indiana 74, Duke 73. The Big Three’s college careers end on a single shot.
theGame.story:
It was supposed to be a coronation. Duke was 31-3, ranked #1 for the fourth straight year, riding a record five consecutive #1 seeds. Williams, Boozer, and Dunleavy were the best trio in college basketball. The Vegas line was 13 points. Indiana was a 5-seed with a 19-12 record. This wasn’t a game. It was a formality.

The first half confirmed it. Duke led 42-29, controlling the tempo and the glass. Boozer was dominant inside. Williams was orchestrating. Indiana looked overmatched.

Then the second half began, and the Hoosiers — led by Bloomington-native sophomore Jared Jeffries, senior Dane Fife, and senior Tom Coverdale — came out of the locker room transformed. The Rupp Arena crowd, only three hours from Bloomington, was overwhelmingly pro-Indiana, and the Hoosiers fed off the energy. They chipped away possession by possession. A.J. Moye blocked a Boozer shot and the building shook. Indiana cut it to six with 2:45 left when Dunleavy hit a three to seemingly restore order.

It didn’t. Coverdale tied it at 70 with two free throws. Then he gave Indiana its first lead of the game on a baseline jumper that hit the rim three times — three agonizing bounces — before falling through. Moye hit two free throws. Indiana 74, Duke 70 with 11 seconds left.

Williams drove and launched a three-pointer. It went in. He was fouled by Fife. The arena froze. Williams stepped to the free throw line with a chance to tie the game, one shot, the season on the line.

He missed.

Boozer tipped at the rebound. It didn’t fall. The horn sounded. Indiana 74, Duke 73. The largest point-spread upset in NCAA Tournament history for a Sweet Sixteen game or later.

Williams, the two-time National Player of the Year, walked off the court in tears. It was the last college game for all three juniors — Williams, Boozer, and Dunleavy all declared for the NBA Draft within weeks. The dynasty that had produced 133 wins in four years, three #1 AP finishes, two title game appearances, and one championship was over.

Indiana rode the magic all the way to the national championship game, where they lost to Maryland — the same Maryland team Duke had beaten four times the season before. The irony was almost too perfect: the team Duke couldn’t beat in Lexington lost to the team Duke had beaten everywhere else.

Six days after being drafted second overall by the Chicago Bulls, Jay Williams crashed his motorcycle in Chicago and nearly lost his leg. The NBA career that should have been historic was reduced to one injury-marred season. The missed free throw in Lexington wasn’t just the end of a season. It was the last moment when everything was still possible.

accTourney.story: Duke opened its quest for a record fourth consecutive ACC Tournament title with a comfortable win over Virginia in Charlotte. The Blue Devils were on a mission, and no one in the bracket could match their firepower.

A dominant semifinal win over NC State set up the championship game. Duke had beaten the Wolfpack three times this season by a combined 86 points. NC State was actually a strong team — they would make the Round of 32 before falling to UConn — but they had no answer for Duke.

Wait — NC State again? Yes. The Wolfpack, coached by Herb Sendek and featuring senior point guard Archie Miller (who would go on to coach Indiana), earned a rematch after winning their semifinal. The result was the same, only worse: a 30-point demolition, 93-64. Duke won every ACC Tournament game by double digits, and the fourth consecutive tournament title was the first in ACC history. No team had ever done it. The dynasty’s regular-season dominance (six straight outright titles) and tournament dominance (four straight championships) was without precedent in the modern era of the conference.

ncaaTourney.story: A 47-point annihilation — the most lopsided tournament win in Duke’s K era at that point. The Blue Devils were angry, focused, and determined to defend their title. Winthrop scored 37 points. Duke scored 84 without breaking a sweat.

A tougher test from Mike Brey’s Fighting Irish, a former Duke assistant. Notre Dame pushed Duke but couldn’t match the Blue Devils’ talent. Williams and Boozer were too much. Duke advanced to the Sweet Sixteen for the fifth consecutive year.

The end. Duke led 42-29 at halftime, and the 13-point spread was exactly what Vegas predicted. Then Indiana stormed back, A.J. Moye blocked Boozer, Coverdale’s baseline jumper rattled three times before falling to give IU the lead, and Williams’ three-and-one chance to tie ended with a missed free throw. The Big Three’s college careers ended on a single shot in Lexington. Indiana would ride the win to the national championship game. Williams would suffer a motorcycle accident six days after being drafted. The what-ifs of 2002 remain among the most painful in the Brotherhood’s history.

seasons.accTournament: Champions (record 5th straight; beat NC State 84-77 in final)
seasons.ncaaTournament: Sweet Sixteen (#3 seed West; lost to Kansas 69-65)
seasons.tagline: The Rebuild That Nobody Believed In. Only 34% of scoring returns, Duhon takes over, a Rutgers transfer plays power forward, a pudgy freshman named Redick catches fire — and Duke wins its fifth straight ACC Tournament anyway.
seasons.overview:
The numbers were staggering and not in Duke’s favor. Only 49.4% of minutes played and 34.0% of scoring returned from the 2001-02 roster. Jay Williams (second overall pick), Mike Dunleavy Jr. (third overall), and Carlos Boozer (35th) were all gone to the NBA. Senior Casey Sanders graduated. In a single offseason, Duke lost three All-Americans and its starting center. It was the most talent-depleted roster K had fielded since the post-foundation year of 1987.

The rebuild centered on junior point guard Chris Duhon, who had been the steady backup behind Williams for two years and now inherited the keys. Dahntay Jones, a Rutgers transfer who had sat out the 2001-02 season per NCAA transfer rules while being allowed to practice, emerged as the team’s leading scorer. Jones was a 6-foot-6 wing, but K moved him to power forward because Duke’s frontcourt was so thin — making him perhaps the most undersized starting four in major conference basketball. The gamble worked. Jones averaged approximately 17 points per game in January and was the team’s emotional engine.

The freshman class was transformative, though nobody fully appreciated it at the time. J.J. Redick, a pudgy shooting guard from Roanoke, Virginia, who had watched Laettner hit The Shot on television as a child and told his father he was going to play at Duke, arrived as the nation’s #11 recruit. Shelden Williams, a 6-foot-9 center from Oklahoma City, brought rim protection and physicality. Shavlik Randolph added depth in the frontcourt. Daniel Ewing, a sophomore guard, was growing into a reliable two-way player.

The season had its share of struggles. Duke’s six-year streak of 12-win ACC seasons ended at 11-5, good for second place (tied with Wake Forest) behind a Maryland team that was riding the Juan Dixon-led wave to its own national championship a year earlier. The Blue Devils lost on the road at Florida State, at Wake Forest, and at North Carolina — environments where the post depth and interior toughness that Williams, Boozer, and Dunleavy had provided were sorely missed. Krzyzewski himself noted the absence of vocal leaders, saying after one loss: “We don’t have Battier and Dunleavy talking.”

But the season produced two signature moments that previewed what was coming.

The first was the retirement of Jason Williams’ #22 jersey at Cameron Indoor Stadium during the February 5 game against UNC. Grant Hill and Williams himself were in the crowd. It was an emotional ceremony that reminded everyone of what had been — and what this current team was trying to sustain without those players. Duke won the game 83-74, with Jones scoring 23 points and grabbing 13 rebounds in front of the former greats. The game also featured an early confrontation between Jones and UNC coach Matt Doherty — Jones backed into Doherty after making a three-pointer, words were exchanged, and K and Doherty had to be separated by officials. And a fully blue-painted streaker ran baseline to baseline before being arrested.

The second signature moment was the ACC Tournament championship game — and specifically, the arrival of J.J. Redick as a star. NC State, coached by Herb Sendek, built a 15-point lead in the second half of the title game. Duke looked dead. Then Redick went supernova, scoring 23 of his 30 points in the final 10 minutes and 5 seconds of the game. He hit four critical three-pointers and iced a free throw with 2:51 left that gave Duke the lead for good. The Blue Devils won 84-77, claiming a record fifth consecutive ACC Tournament championship — a streak that may never be matched in the modern era. It was the game where everyone watching understood that Redick wasn’t just a shooter. He was a closer. He was the future.

The regular season finale at Chapel Hill was the most heated Duke-UNC game in years. With 8:17 remaining and the game tied at 63, Dahntay Jones elbowed Raymond Felton while going for a rebound, bloodying the freshman point guard. As Doherty rushed over to check on Felton near the Duke bench, Duke assistant coach Chris Collins began jawing at Doherty. Reserve Andre Buckner then shoved the UNC head coach, and both benches nearly erupted into a full brawl before officials restored order. Rashad McCants scored 26 and Felton had 18 points, 8 rebounds, and 10 assists as Carolina won 82-79, snapping Duke’s six-game winning streak in the rivalry. Jones’ 30-foot three-pointer that would have tied it was waved off for coming after the buzzer. It was UNC’s first victory over Duke since 2001, and it was Doherty’s last stand — he would be forced to resign weeks later, paving the way for Roy Williams to finally come home from Kansas.

In the NCAA Tournament, Duke was a #3 seed in the West Region. The Blue Devils handled Colorado State 67-57 and Central Michigan 86-60 (Redick scored 26) before meeting #2 Kansas in the Sweet Sixteen in Anaheim. Nick Collison — the player Roy Williams had promised he’d stay at Kansas for — dominated with 33 points and 19 rebounds, one of the most dominant individual performances in tournament history. Redick, the hero of the ACC Tournament two weeks earlier, shot 2-of-16. Duke fell 69-65, ending the season at 26-7.

The record looks modest by Duke standards. But what K accomplished with this roster — a fifth straight ACC Tournament title with only 34% of the previous year’s scoring, a Sweet Sixteen appearance with freshmen playing 40% of the minutes, and the development of Redick and Shelden Williams into the stars who would carry the next four years — was one of his finest coaching jobs. The 2002-03 season is the bridge between the Williams-Boozer-Dunleavy dynasty and the Redick-Shelden Williams era. Every great program needs a bridge year. Not every bridge year produces a conference tournament championship.

unc.story: Jason Williams’ #22 jersey was retired at halftime, with Grant Hill and Williams in attendance. The ceremony was emotional — a reminder of what Duke had been and what this rebuilding team was fighting to sustain. Dahntay Jones delivered on the occasion with 23 points and 13 rebounds, but the game was heated from the start. Jones backed into UNC coach Matt Doherty after hitting a three-pointer within the first five minutes, and Krzyzewski and Doherty had to be separated by officials. A fully blue-painted streaker ran baseline to baseline before being arrested with 24 seconds left in the half. UNC, led by 25 from Raymond Felton, took a 43-38 halftime lead, but Duke scored on its final 11 possessions to pull away. It was Duke’s sixth straight win over Carolina. Doherty’s Tar Heels were 11-10 and falling.

The most violent Duke-UNC game in years. With 8:17 remaining and the score tied at 63, Dahntay Jones elbowed Raymond Felton going for a rebound, bloodying the freshman guard. UNC coach Matt Doherty rushed to check on Felton near the Duke bench, where assistant coach Chris Collins began jawing at him. Reserve Andre Buckner then shoved Doherty, triggering a near-brawl that officials barely contained. The scuffle ignited an already electric Dean Dome crowd. Rashad McCants scored 26, Felton had 18 points, 8 rebounds, and 10 assists, and Carolina won 82-79 — snapping Duke’s six-game winning streak in the rivalry. Jones’ desperation 30-footer that would have tied the game was waved off for coming after the buzzer. It was UNC’s first win over Duke since 2001. It was also Matt Doherty’s last stand — he would be forced to resign weeks later, and Roy Williams would finally come home from Kansas.

theGame.title: Redick’s Eruption: The ACC Championship Comeback
theGame.headline: Down 15 in the second half, freshman J.J. Redick scores 23 of his 30 points in the final 10 minutes to deliver Duke’s record fifth consecutive ACC Tournament championship.
theGame.story:
Nobody expected this team to be here. Duke had lost three All-Americans to the NBA Draft and returned just 34% of its scoring. The Blue Devils were a #3 seed in the West Region, not a #1 for the first time in five years. They had lost seven games. And now, in the ACC Tournament championship against NC State, they trailed by 15 points in the second half.

Herb Sendek’s Wolfpack were playing with fury, led by their interior presence and riding the emotion of a potential upset. Duke looked exhausted. The run was over.

Then J.J. Redick happened.

The freshman from Roanoke, Virginia — still baby-faced, still carrying the extra weight he’d shed by his sophomore year — simply took over the game. He scored 23 of his 30 points in the final 10 minutes and 5 seconds. He hit four three-pointers in the stretch, each one bigger than the last. His free throw with 2:51 remaining gave Duke the lead for good. NC State’s bench, which had been celebrating minutes earlier, went silent. Sendek, watching his lead evaporate in real time, grew increasingly animated on the sideline before the camera caught him looking utterly defeated.

Duke won 84-77. Fifth consecutive ACC Tournament championship. No program in ACC history had ever done it, and none has done it since. The streak — 1999, 2000, 2001, 2002, 2003 — spanned the Langdon-Brand era, the Carrawell year, the Battier championship, the Williams-Boozer-Dunleavy Big Three, and now a team with three freshmen in the rotation. That’s not a streak. That’s a dynasty.

Redick would go on to score 2,769 career points at Duke, become a two-time National Player of the Year, and play 15 NBA seasons before becoming the head coach of the Los Angeles Lakers. But the ACC Tournament championship game against NC State was the moment everyone first saw what he would become: a player who rises when the stakes are highest, who hits shots that no one else on the floor would dare attempt, and who refuses to let a season end on someone else’s terms.

“That’s the reason I came to Duke,” Redick said after the game, “to continue the tradition of being one of the top programs in the country.” He was eighteen years old.

accTourney.story: Duke opened ACC Tournament play with a comfortable win over Virginia, advancing to the semifinal. The Blue Devils were the #2 seed, having finished second in the ACC behind Maryland.

The Redick Eruption. Down 15 in the second half, freshman Redick scored 23 of his 30 points in the final 10:05 to deliver Duke’s record fifth straight ACC Tournament title. Four threes in the stretch. A free throw with 2:51 left for the lead. The moment the future Hall of Famer announced himself to the world. Eighteen years old.

ncaaTourney.story: A grind-it-out win in Anaheim. Colorado State played physical and kept it close, but Duke’s defense held firm. Not a vintage performance, but a win is a win for a team that had no business being this deep into March with this roster.

Duke’s offense finally clicked. Redick scored 26 points and the Blue Devils pulled away in the second half for a comfortable 26-point victory. Duke advanced to the Sweet Sixteen for the sixth consecutive season — a remarkable achievement for a team that had been expected to take a significant step back.

The end came in Anaheim against Nick Collison and Kansas. Collison — the player Roy Williams had promised he’d stay at Kansas to coach — put on one of the most dominant individual performances in tournament history: 33 points and 19 rebounds, a force of nature that Duke simply couldn’t contain. J.J. Redick, the hero of the ACC Tournament two weeks earlier, shot 2-of-16 — a brutal night for the freshman. Duke lost 69-65 in a game that was closer than the shooting numbers suggested, with Duhon and Jones competing until the final whistle. Kansas would reach the national championship game before falling to Syracuse. For Duke, the season ended at 26-7 — a record that looked modest but represented one of K’s finest coaching jobs given the talent that had departed.

seasons.accTournament: Runner-up (lost to #6 seed Maryland 95-87 OT in championship; ended record 5-year title streak and 17-game ACC Tournament winning streak)
seasons.ncaaTournament: Final Four (#1 seed East; beat Alabama State 96-61, Seton Hall 90-62, Illinois 72-62, Xavier 66-63; lost to UConn 79-78 in national semifinal)
seasons.tagline: The Year Everything Almost Changed. Luol Deng arrives as the nation’s #2 recruit behind LeBron. Duhon plays the game of his life against Roy Williams. Emeka Okafor rips the ball from Deng’s hands with 26 seconds left. And in July, Kobe calls Coach K. Duke’s heart stops beating for a month.
seasons.overview:
The 2003-04 season was a restoration year that became something far more dramatic. With 75.1% of minutes and 72.0% of scoring returning from the 2002-03 roster, plus the addition of five-star freshman Luol Deng — rated the #2 recruit in the nation behind only LeBron James — Duke had the pieces to compete for a championship again. The question was whether the young core could grow fast enough.

Chris Duhon returned for his senior year with a chip on his shoulder. After a disappointing junior season in which he’d been named ACC Preseason Player of the Year but shot just 27% from three and managed only Third Team All-ACC, he entered 2003-04 determined to prove his doubters wrong. He would deliver, running the point with the kind of poise and leadership that defined the best Duke teams. J.J. Redick and Shelden Williams, now sophomores, were beginning to scratch the surface of their extraordinary talent. Daniel Ewing provided steady two-way play at shooting guard. And Deng was everything advertised — a do-it-all wing who could post up, stretch defenses to twenty feet, and impact both ends of the floor. He averaged 15.1 points and 6.9 rebounds and was arguably the team’s most impactful player from day one.

Duke won the ACC regular season championship for the sixth time in eight years with a 13-3 conference record and rolled into the ACC Tournament as the #1 seed, looking for an unprecedented sixth consecutive tournament title. But the Blue Devils’ Achilles heel — rebounding — haunted them. Duke ranked 282nd nationally in defensive rebounding, a product of undersized lineups that featured Deng venturing away from the glass and perimeter-oriented wings in Redick and Ewing. Teams had a blueprint to beat Duke: pound the boards and force them to shoot from outside.

Two losses crystalized the vulnerability. On March 3, Georgia Tech came into Cameron Indoor Stadium and snapped the Blue Devils’ 41-game home winning streak, the longest active run in the nation, winning 76-68. Three days later, Duke hosted UNC for Senior Night — Duhon’s last game in Cameron — and won a tight, physical game with Deng scoring 25 points on 12-of-16 shooting off the bench, as K gave walk-on senior Nick Horvath the start. Redick dove on the floor for a crucial loose ball in the final minutes, a play K later called his biggest at Duke. Neither team led by more than six in the second half.

Then came the ACC Tournament championship game in Greensboro, and the dynasty finally cracked. Sixth-seeded Maryland, riding sophomore John Gilchrist’s scorching three-game run (he averaged 24 points, 6.3 assists, and 5.3 rebounds through the tournament), built an 11-point first-half lead before Duke stormed back to lead by 12 with under five minutes remaining. But Maryland closed regulation on a 15-3 run, with Gilchrist’s driving layup drawing Shelden Williams’ fifth foul and the ensuing free throw tying the game at 77 with 20 seconds left. In overtime, Duke went cold. Maryland won 95-87, ending the five-year championship streak and a 17-game ACC Tournament winning streak that dated to 1998. Gary Williams’ manic celebration on the ladder as the Greensboro crowd chanted his name was the image of the day. K was gracious in defeat: “My hat is off to Gary and his program. He’s a fighter and his teams won’t quit.”

Despite the tournament loss, Duke earned a #1 seed in the NCAA Tournament’s East Region for the sixth time in seven years. The Blue Devils were dominant through the first four rounds: Alabama State fell 96-61 (Shavlik Randolph led with 20 points), Seton Hall was dismantled 90-62 (Redick 21, Deng 20), and Illinois went down 72-62 as Duke’s guards disrupted the Illini’s rhythm. In the Elite Eight, a tense 66-63 win over Xavier clinched the Atlanta Regional title, with Deng earning regional MVP honors (19 points) and Shelden Williams anchoring the defense with 12 points, 13 rebounds, and 5 blocks.

The Final Four in San Antonio’s Alamodome brought Duke face-to-face with #2 seed UConn and its dominant center, Emeka Okafor. Duke built a 75-67 lead with four minutes remaining. Then UConn mounted one of the most devastating runs in Final Four history: 12 unanswered points. The decisive sequence was pure Okafor — he missed a shot that bounced around the rim, ripped the rebound out of Luol Deng’s hands, and laid it in to give UConn a 76-75 lead with 26 seconds left. “I just saw this orange object floating in the air, and it said ‘Grab me,’” Okafor said. Duke called timeout. Redick drove the lane but Okafor was waiting, disrupting the shot without a foul being called. Rashad Anderson hit two free throws to push the lead to three. Redick’s desperate three-pointer barely grazed the rim. Duhon banked in a 38-foot three at the buzzer, but it only cut the final margin to 79-78 — a meaningless basket that made the score look closer than the gut-punch felt.

Deng recorded a double-double with 16 points and 12 rebounds. Redick and Duhon each contributed. Ewing hit three buckets in the stretch run, including a three-pointer that gave Duke its 73-64 lead. But UConn held Duke scoreless over the final 4:36 of regulation (excluding Duhon’s buzzer bank), and Okafor was simply immovable. Deng later called it the most haunting loss of his career: “Till now, that’s the one loss that every time I think of losing, that’s the first thing that comes to mind.”

UConn went on to beat Georgia Tech for the national championship. Okafor was named tournament MOP. For Duke, the season ended at 31-6 — a record that ranked among the best in program history but fell one game short of the title game.

What happened next nearly tore the program apart.

Deng declared for the NBA Draft after his one season and was selected seventh overall by the Phoenix Suns (immediately traded to the Chicago Bulls). He would go on to play 15 NBA seasons, earn two All-Star selections, and become one of the most respected figures in global basketball.

But the real drama was Coach K. After the Lakers fired Phil Jackson following the 2004 NBA Finals loss to Detroit, Los Angeles offered Krzyzewski a five-year, $40 million contract with partial ownership of the franchise. Kobe Bryant personally called K to recruit him. K walked into a Duke gym and told players: “You’ll never guess who called. Kobe Bryant. He wants me to come coach him.” The negotiations went on for weeks. Redick, then a rising sophomore, began calling friends at other schools to explore transferring. “Coach was the primary reason I went to Duke,” Redick later said. “So I definitely called some friends at different schools. I was like, ‘Hey, do you guys have a spot? I might be transferring.’”

In July 2004, K announced he was staying. “Your heart has to be in whatever you lead,” he said. “It became apparent that this decision was somewhat easier to make because you have to follow your heart and lead with it, and Duke has always taken up my whole heart.” Twenty years later, K told ESPN that if the same offer came today, at 57, he would take it. But in 2004, he stayed — and the decision shaped the next two decades of college basketball. He would coach Redick through two more Player of the Year seasons, lead Duke to two more national championships in 2010 and 2015, and become the winningest coach in Division I history. Redick, whose Duke career was saved by K’s decision, would eventually become the head coach of the Los Angeles Lakers — the very team that had tried to take his coach away.

unc.story: The first Duke-UNC game of the Roy Williams era was an instant classic, and Duke won it in the most improbable fashion. Roy’s Tar Heels — featuring sophomores Raymond Felton, Rashad McCants, Sean May, and Jawad Williams, the same core that would win a national title 14 months later — built a 69-62 lead with 5:45 remaining on a McCants fast-break dunk. Duke’s defense then shut UNC out for more than four minutes. Luol Deng hit two free throws to give Duke a 72-69 lead with 1:06 left. Sean May scored with 53 seconds remaining. Redick restored the three-point lead on a drive with 38 seconds left. Then Jawad Williams hit a game-tying three with 18 seconds left to force overtime. In the extra period, Shelden Williams was dominant — 22 points, 12 rebounds, 5 blocks on the night — and his defense forced UNC into a 35-second shot clock violation with 22 seconds remaining. McCants hit a three with about 15 seconds left to tie it again. Duke did not call timeout. Duhon took the inbound, found a crease, raced the length of the floor, and finished with a reverse layup with 6.5 seconds left. UNC’s desperation three missed at the buzzer. Duke 83, Carolina 81. K said afterward: “I think you do it an injustice to say it was a great Duke-Carolina game. It was a great game. You can’t match the intensity level.” Grant Hill was in the stands wearing a turtleneck. Donald Trump was not yet in the building. ESPN had built its entire week of programming around this game. It delivered.

Duhon’s Senior Night. Three days after Georgia Tech snapped Duke’s 41-game home winning streak, the Blue Devils hosted Roy Williams’ Tar Heels in Cameron for the first time. The crowd featured former Duke stars Grant Hill and Bobby Hurley — and, sitting courtside under the basket, Donald Trump. K started walk-on senior Nick Horvath, giving Deng a moment off the bench to honor Duhon and the seniors. Deng responded with 25 points on 12-of-16 shooting, mostly in the paint. Neither team led by more than six in the second half. The decisive moment came when McCants lost the ball trying to pull up for a three and Redick dove to the floor to secure it, calling timeout. K later said the loose ball play was Redick’s biggest moment at Duke — not any of his thousands of made three-pointers, but a hustle play on the floor in a rivalry game. Duke won its 14th game in the last 16 against Carolina. Williams noted the familiar sting: “These will be very similar to the kind of comments I made over at our place.” He was 0-2 against K.

theGame.title: Okafor Rips It Away: The UConn Final Four Heartbreak
theGame.headline: Duke leads 75-67 with four minutes left. UConn scores 12 unanswered. Okafor rips the rebound from Deng’s hands and scores with 26 seconds remaining. Redick’s tying three barely grazes iron. Duhon’s 38-foot bank shot at the buzzer makes the score 79-78 — and means absolutely nothing.
theGame.story:
For three hours in San Antonio, the 2003-04 Duke Blue Devils looked like the team that would cap a remarkable season with a national championship appearance. They had survived a tense Elite Eight battle with Xavier. They had Luol Deng playing the best basketball of his one-year Duke career. Chris Duhon was orchestrating the offense with the calm of a fifth-year senior. And with four minutes remaining in the national semifinal, Duke held a 75-67 lead over second-seeded UConn.

Then the floor collapsed.

Daniel Ewing, who had been outstanding — hitting three straight buckets including a three-pointer that built the eight-point cushion — and the rest of the Blue Devils simply stopped scoring. UConn’s Emeka Okafor took over both ends of the court. He launched himself to swat a Deng shot, then hit a smooth 12-footer on the other end to cut the deficit to one. Ben Gordon, the Huskies’ brilliant guard, found seams in Duke’s suddenly porous defense. The lead evaporated.

The sequence that ended Duke’s season will live forever in Final Four lore. With 26 seconds remaining and Duke trailing 75-74, Okafor missed a shot. The ball bounced off the rim and hung in the air for what felt like a full second. Deng had position. He reached for the rebound. And Okafor, with the strength and reflexes that would make him the #2 pick in the NBA Draft five weeks later, simply ripped the ball from Deng’s hands — spun, saw the rim, and laid it in. UConn 76, Duke 75.

“I just saw this orange object floating in the air, and it said ‘Grab me,’” Okafor said afterward. “I grabbed it, spun, saw the rim, thought it would be a good idea to put it in, and that’s what I did.”

Duke called timeout. Redick drove the lane on the ensuing possession, looking for either a layup or a foul. Okafor was waiting. He got a hand on the ball without a whistle. UConn’s Rashad Anderson hit two free throws to push the lead to 78-75. Redick’s three-point attempt to tie barely grazed the rim. Okafor grabbed the rebound, hit a free throw, and it was 79-75. Duhon raced upcourt and banked in a 38-foot three-pointer as time expired — making the final score 79-78, a margin that suggested a close game. It was not a close game. Duke had been outscored 12-0 in the final four minutes and thirty-six seconds before Duhon’s meaningless heave.

Deng finished with 16 points and 12 rebounds. Ewing had been brilliant before the drought. Redick competed. But Duke could not overcome its season-long rebounding deficit against UConn’s physical frontline, and the final sequence — Okafor physically overpowering Deng for a rebound that should have been Duke’s — was the season’s fatal flaw distilled into a single play.

Deng called it the most haunting loss of his career. He later said on the Knuckleheads Podcast: “Till now, I tell people it’s the one loss that, you know, I’ve lost many times, but that’s the one loss that every time I think of losing, that’s the first thing that comes to mind.”

UConn went on to beat Georgia Tech 82-73 for the national championship. For Duke, the off-season brought a different kind of earthquake: Deng declared for the NBA Draft, and the Los Angeles Lakers offered K a five-year, $40 million contract to replace Phil Jackson. For one month in the summer of 2004, the future of Duke basketball — and of a sophomore named J.J. Redick — hung in the balance.

accTourney.story: Duke advanced past Georgia Tech in the ACC Tournament semifinal, avenging the regular-season loss that had snapped their 41-game home winning streak. The Blue Devils’ defensive intensity was elevated in tournament play, setting up a championship game date with a surging Maryland team.

The five-year dynasty ended in Greensboro. Duke led Maryland by 12 with under five minutes remaining, but the sixth-seeded Terrapins — riding sophomore John Gilchrist’s tournament-long rampage — closed regulation on a 15-3 run. Gilchrist drove for a layup that drew Shelden Williams’ fifth foul with 20 seconds left, and his free throw tied it at 77. In overtime, Duke went cold while Maryland poured it on, winning 95-87 behind Gilchrist’s 26 points. It ended Duke’s 17-game ACC Tournament winning streak, dating to a 1998 loss. Gary Williams climbed the ladder to cut down the net as the crowd chanted his name — Maryland’s first ACC Tournament title in 20 years. Krzyzewski was gracious: “My hat is off to Gary and his program. He’s a fighter and his teams won’t quit.” Redick was blunt: “We didn’t execute, we didn’t take care of the ball and we didn’t make our free throws.” The dynasty’s unprecedented five-year run was over.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke came out sharp in Raleigh, building a 25-point lead midway through the first half. Shavlik Randolph led with 20 points and 8 rebounds, while Daniel Ewing added 19 — 18 in the first half alone. The full-court press suffocated Alabama State’s offense.

A 28-point rout behind balanced scoring and suffocating defense. Redick topped the scorers with 21 points on 6-of-10 shooting, including a crucial three just before halftime that extended the lead to 40-28. Deng added 20. Shelden Williams contributed a double-double. Duke advanced to the Sweet Sixteen with no drama.

Duke’s guards disrupted Illinois’ rhythm, holding the Illini to 43.8% shooting while pulling away in the second half after leading by just one at the break. The defense was the story — Duke forced turnovers and contested every shot as Illinois, a Big Ten powerhouse, never found its footing.

A tense, grinding affair in Atlanta. Xavier shot just 37.3% but kept it close, pulling within three multiple times. Deng earned regional MVP honors with 19 points, while Shelden Williams was immense — 12 points, 13 rebounds, 5 blocks. Duke’s largest lead was six with under 25 seconds remaining. Xavier’s final shots missed, and Duke punched its ticket to San Antonio for its first Final Four since the 2001 championship.

Emeka Okafor ripped the ball from Luol Deng’s hands with 26 seconds remaining and scored to give UConn a 76-75 lead. Duke had led 75-67 with four minutes left. UConn outscored Duke 12-0 in the final 4:36 before Duhon’s meaningless 38-foot bank shot at the buzzer. Okafor controlled both ends. Redick’s tying three barely grazed the rim. Deng had 16 and 12 in what he’d later call the most haunting loss of his career. UConn went on to win the national championship. Duke went home to face an even bigger earthquake: the Lakers’ $40 million offer to Coach K.
