seasons.accTournament: Champions (record 18th ACC Tournament title; beat Georgia Tech in championship)
seasons.ncaaTournament: National Champions (#1 seed South; beat Arkansas-Pine Bluff 73-44, California 68-53, Purdue 70-57, Baylor 78-71, West Virginia 78-57, Butler 61-59)
seasons.tagline: Good, Not Great — Then Great. Four years of scars. VCU. West Virginia. Villanova. Five players who had been through all of it return and refuse to let the story end the same way. K calls them “good, not great.” They prove him wrong in the most beautiful way possible. Zoubek grabs the rebound. Hayward’s half-court shot hits the backboard, hits the rim, and bounces out. Duke wins its fourth national championship. The climb is over.
seasons.overview:
Mike Krzyzewski looked at his roster before the 2009-10 season and said what he believed: “They’re good, not great.” He wasn’t being modest. He was being honest. This team had no lottery picks, no future All-Stars, no singular talent that would make opponents change their game plan out of fear. What it had was something rarer and, in the end, more valuable: five players who had been through everything together.

Senior Jon Scheyer had arrived as a pudgy freshman in 2006, watched Redick and Williams play their Senior Night game, and spent four years transforming himself from a sixth man into a consensus second-team All-American and the ACC’s leader in assist-to-turnover ratio, free throw percentage, and three-pointers per game. He set the ACC all-time single-season record for minutes played at 1,470 — a number that reflected both his durability and his indispensability. He led the team in scoring (18.2), assists (4.9), free throw percentage (.878), and steals (1.6).

Junior Kyle Singler was the team’s most complete player, averaging 17.7 points and 7.0 rebounds with a .399 three-point percentage. He played with the quiet ferocity that had defined him since arriving from Medford, Oregon — never flashy, always essential, willing to guard any position and take any shot when the moment demanded it.

Junior Nolan Smith had grown from a raw freshman into a dynamic scorer and capable floor general, averaging 17.4 points and learning to play alongside Scheyer in a two-guard system that gave Duke unprecedented backcourt versatility.

Senior Lance Thomas brought defensive toughness and a willingness to do whatever the team needed, from guarding the opponent’s best player to setting screens to diving on the floor for loose balls. His hard foul on Butler’s Gordon Hayward in the championship game — reviewed and ruled non-flagrant — was the embodiment of the team’s identity: we will compete on every possession, and we will not apologize for it.

And then there was senior Brian Zoubek. The 7-1 center from New Jersey had spent four injury-plagued years on the margins of the program, never healthy enough to establish himself. In 2009-10, K inserted him into the starting lineup, and he blossomed. Zoubek averaged 7.6 rebounds and provided the interior presence that Duke had lacked since Shelden Williams’ departure — not as a scorer, but as a rebounder, shot-alterer, and physical enforcer. The Plumlee brothers (Miles as a sophomore, Mason as a freshman) added frontcourt depth that no Duke team since 2006 had possessed.

Between them, the five core players had played 638 games together. They knew each other’s movements, tendencies, and breaking points. They had survived VCU (first-round loss, 2007), West Virginia (physical beatdown, 2008), and Villanova (blowout, 2009). Three consecutive years of March failure had hardened them in ways that no practice could replicate.

The season started with a statement: Duke won the NIT Season Tip-Off and rolled through a non-conference schedule that included wins over Connecticut and Gonzaga. ACC play was dominant — 13-3, tied for the regular season championship. The Blue Devils went undefeated at Cameron Indoor Stadium for the entire season, setting a school record with 17 home wins. They won the ACC Tournament for a record 18th time, tying and then surpassing UNC for the most in conference history.

Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as the #1 seed in the South Region. The draw was kind but never easy.

The tournament run was methodical and relentless. Arkansas-Pine Bluff fell 73-44 in the first round. California went down 68-53 in the second round. Fourth-seeded Purdue was dispatched 70-57 in the Sweet Sixteen, with Zoubek grabbing 10 rebounds. Third-seeded Baylor fell 78-71 in the Elite Eight, with Lance Thomas pulling down 8 offensive rebounds in a physical, grinding affair.

The Final Four in Indianapolis brought a familiar opponent: second-seeded West Virginia. The same program that had physically manhandled Duke out of the tournament two years earlier. But this was a different Duke team. Zoubek was healthy and starting. The Plumlees provided interior reinforcement. Thomas and Singler were two years older and two years tougher. Duke demolished the Mountaineers 78-57 — the most emphatic victory of the tournament run and the sweetest revenge on the program’s four-year journey. Singler had 21 points. Scheyer added 23. The team committed just 6 turnovers against 20 assists. It was total domination.

The championship game against fifth-seeded Butler was unlike anything else. Butler, coached by 33-year-old Brad Stevens, was the real-life “Hoosiers” — a tiny school from Indianapolis playing for the national title in a stadium just miles from its campus. The crowd of 70,390 at Lucas Oil Stadium was overwhelmingly pro-Butler. Duke was the villain in someone else’s fairy tale.

Nobody led by more than six points. Butler shot 34.5% (20-of-58) but competed on every possession with the defensive intensity and team cohesion that had carried them through the tournament. Duke won with defense — holding Butler to its worst shooting night in months — and with clutch shot-making from Singler (19 points, 3-of-6 from three, 6-of-6 from the line in the second half). Zoubek finished with 10 rebounds and 2 blocks, altering shots at the rim all night.

The final sequence will live forever in college basketball history. With Duke leading 61-59 and 3.6 seconds remaining, Butler’s Gordon Hayward missed a shot and Zoubek grabbed the rebound. He was fouled immediately. Zoubek made the first free throw. He intentionally missed the second, knowing that a Butler three-pointer would only tie the game if Duke led by three rather than two. But the miss was long, and Butler got the ball at half-court.

Hayward took the inbound, dribbled twice, and launched a half-court heave as time expired. The ball flew toward the basket, hit the backboard, hit the rim — and bounced out.

70,390 people gasped. Then the Duke bench erupted.

“Both teams and all the kids on both teams played their hearts out,” Krzyzewski said. “There was never more than a couple, a few points separating, so a lot of kids made big plays for both teams.”

Singler was named Final Four Most Outstanding Player (averaging 20.0 points and 9.0 rebounds in the two games). Scheyer and Smith were named to the All-Final Four team. Scheyer, Singler, and Smith had each scored over 600 points during the season — only the second trio in Duke history to do so, after Jason Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Mike Dunleavy Jr. in 2001-02.

After the game, before the team said a prayer, K told them: “I’ve said throughout the year they were good, then they were really good, then they were really good with great character. But I told them before we said a prayer, that: ‘You are a great team.’”

It was K’s fourth national championship, tying Adolph Rupp for second on the all-time list behind John Wooden. It was Duke’s first title since 2001 — a nine-year drought that had included the Lakers drama, the Redick era’s unfulfilled promise, the VCU humiliation, and three years of rebuilding with a core that refused to break.

The 2009-10 national championship was not won by the most talented Duke team. It was won by the most together one — a team forged by failure, hardened by loss, and led by players who had been told they were good, not great, and decided to prove that sometimes good and together is enough to be great.

unc.story: The most lopsided Duke victory over UNC in the modern rivalry. Without Tyler Hansbrough and the senior class that had won the 2009 national championship, Roy Williams’ Tar Heels were in free fall — a young team that would finish 20-17, one of UNC’s worst seasons of the Williams era. Duke demolished them by 32 points at Cameron, the second-largest Duke win in the history of the series. It was a stark role reversal: a year earlier, UNC had scored 101 at Cameron. Now Duke was winning by 32. The balance of Tobacco Road power had shifted decisively back to Durham.

Duke completed the season sweep with another dominant victory at Chapel Hill — remarkably, another 82-50 scoreline, matching the margin from the game at Cameron exactly. The identical scores were an eerie coincidence that underlined how wide the gap had become between the two programs. Scheyer and Singler controlled the game. UNC, struggling through a rebuilding year, had no answer. It was the first time Duke had swept UNC in a season since 2002 and the first time both games had been blowouts since the Doherty-era collapses of 2001-02.

theGame.title: Hayward’s Shot Bounces Out: The Fourth Championship
theGame.headline: Duke 61, Butler 59. Zoubek grabs the rebound with 3.6 seconds left. Makes one free throw. Misses the second intentionally. Gordon Hayward launches from half court. It hits the backboard. It hits the rim. It bounces out. Duke wins its fourth national championship. The four-year climb is over.
theGame.story:
They had been through everything. VCU in 2007 — a first-round loss that ended a nine-year Sweet Sixteen streak. West Virginia in 2008 — a physical beating that exposed a team without toughness. Villanova in 2009 — a 23-point blowout that said: you’re still not ready. Three years of March pain, each one leaving a different scar.

Now they were in Indianapolis, in Lucas Oil Stadium, 70,390 people in the building and most of them rooting against Duke. Butler, coached by 33-year-old Brad Stevens, was the Cinderella story America wanted — a tiny school from the other side of Indianapolis, playing for a national championship in its own backyard, the real-life “Hoosiers” that Hollywood could never have scripted.

Duke was the villain. The Blue Devils embraced it.

The game was a masterpiece of tension. Nobody led by more than six. Butler shot 34.5% but competed with a ferocity that matched Duke’s own. Every possession was contested. Every rebound was fought for. Every timeout felt like a plot twist in a story that couldn’t possibly end cleanly.

Singler was Duke’s anchor: 19 points, 3-of-6 from three, 6-of-6 from the free throw line in the second half. He played all 40 minutes. Scheyer added 15 and ran the offense with the composure of a player who had waited four years for this moment. Zoubek was immense inside — 10 rebounds, 2 blocks, and countless altered shots that forced Butler into the low-percentage contested jumpers that defined their night.

Lance Thomas made the play that defined Duke’s identity. With 5:07 remaining, Hayward drove for what looked like an easy layup. Thomas took him down hard. The officials reviewed the play and ruled it non-flagrant. It was the physical, unyielding, we-will-not-let-you-score-easy toughness that this team had spent four years building. The team that had been pushed around by West Virginia in 2008 was pushing back.

With 3.6 seconds remaining, Duke led 60-59. Hayward missed a shot. Zoubek — the 7-1 senior who had spent four years fighting through injuries to arrive at this moment — grabbed the rebound. He was fouled immediately.

Zoubek made the first free throw. Duke led 61-59. He intentionally missed the second — a strategic decision to prevent Butler from getting the ball at half-court with a chance to tie with a three-pointer.

The miss was long. Butler corralled it at half-court. Hayward took the inbound and dribbled twice toward the Duke basket. With time expiring, he launched a half-court heave.

The ball sailed through the air. It hit the backboard. It hit the rim.

And it bounced out.

70,390 people gasped. The Duke bench erupted. Scheyer fell to the floor. Thomas threw his arms around Singler. K embraced his players. Zoubek, who had spent four years wondering if his body would ever let him play a full season, stood at center court with tears streaming down his face.

Butler’s Brad Stevens said it best: “We just came up a bounce short. There’s certainly nothing to hang your head about.”

K, in the locker room before the team said a prayer, delivered the line he had been building toward all season: “I’ve said throughout the year they were good, then they were really good, then they were really good with great character. But I told them that: ‘You are a great team.’”

Singler was named Final Four Most Outstanding Player (20.0 points, 9.0 rebounds per game in the two Final Four games). Scheyer and Smith were named to the All-Final Four team. It was K’s fourth national championship, tying Adolph Rupp for second all-time behind John Wooden.

The 2009-10 Duke Blue Devils were not the most talented championship team in program history. They had no future NBA All-Stars. They had no consensus National Player of the Year. They had no player selected in the first round of the NBA Draft that June. What they had was 638 games of shared experience, four years of accumulated scar tissue, and an identity that had been forged in the fires of three consecutive early tournament exits.

They were good, not great. Then they were great.

The four-year climb that began in the wreckage of VCU ended with confetti falling in Indianapolis. Every scar had been worth it.

accTourney.story: Duke won its record 18th ACC Tournament championship — surpassing UNC for the most in conference history. The title was the program’s third in four years (2009, 2010) and the 12th under K. Scheyer, Singler, and Smith all contributed in a balanced performance. The seniors cut down the nets knowing they had one more tournament to play — the one that mattered most.

ncaaTourney.story: A 29-point blowout to open the tournament. Duke was sharp, focused, and showed none of the anxiety that had plagued previous tournament openers. The 16-seed was dispatched without drama.

Duke controlled a competitive California team with defensive intensity and balanced scoring. A 15-point win that felt even more comfortable than the margin suggested. The Blue Devils advanced to the Sweet Sixteen — the round that had been their graveyard the previous year against Villanova.

The Sweet Sixteen demon was exorcised. Zoubek grabbed 10 rebounds as Duke’s improved interior presence neutralized Purdue’s physical frontcourt. The 13-point margin was the most comfortable Duke had looked in a Sweet Sixteen game since 2006. The team that had been eliminated in this round three straight years was now advancing past it with authority.

A physical, grinding Elite Eight battle against a talented Baylor team. Lance Thomas pulled down 8 offensive rebounds in a performance that embodied the team’s toughness. Duke survived Baylor’s size and athleticism with defensive discipline and free throw shooting. The Blue Devils were heading to the Final Four for the first time since 2004 — a six-year drought that had felt like a lifetime.

Revenge. The same West Virginia program that had physically dominated Duke out of the 2008 tournament. But this was a different Duke team: Zoubek healthy and anchoring the paint, the Plumlees providing frontcourt depth, Thomas and Singler two years tougher. Duke committed just 6 turnovers against 20 assists — a ratio of 3.33, the best of the season — and demolished the Mountaineers 78-57. Singler had 21. Scheyer added 23. It was total, methodical domination. The team that had been pushed around in 2008 was doing the pushing. The ghost of Buffalo was finally buried.

The game that had everything. Butler, the real-life “Hoosiers,” playing in its own backyard in Indianapolis. 70,390 fans, most rooting against Duke. Nobody led by more than six. Singler’s 19 points (6-of-6 FTs in the second half). Thomas’s hard foul on Hayward at 5:07. Zoubek’s 10 rebounds and 2 blocks. Then the final sequence: Hayward misses, Zoubek grabs the rebound with 3.6 seconds left, makes one free throw, intentionally misses the second. Hayward launches from half court. It hits the backboard. Hits the rim. Bounces out. Duke 61, Butler 59. Fourth national championship. K ties Adolph Rupp. And in the locker room, before a prayer: “You are a great team.”

seasons.tagline: The Greatest What-If. The defending national champions return two All-Americans, add the #1 recruit in the country — and lose him after eight games. Kyrie Irving jams his toe against Butler on December 4 and disappears for 26 games. Nolan Smith becomes the ACC Player of the Year. Seth Curry grows up overnight. Duke goes 24-2 without its superstar freshman and wins a third straight ACC Tournament championship. Irving returns for the NCAAs, scores 28 in the Sweet Sixteen, but it isn’t enough. Derrick Williams drops 32 and Arizona ends the dream 93-77. Seven future NBA players were on this roster. The team that might have gone undefeated instead became college basketball’s most tantalizing what-if.
seasons.overview:
The 2010-11 Duke Blue Devils entered the season as the defending national champions, ranked #1 in the preseason AP poll, and armed with a roster that looked, on paper, like the most talented team Mike Krzyzewski had ever assembled. Seniors Nolan Smith and Kyle Singler returned as proven stars from the championship run. The Plumlee brothers — junior Miles and sophomore Mason — anchored the frontcourt. Sophomore Seth Curry and sophomore Andre Dawkins provided backcourt depth. Sophomore Ryan Kelly added stretch-four versatility. And arriving as the crown jewel of the recruiting class was Kyrie Irving, the #1 overall recruit in America, a point guard from St. Patrick’s in New Jersey whose handle, vision, and scoring ability were already drawing Chris Paul comparisons.

For eight games, the dream was real. Irving averaged 17.4 points on 53.2% shooting with 5.1 assists, 3.8 rebounds, and 1.5 steals per game. Duke was 8-0, obliterating everyone in its path. The Smith-Irving-Singler trio looked unstoppable — three future NBA players sharing backcourt responsibilities, each capable of creating for himself and others, each deadly from outside. Cleveland Cavaliers scouts attended every game, already locked in on Irving as a potential #1 pick.

Then, on December 4, 2010, in a rematch of the national championship game against Butler, Irving jammed his right big toe. He went back in and finished the game. It seemed like nothing — a stubbed toe, the kind of injury that happens a dozen times a season. “I feel like he just stubbed his toe,” the team’s athletic trainer recalled. “Something you see in a lot of basketball games.” But the injury didn’t heal. The ligament damage was worse than anyone initially realized. Irving missed 26 games. The #1 recruit in the country, the player who was supposed to make Duke unbeatable, was gone.

What happened next proved the character of this team. Rather than collapse, Duke adapted. Seth Curry, a quiet sophomore from the basketball family that produced his older brother Steph, moved into the starting point guard role and played with poise far beyond his experience. Nolan Smith, freed from sharing playmaking duties, unleashed the full scope of his game. He became the first player in ACC history to lead the conference in both scoring and assists in the same season, averaging 20.6 points, 5.1 assists, 4.5 rebounds, and 1.2 steals per game. He scored a career-high 34 points in a home win over #20 North Carolina, making 13 field goals. He scored 25 or more points in ten games and 18 or more in all sixteen ACC games. He was named ACC Player of the Year, a consensus first-team All-American, and a finalist for the Naismith Trophy, Wooden Award, Oscar Robertson Trophy, and Bob Cousy Award. He was the son of the late Derek Smith, a member of Louisville’s 1980 championship team who died in 1996 when Nolan was eight years old. The tattoo on his right arm reads: “Forever Watching.” His senior season was a masterpiece born from necessity.

Kyle Singler, the steady, unflashy Oregonian who never missed a big moment, averaged 16.9 points and 6.3 rebounds while earning first-team All-ACC honors for the second consecutive season. Together, Smith and Singler carried the load through conference play, going 13-3 in the ACC and finishing as co-regular-season champions with North Carolina.

The UNC rivalry produced a classic split. At Cameron Indoor on February 9, Smith erupted for 34 points in a 79-73 victory — a performance that announced his candidacy for every national award. At Chapel Hill on March 5, UNC exacted revenge on Senior Night with an 81-67 win that clinched the outright regular-season title. Smith scored 30 and Curry added 20, but the Tar Heels, led by Harrison Barnes’ 18 and Kendall Marshall’s 15 points and 11 assists, pulled away in the second half. Duke got the last word the following week, beating Maryland, Virginia Tech, and UNC in the ACC Tournament to claim the program’s record 19th conference tournament championship and its third in a row.

As the #1 seed in the West Region, Duke entered the NCAA Tournament with a tantalizing storyline: Kyrie Irving was coming back. He had missed 26 games, hadn’t played competitive basketball since early December, and was attempting to integrate back into a team that had learned to win without him. In the first round against Hampton, Irving came off the bench and led the team with 14 points in 20 minutes. Duke won 87-45. In the second round against Michigan, Smith scored 24 as Duke survived 73-71 — a game that also gave K his 900th career victory, making him only the fourth coach to reach that milestone.

The Sweet Sixteen, held in Anaheim, brought #5-seeded Arizona and its transcendent sophomore, Derrick Williams. Duke led 44-38 at halftime despite Williams scoring 25 first-half points, including going 5-for-5 from three-point range. But in the second half, Arizona erupted for 55 points. Williams finished with 32 and 13 rebounds in a career-defining performance. Irving, playing in only his eleventh game of the season, was magnificent in defeat — 28 points on 8-of-17 shooting — but the chemistry that might have existed with a full season of development simply wasn’t there. Arizona won 93-77. “I’ve never seen Duke get beat like this,” Kenny Smith said on the broadcast. “I’ve never seen a Duke team this rattled.”

The season ended at 32-5. Kyrie Irving declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #1 overall by the Cleveland Cavaliers. Nolan Smith went 21st to the Portland Trail Blazers. Kyle Singler went 33rd to the Detroit Pistons. In total, seven players from this roster would play in the NBA: Irving, Smith, Singler, Mason Plumlee, Miles Plumlee, Seth Curry, and Ryan Kelly.

For Duke fans, the 2010-11 season lives in a permanent state of torment and wonder. The belief — held by Krzyzewski, by analysts, by anyone who watched those first eight games — is that a healthy Kyrie Irving would have produced an undefeated season and back-to-back national championships. Duke Basketball Report put it bluntly: “It’s our firm belief that the Blue Devils were poised for an undefeated season when Irving went out with a toe injury and a second set of back-to-back national championships.” Instead, a stubbed toe — just a stubbed toe — changed everything. The greatest what-if in Duke basketball history.

The Arizona sophomore scored 32 points with 13 rebounds — including 25 first-half points and 5-for-5 from three — in one of the greatest individual tournament performances in history. seasons.tagline: The Record, The Buzzer-Beater, and The Collapse. Coach K becomes the all-time winningest coach in college basketball history, embracing his mentor Bob Knight at midcourt in Madison Square Garden after win #903. Austin Rivers, Doc’s kid, hits one of the most iconic shots in rivalry history — a buzzer-beating three at Chapel Hill to beat UNC 85-84. Duke goes 8-0 on the ACC road, the 13th team in conference history to run the table away from home. Then March arrives, and #15-seed Lehigh — led by a skinny guard named C.J. McCollum who pours in 30 — ends everything in the most painful way possible. A season of history and heartbreak.
seasons.overview:
The 2011-12 Duke Blue Devils entered the season with more questions than answers. Gone were Kyrie Irving (#1 overall pick), Nolan Smith (#21), and Kyle Singler (#33) — three of the previous year’s four leading scorers and the heart of back-to-back #1-ranked teams. Only 47.2% of the previous season’s scoring returned. There was no established point guard, no proven go-to scorer, and a roster full of talented but largely unproven role players stepping into bigger roles.

The answer, it turned out, wore #0 and carried the last name of an NBA head coach.

Austin Rivers, the #2 overall recruit in the 2011 class and the son of Boston Celtics head coach Doc Rivers, arrived in Durham as the most hyped Duke freshman since Irving. And unlike Irving, he stayed healthy. Rivers became just the third Duke player to lead the team in scoring as a freshman, averaging 15.5 points per game. He scored in double figures in 30 of 34 games. He was the unanimous ACC Rookie of the Year and the first Duke freshman in program history to earn All-America honors.

Around Rivers, a supporting cast emerged. Seth Curry, now a junior, evolved from a spot-up three-point shooter into a complete player, averaging 13.6 points and leading the team in assists while shooting 43% from three. Mason Plumlee, a junior, took a quantum leap — averaging 11.1 points and 9.2 rebounds while leading the team with 33 dunks and anchoring the defense with 26 blocks. His older brother Miles, a senior, contributed 6.5 points and 5.7 rebounds. Ryan Kelly, a junior stretch four at 6-11, averaged 11.8 points and provided the floor spacing that opened driving lanes for Rivers and Curry. Junior Andre Dawkins added 8.2 points off the bench. And a trio of freshmen arrived: Quinn Cook, the future team captain from Washington, D.C.; Alex Murphy; and Michael Gbinije.

The season’s most historic moment came on November 15, 2011, at Madison Square Garden. Duke faced Michigan State in the Champions Classic, and when the Blue Devils won 74-69 behind 26 points from Andre Dawkins, Mike Krzyzewski recorded his 903rd career victory — breaking the all-time record previously held by his mentor and former coach, Bob Knight. Knight was in the building. At the final buzzer, the two men embraced at midcourt. “I just told him, ‘Coach, I’m not sure people tell you this, but I love you and I love what you’ve done for me, and thank you,’” Krzyzewski said afterward. It was one of the most powerful moments in the history of the sport.

Duke started 15-0 and was ranked #1 in the AP poll for nine consecutive weeks. The first loss came on January 14 at Florida State, 76-73 — one of only four losses all season that came in true road games. The Blue Devils would go on to post a perfect 8-0 ACC road record, becoming only the 13th team in the conference’s 58-year history to accomplish that feat. They won 16 games by 10 points or fewer — the second-most such victories in program history — a testament to a team that won on toughness and grit more than overwhelming talent.

The rivalry games produced one of the most iconic moments in Duke-UNC history.

On February 8, 2012, at the Dean Smith Center in Chapel Hill, Duke trailed by 10 points with three minutes remaining. UNC, ranked #5 and led by Tyler Zeller’s dominant first half, appeared to have the game sealed. But Duke launched a furious rally. Tyler Thornton hit a three. Ryan Kelly scored on a baseline jumper. UNC missed critical free throws down the stretch. Zeller accidentally tipped in an errant Duke three-point attempt, cutting the lead to one. And then, with time expiring, Austin Rivers caught the ball on the right wing, rose, and buried a three-pointer as the buzzer sounded. Duke 85, North Carolina 84. Rivers finished with 29 points. The celebration at midcourt was bedlam. It remains one of the five most replayed shots in the history of the rivalry.

The return game at Cameron Indoor on March 3 — Senior Night for Miles Plumlee — was the opposite. UNC never trailed, built a commanding lead, and won 88-70, clinching the outright ACC regular-season championship and denying Duke a share of the title. Smith and Curry combined to score the majority of Duke’s points, but the Tar Heels were simply too deep and too talented.

In the ACC Tournament, Duke beat Virginia Tech before falling to Florida State in the semifinals — a loss that stung because Curry’s desperation heave at the buzzer rimmed out, leaving Duke one possession short. Florida State went on to beat UNC for the tournament title.

Duke earned a #2 seed in the South Region of the NCAA Tournament, with overall #1 seed Kentucky at the top of the bracket — a potential rematch exactly twenty years after the Laettner game. But the Blue Devils never got close. In the Round of 64, #15-seed Lehigh, the Patriot League champion, stunned Duke 75-70. Mountain Hawks guard C.J. McCollum — a future NBA starter — poured in 30 points, including 18 in the second half, dismantling Duke’s defense with a performance that announced his own future stardom. Mason Plumlee and Rivers each scored 19, but it wasn’t enough. It was only the fourth first-round upset loss in 28 NCAA Tournament appearances under Krzyzewski.

“The game is a great game,” K said afterward. “I’ve been in it for 37 years, and it takes you to incredible highs. And it also takes you to incredible lows. And tonight’s one of those lows. But it wasn’t just our doing. They played that well.”

Miles Plumlee, playing his final game, offered advice for future Blue Devils: “First, I would tell them you’re becoming part of a family and we’ve got the best coaches, best staff, the best everything.”

Austin Rivers declared for the NBA Draft and was selected 10th overall by the New Orleans Hornets. Miles Plumlee was drafted 26th by the Indiana Pacers. The season ended in the most painful way possible, but the pieces left behind — Mason Plumlee, Seth Curry, Ryan Kelly, Quinn Cook — would form the backbone of Duke teams that would reach the Elite Eight in 2013 and the national championship in 2015.

seasons.ncaaTournament: #2 seed Midwest; beat Albany 73-61, Creighton 66-50, Michigan State 71-61; lost to #1 Louisville 85-63 Elite Eight
seasons.tagline: The Resurrection of Ryan Kelly. Senior Mason Plumlee becomes ACC Player of the Year. Freshman Quinn Cook runs the point. Duke beats four top-5 teams and goes 16-0 at home. Then Ryan Kelly misses 13 games with a foot injury and returns to score 36 against Miami in one of the greatest individual performances in Cameron Indoor history. “One for the ages,” K says. “Probably as good a performance as any player has had — a Duke player has had — in Cameron.” Seth Curry drops 29 on Michigan State to reach the Elite Eight. But Louisville’s full-court press, Kevin Ware’s horrifying broken leg, and a 17-2 Cardinals run end the season 85-63 in Indianapolis. Plumlee goes out with 17 and 12 in his final game. Three seniors, three futures, one last ride together.
seasons.overview:
The 2012-13 Duke Blue Devils were the culmination of a senior class that had arrived in Durham during the 2010 national championship season and grown up together through Irving’s injury, Austin Rivers’ one-and-done departure, and the Lehigh catastrophe. Seniors Mason Plumlee, Seth Curry, and Ryan Kelly had seen everything. Now it was their turn to lead.

Plumlee was the headliner. The middle Plumlee brother, who had arrived as a raw, athletic freshman in 2009, emerged as one of the most complete players in the country. He averaged 17.1 points on 59.9% shooting and 9.9 rebounds per game, posted 18 double-doubles, led the team with 33 dunks and 26 blocks, and was named ACC Player of the Year, first-team All-ACC, first-team Academic All-American, and a Senior CLASS Award finalist. His 32-point, 9-rebound demolition of Wake Forest in January was the kind of performance that announced a player had become unstoppable.

Seth Curry, Steph’s little brother who had transferred from Liberty before the 2010 championship season, completed one of the most satisfying developmental arcs in Duke history. He averaged 17.5 points per game and shot 43.8% from three-point range, earning first-team All-ACC honors. His entire Duke career had been spent ranked in the top 10 of the AP poll. He led the team in scoring in the NCAA Tournament at 21.0 points per game, including a masterful 29-point, 6-for-9-from-three performance against Michigan State in the Sweet Sixteen that sent Duke to the Elite Eight.

Ryan Kelly, the 6-11 stretch four from Raleigh, was the third senior and the season’s most dramatic storyline. Kelly averaged 12.9 points and 5.3 rebounds when healthy, and Duke was 21-1 with him in the lineup and 4-0 against top-5 opponents. But in mid-January, a foot injury sidelined him for 13 straight games. Duke went 9-4 without him, losing its grip on the #1 ranking and its aura of invincibility. Then, on March 2, Kelly returned against #5 Miami in Cameron Indoor Stadium and produced one of the greatest individual performances in the building’s history: 36 points on 12-of-19 shooting, leading Duke to a 79-76 victory. K was almost speechless afterward: “I mean, me saying ‘spectacular’ or whatever doesn’t do his performance justice. One for the ages. Probably as good a performance as any player has had — a Duke player has had — in Cameron.”

Sophomore Quinn Cook, who had shown flashes during the Battle 4 Atlantis tournament as a freshman (he won tournament MVP), ran the point with increasing confidence and led the team in assists. Freshman Rasheed Sulaimon provided a scoring punch off the bench, including a second-half takeover against #4 Ohio State when he scored all 17 of his points after halftime to spark a comeback from an 8-point deficit. Sophomore Amile Jefferson contributed rebounding and defense. Josh Hairston and Tyler Thornton provided senior-level toughness.

The non-conference schedule was elite. Duke won the Battle 4 Atlantis in the Bahamas, beating Minnesota, VCU, and #2 Louisville on three consecutive days — with Cook earning MVP honors. They beat #3 Kentucky and #4 Ohio State in Cameron. They were ranked in the top 10 of the AP poll for the entire season, including five weeks at #1. They went 16-0 at home and beat four top-5 opponents.

The UNC rivalry produced a season sweep. At Cameron Indoor on February 13, Plumlee had 23 points and 11 rebounds and Cook scored 18 as Duke rallied from a 26-minute deficit to beat the Tar Heels 73-68. UNC, led by P.J. Hairston’s 23, led for the first 26 minutes before Duke’s experience took over. Seth Curry’s three from in front of the bench gave Duke its first lead, and Tyler Thornton — who had missed 12 of his previous 14 three-point attempts — hit two consecutive corner threes to blow the game open. At Chapel Hill on March 9, Duke completed the sweep in a game that sealed the program’s confidence heading into March.

The ACC Tournament ended early with a quarterfinal loss to Maryland. But as a #2 seed in the Midwest Region, Duke found its tournament form. Albany fell 73-61. #22 Creighton was dispatched 66-50. In the Sweet Sixteen, Curry’s 29-point masterpiece against #9 Michigan State — 6-for-9 from three, nine points during one decisive 11-2 run — sent Duke to the Elite Eight with a 71-61 victory. K improved to 3-1 against Tom Izzo in the NCAA Tournament.

The Elite Eight brought #1 overall seed Louisville and one of the most emotionally devastating games in tournament history. The game was tied 42-42 early in the second half when Louisville guard Kevin Ware landed awkwardly after contesting a three-pointer and suffered a compound fracture of his right leg — the bone visibly breaking through the skin on national television. Players from both teams were in tears. Ware was stretchered off the court. The Cardinals, galvanized by the horror, went on a 17-2 run. Duke missed 10 consecutive field goals against Louisville’s suffocating full-court press. Russ Smith and Peyton Siva combined for 39 points and just one turnover. The final score was 85-63 — the largest tournament loss of Krzyzewski’s career since the 1990 UNLV title game.

Plumlee went out fighting: 17 points and 12 rebounds, his 18th double-double. Curry and Cook each added 12. It wasn’t enough. Louisville went on to win the national championship (later vacated by the NCAA).

Mason Plumlee was drafted 22nd overall by the Brooklyn Nets. Ryan Kelly went 48th to the Los Angeles Lakers. Seth Curry went undrafted but built an 11-year NBA career. The senior class departed with 111 wins against 14 losses, a .888 winning percentage that ranked third in program history. They had been to a national championship, an Elite Eight, survived a 15-seed upset, and carried Duke through the departure of Kyrie Irving and Austin Rivers. They were the bridge between the 2010 title and the 2015 title. The Brotherhood depended on players exactly like them.

uncGames.story: UNC led for the first 26 minutes behind P.J. Hairston's 23 points. Duke trailed entering the second half. But the seniors took over: Plumlee finished with 23 and 11, Cook scored 18, and Seth Curry's three from in front of the bench gave Duke its first lead. Tyler Thornton, who had missed 12 of his previous 14 three-point attempts, drilled two consecutive corner threes to blow the game open. Duke's sixth straight win over UNC and sixth in eight meetings.

Duke completed the season sweep of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, its third sweep in four years (2010, 2013). The victory sealed the seniors' dominance of the rivalry — Plumlee, Curry, and Kelly finished their careers having won six of eight against the Tar Heels.

theGame.title: Ryan Kelly's Resurrection
theGame.headline: After missing 13 straight games with a foot injury, Ryan Kelly returns and scores 36 points to lead Duke past #5 Miami. K calls it 'one for the ages — probably as good a performance as any player has had — a Duke player has had — in Cameron.'
theGame.story: Ryan Kelly hadn't played a basketball game in six weeks. A foot injury — the same kind of injury that haunts big men, the same kind that had sidelined Kyrie Irving two years earlier — had kept the 6-11 senior out of 13 consecutive games. Duke went 9-4 without him, losing its #1 ranking and looking vulnerable for the first time all season. When Kelly returned on March 2 against #5 Miami, no one expected what happened. He scored 36 points on 12-of-19 shooting, including four three-pointers, leading Duke to a 79-76 win over the Hurricanes. He played like a man who had been storing energy for six weeks and released it all in 37 minutes. The Cameron Crazies erupted. K searched for words in the postgame press conference and couldn't find ones big enough. Duke was 21-1 with Kelly in the lineup that season. They were a different team with him — and his return announced that Duke was ready for March.

accTourney.story: Duke's ACC Tournament ended early with a quarterfinal loss to Maryland, a disappointing exit for a team that had been ranked in the top 10 all season.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke handled the Great Danes comfortably in the tournament opener, with balanced scoring across the lineup.
Duke's defense suffocated #22 Creighton, holding Doug McDermott and the Bluejays to 50 points and advancing to the Sweet Sixteen.

Seth Curry's masterpiece. The senior sharpshooter scored 29 points on 6-for-9 from three-point range, including nine points during a decisive 11-2 run that broke Michigan State's back. Sulaimon hit 12-of-14 free throws. Duke reached the Elite Eight and K improved to 3-1 against Tom Izzo in the NCAA Tournament. It was Duke's 30th win, K's 13th 30-win season — tied with Kentucky for the most in NCAA history.

The game was tied 42-42 in the second half when Louisville guard Kevin Ware landed awkwardly after contesting a three-pointer and suffered a compound fracture of his right leg — the bone visibly breaking through the skin on national television. Players from both teams were in tears. The Cardinals, galvanized by the horror, went on a 17-2 run. Duke missed 10 consecutive field goals against Louisville's suffocating full-court press. Russ Smith and Peyton Siva combined for 39 points and just one turnover. Mason Plumlee went out fighting — 17 points and 12 rebounds, his 18th double-double — but the final score was 85-63, the largest tournament defeat of K's career since the 1990 UNLV title game. Louisville went on to win the national championship (later vacated by the NCAA). The senior class of Plumlee, Curry, and Kelly departed with 111 wins, 14 losses, and a .888 career winning percentage.

unc.story: Mason Plumlee dominated with 23 points and 11 rebounds as Duke rallied from a 26-minute deficit. Quinn Cook scored 18 and hit critical free throws down the stretch. UNC led for most of the game, but Plumlee's interior presence — finishing over and around the Tar Heel bigs — tilted the game in the final minutes. The win was Duke's fourth straight over Carolina.

Seth Curry was unconscious, hitting his first seven shots as Duke raced to a massive early lead and never looked back. Plumlee added 23 points and 13 rebounds for the double-double. UNC shot just 34% from the field and an abysmal 1-of-14 from three-point range. The 16-point blowout in Chapel Hill completed the season sweep — Duke's first since 2010 — and sent K's team into March with serious momentum.

seasons.accTournament: Runner-up (beat Clemson 63-62, NC State; lost to Virginia 72-63 in championship)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #3 seed Midwest; lost to #14 Mercer 78-71 in Round of 64
seasons.tagline: Jabari and the Heartbreak. The most talented freshman since Kyrie Irving arrives alongside Mississippi State transfer Rodney Hood, and for stretches they look like the best one-two punch in college basketball. Parker averages 19.1 and 8.7, earns ACC Rookie of the Year and consensus All-American honors, and drops 30 and 11 on UNC. Hood shoots 54% and 42% from three. K gets his 900th win at Duke. But the defense is porous all season, Duke falls from the AP top 10 for the first time in 122 weeks, and in the NCAA Tournament, a veteran Mercer squad shoots 56% and closes on an 18-8 run to deliver one of the most stunning upsets of the K era. Parker finishes with 14 on 4-of-14 shooting and four turnovers. He declares for the NBA Draft and goes #2 overall. Hood goes #23. The season that had everything except March.
seasons.overview:
The 2013-14 Duke Blue Devils were a team defined by extraordinary offensive talent and a defensive vulnerability that ultimately proved fatal. Freshman Jabari Parker — the #1 recruit in America, the kid on the cover of Sports Illustrated, the son of an NBA player and a devout Mormon family from Chicago’s South Side — arrived alongside Mississippi State transfer Rodney Hood, and together they formed the most potent scoring tandem Duke had fielded since J.J. Redick and Shelden Williams.

Parker averaged 19.1 points and 8.7 rebounds per game, shooting 54.3% from the field. He was named ACC Rookie of the Year and earned consensus first-team All-American honors — the first Duke freshman to achieve that since Austin Rivers, and only the seventh in program history. His 30-point, 11-rebound destruction of #14 North Carolina at Cameron Indoor was the season’s signature performance. Roy Williams said afterward: “It’s hard to say what ‘it’ is, but whatever the hell ‘it’ is, Jabari found it.”

Hood was the perfect complement. The 6-8 wing from Meridian, Mississippi averaged 16.1 points on 51.0% shooting and 41.7% from three — the kind of efficient, smooth scorer who let the game come to him and rarely forced anything. Junior Quinn Cook ran the point and led the ACC in assists at 6.2 per game while adding 12.7 points. Senior Tyler Thornton started 25 games as the defensive anchor. Senior Andre Dawkins returned for a final season after a year away — the last remaining player from the 2010 national championship team. Sophomore Rasheed Sulaimon contributed 7.5 points off the bench, and sophomore Amile Jefferson provided rebounding and energy. Freshmen Matt Jones and Semi Ojeleye added depth.

The season began with a Battle 4 Atlantis championship in the Bahamas. Duke beat four top-5 opponents and spent time ranked #1. On January 25, K earned his 900th career victory at Duke with a win over Florida State. But the cracks were there from the start. In the ACC opener, Duke lost 79-77 to Notre Dame — the first time K had lost to one of his former assistant coaches after 18 consecutive wins. The loss dropped Duke from the AP top 10 for the first time in 122 weeks, a streak that had run since November 2007.

The defense was the persistent problem. Duke ranked outside the top 100 nationally in defensive efficiency for much of the season — an anomaly for a Krzyzewski team. The talent on the perimeter was undeniable, but the interior was thin. Without a rim protector like Mason Plumlee, opponents attacked the paint at will. Duke was 26-6 entering the NCAA Tournament but had nine losses, the most in a K-era team to receive a top-four seed.

The UNC rivalry games showcased both the ceiling and the inconsistency. At Cameron Indoor on February 13, Parker erupted for a season-high 30 points and 11 rebounds, and Hood added 24, as #4 Duke demolished #14 UNC in one of the most dominant performances in the rivalry’s modern history. The return game at Chapel Hill saw UNC exact revenge as the Tar Heels controlled the game from start to finish.

Duke reached the ACC Tournament championship game, where Virginia — playing in its first ACC title game since 1976 — controlled the tempo and won 72-63. Parker scored 23 but was 9-of-24 from the field. K earned a technical foul for throwing a marker.

As the #3 seed in the Midwest Region, Duke faced #14 Mercer in the Round of 64 in Raleigh. What followed was one of the most stunning upsets of the K era. Mercer, led by a roster of five seniors playing in their first NCAA Tournament since 1985, shot 56% from the field and 58% in the second half. Duke led 63-58 with under five minutes remaining, then went scoreless for nearly four minutes. Mercer closed on an 18-8 run. Jakob Gollon scored 20 and Daniel Coursey had 17. Parker was held to 14 points on 4-of-14 shooting with four turnovers. Hood managed just 6 points. “They sliced us up,” Hood said. “There’s no other way to put it.”

Only 3.3% of ESPN Tournament Challenge brackets picked Mercer to win. It was K’s fifth first-round loss in 29 tournament appearances.

Parker declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #2 overall by the Milwaukee Bucks. Hood went #23 to the Utah Jazz. The season ended with the bitter aftertaste of wasted potential — a team that could score on anyone but couldn’t stop anyone, a team with two future first-round picks that couldn’t survive a 14-seed. The incoming freshman Tyus Jones, watching from afar, texted K after the Mercer loss: “This won’t happen next year.”

uncGames.story: Jabari Parker's masterpiece. The freshman erupts for a season-high 30 points and 11 rebounds, including two three-pointers, as #4 Duke demolishes #14 UNC. Hood adds 24 points. Parker's largest lead stretches to 19 (74-55). Roy Williams on Parker: 'It's hard to say what it is, but whatever the hell it is, Jabari found it.' One of the most dominant individual performances in the modern history of the rivalry.
UNC exacts revenge in the regular-season finale at Chapel Hill, controlling the game from start to finish. Duke splits the season series.

theGame.title: Mercer's Miracle, Duke's Nightmare
theGame.headline: #14-seed Mercer shoots 56% and closes on an 18-8 run to stun #3-seed Duke in the Round of 64. Parker finishes 4-of-14 with four turnovers. Hood manages just 6 points. Only 3.3% of ESPN brackets picked the upset.
theGame.story: Duke led 63-58 with under five minutes remaining against a Mercer team making its first NCAA appearance since 1985. The Bears' five seniors had nothing to lose and everything to prove. What followed was a collapse that haunts Duke fans to this day. Duke went scoreless for nearly four minutes. Mercer shot 58% in the second half, slicing through Duke's porous defense with ease. Jakob Gollon scored 20 and Daniel Coursey had 17. Parker, the likely #2 pick in the NBA Draft, was held to 14 points on 4-of-14 shooting with four turnovers. Hood had just 6 points. The final score was 78-71. 'They sliced us up,' Hood said. 'There's no other way to put it.' It was K's fifth first-round loss in 29 tournament appearances and the second time in three years a mid-major had ended Duke's season in the opening round (Lehigh in 2012). Watching from afar, incoming freshman Tyus Jones texted Coach K: 'This won't happen next year.'

accTourney.story: Parker scored 18, Hood added 17, and Hood's two free throws with 3.8 seconds remaining sealed a narrow one-point victory over Clemson.
Duke advanced past NC State to reach the ACC Tournament championship game.

Virginia won its first ACC Tournament title since 1976, controlling the tempo and holding Duke's offense in check. Parker scored 23 but was 9-of-24 from the field. K earned a technical foul for throwing a marker. Malcolm Brogdon led Virginia with 23.

ncaaTourney.story: One of the most stunning upsets of the K era. Mercer's five seniors shot 56% from the field and closed on an 18-8 run. Duke led 63-58 with under 5 minutes left, then went scoreless for nearly four minutes. Parker was 4-of-14 with four turnovers. Hood had 6 points. Only 3.3% of ESPN brackets picked the upset. K's fifth first-round loss in 29 tournament appearances.

unc.story: A winter storm postponed this game over a week, and when it was finally played, Duke couldn't handle UNC's second-half surge. The Blue Devils led 51-40 with 15 minutes left, then went ice cold — missing 13 straight shots during a six-minute scoreless drought. Marcus Paige scored all 13 of his points in the second half, including two clutch baskets in the final 2:30. Leslie McDonald hit the go-ahead jumper. Parker and Cook each scored 17, but Duke shot just 36% after halftime.

If this was Jabari Parker's last game at Cameron — and it was — what a farewell. The freshman phenom erupted for a season-high 30 points and 11 rebounds, hitting 10-of-17 from the field and showcasing every tool in his arsenal. Rodney Hood added 24. Duke never trailed, shot 51% overall and 57% in the second half, and dominated UNC 34-20 on the glass. The Cameron crowd included NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell, Cowboys coach Jason Garrett, and Tony Romo cheering from behind the scorers' table. Roy Williams on Parker: 'Whatever the hell "it" is, Jabari found it.'

seasons.accTournament: Semifinalists (lost to eventual champion Notre Dame)
seasons.ncaaTournament: National Champions (#1 seed South; beat Robert Morris 85-56, San Diego State 68-49, Utah 63-57, Gonzaga 66-52, Michigan State 81-61, Wisconsin 68-63)
seasons.tagline: "This Won’t Happen Next Year." Tyus Jones had texted those words to Coach K after the Mercer loss. He was right. Jones and Jahlil Okafor arrive as a package deal — a point guard and a center who’d been plotting this since the U17 World Championship. They bring Justise Winslow and Grayson Allen with them. Senior Quinn Cook moves to shooting guard and becomes the leader the team needs. Rasheed Sulaimon is dismissed in January — K declares “eight is enough” and the team goes 17-1 the rest of the way. Dean Smith passes away in February; the Duke-UNC game is played in his memory. K earns his 1,000th career victory at St. John’s. Then March: Robert Morris, San Diego State, Utah, Gonzaga, Michigan State, Wisconsin. Down nine in the championship game, Grayson Allen puts Duke on his back, Tyus Jones scores 19 second-half points including two threes in the final five minutes, and Okafor re-enters with Duke up one and scores on back-to-back possessions to seal it. Duke 68, Wisconsin 63. K’s fifth ring. The promise fulfilled.
seasons.overview:
The 2014-15 season is the story of a promise kept.

After the Mercer loss in March 2014, incoming freshman Tyus Jones texted Coach K five words: “This won’t happen next year.” It was the kind of thing a recruit says to sound committed. It was also, as it turned out, a prophecy.

Jones and Jahlil Okafor had been planning to attend college together since they’d played on the U17 World Championship team as fifteen-year-olds. They announced their commitments to Duke on the same day in simultaneous press conferences from different states — Jones in Apple Valley, Minnesota, Okafor in Chicago. A week later, five-star small forward Justise Winslow from Houston committed too. Guard Grayson Allen from Jacksonville had committed earlier. It was the #1 recruiting class in America, the first time a school other than Kentucky had claimed that distinction in three years.

They joined a Duke team anchored by senior Quinn Cook, the kid from D.C. who had been at Duke since 2011 and had never hung a banner. Cook moved from point guard to shooting guard to accommodate Jones, a sacrifice that perfectly captured the selflessness Krzyzewski demanded. Cook was the team’s emotional leader, its heartbeat in the locker room, the guy who had stayed in Durham all summer to work out because he told the media “I don’t feel like I’ve accomplished anything.” Junior Amile Jefferson provided toughness and rebounding. Sophomore Matt Jones gave the team a long, versatile wing defender.

Duke started 14-0, the seventh such start in school history. K earned his 1,000th career victory on January 25, 2015, at St. John’s in Madison Square Garden — the first Division I men’s coach to reach that milestone. Jones hit the shots that sealed the comeback in a 77-68 win. But January also brought adversity: back-to-back double-digit losses to NC State and Miami raised questions about the team’s toughness and depth. And on January 29, Rasheed Sulaimon — the team’s most experienced reserve — was dismissed from the program. Duke was suddenly playing with eight scholarship players. K adopted a new mantra: “Eight is enough.”

The dismissal was supposed to be devastating. Instead, it was liberating. Justise Winslow’s minutes expanded. Grayson Allen went from end-of-bench freshman to critical rotation player. Matt Jones became the primary wing defender. The roles crystallized. Duke went 17-1 the rest of the way, losing only to Notre Dame in the ACC Tournament semifinals.

The rivalry with UNC was played against an emotional backdrop. Dean Smith, the legendary Tar Heel coach, passed away on February 7, 2015, at the age of 83. The February 18 game at Duke was played in his memory. Duke won 92-90 in a classic that went to overtime — Tyus Jones got hot for the Blue Devils to send the game to overtime, where Duke prevailed. The second meeting at Chapel Hill saw UNC control the action in an 84-77 victory. The ACC Tournament semifinal loss to Notre Dame meant Duke entered March without a conference championship banner.

As the #1 seed in the South Region, Duke was brilliant in the NCAA Tournament. Robert Morris fell 85-56. San Diego State was dispatched 68-49. Fifth-seeded Utah pushed Duke in the Sweet Sixteen before falling 63-57. In the Elite Eight, Tyus Jones earned six assists with zero turnovers and scored 15 points as Duke dismantled #2 Gonzaga 66-52, advancing to the Final Four. Jones was named South Regional Most Outstanding Player.

The Final Four in Indianapolis brought seventh-seeded Michigan State. The Spartans jumped out to a 14-6 lead before Duke erupted on a 14-2 run. Michigan State missed 17 of its final 20 first-half shots. Duke won 81-61, its most dominant Final Four performance in decades. The expected opponent in the championship game was undefeated #1 Kentucky — 38-0 and hunting for perfection. But Wisconsin’s Frank Kaminsky stunned the Wildcats in the other semifinal, setting up Duke-Wisconsin for the title.

The championship game was a masterpiece of resilience. Wisconsin built a nine-point second-half lead. Okafor was in foul trouble. The season appeared to be slipping away. Then Grayson Allen, the lowest-ranked recruit in Duke’s freshman class, put the team on his back. K said afterward: “We were kind of dead in the water. Grayson just put us on his back.” Allen scored 16 points off the bench, sparking the comeback that brought Duke back from the dead.

Tyus Jones took it from there. He scored 19 of his game-high 23 points in the second half, including 10 in the final seven minutes. He buried two three-pointers in the final five minutes to give Duke breathing room. With 3:30 remaining and Duke clinging to a 59-58 lead, Okafor re-entered the game and immediately scored on back-to-back possessions to push the lead to 63-58. A controversial out-of-bounds call went Duke’s way. Jones hit another three. Wisconsin’s Frank Kaminsky scored 21 and grabbed 12 rebounds, but it wasn’t enough. Duke 68, Wisconsin 63.

Jones was named the Final Four’s Most Outstanding Player — the first Duke player to earn both the Regional and Final Four MOP in the same tournament. He averaged 13.0 points and 4.5 assists across the six tournament games. Duke allowed just 56.3 points per game in the tournament — the defense that was supposed to collapse after Sulaimon’s dismissal had instead become the team’s identity.

Quinn Cook finally got his banner. He finished his career with over 4,000 minutes played — one of the most decorated four-year careers in program history. The freshmen who had joined him made the leap: Okafor was drafted #3 by the Philadelphia 76ers, Winslow went #10 to the Miami Heat, Jones went #24 to the Cleveland Cavaliers. Allen, the kid who had saved the championship game, stayed for three more years.

It was K’s fifth national championship, tying him with the all-time record. It was Duke’s fifth title overall. It was the culmination of the Resurgence Era — three championships in six years (2010, 2015) from a program that had been told by everyone outside the building that its best days were behind it.

“This won’t happen next year,” Tyus Jones had texted. It didn’t. What happened instead was perfection.

uncGames.story: Played in the days after Dean Smith's passing on February 7, with players and coaches from both sides joining at midcourt for a moment of silence before tipoff. Duke trailed UNC with about two and a half minutes left in regulation, but Tyus Jones got hot to send the game to overtime. Duke prevailed in the extra period. An emotional, beautifully played game in memory of the greatest coach in Tar Heel history.

UNC controlled the game at Chapel Hill, avenging the overtime loss at Cameron. Tyus Jones had a career-high 24 points, 6 rebounds, and 7 assists — becoming the first Duke freshman to record 20+ points, 5+ rebounds, and 5+ assists in consecutive games against UNC. But it wasn't enough as the Tar Heels won 84-77.

theGame.title: The Fifth Championship: Duke 68, Wisconsin 63
theGame.headline: Down nine in the second half. Okafor in foul trouble. Dead in the water. Then Grayson Allen puts the team on his back. Then Tyus Jones scores 19 second-half points. Then Okafor re-enters and scores on back-to-back possessions. Duke 68, Wisconsin 63. K's fifth ring.
theGame.story: Wisconsin built a nine-point second-half lead and appeared to have Duke on the ropes. Jahlil Okafor was in foul trouble and on the bench. The Badgers' Frank Kaminsky was dominant. Then Grayson Allen — the lowest-ranked recruit in Duke's freshman class, a kid who had barely played before Rasheed Sulaimon's dismissal — changed everything. He scored 16 points off the bench, attacking the rim with a fearlessness that shocked Wisconsin and electrified the Duke bench. K said afterward: 'We were kind of dead in the water. Grayson just put us on his back.' Tyus Jones took over from there. He scored 19 of his game-high 23 points in the second half, including 10 in the final seven minutes and two three-pointers in the final five minutes. With Duke clinging to a 59-58 lead with 3:30 remaining, Okafor re-entered the game and immediately scored on back-to-back possessions to push it to 63-58. A controversial out-of-bounds replay review went Duke's way. Jones hit another three. Wisconsin's Kaminsky finished with 21 and 12, but Duke's defense held when it mattered most. The Blue Devils allowed just 56.3 points per game across six tournament games — the defense that was supposed to collapse after Sulaimon's dismissal had become the team's identity. Jones was named Final Four Most Outstanding Player. Cook got his banner. K got his fifth ring.

accTourney.story: Duke fell to eventual champion Notre Dame in the ACC Tournament semifinals. The loss meant Duke entered March without a conference championship banner — but it also gave the team an extra day of rest and a chip on its shoulder heading into the NCAA Tournament.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke's defense set the tone immediately, suffocating Robert Morris in a 29-point rout that sent a message to the rest of the bracket.
Another dominant defensive performance. Duke held San Diego State to 49 points and advanced comfortably to the Sweet Sixteen.
The closest game of Duke's tournament run. Utah pushed the Blue Devils, but Jones hit 9-of-10 free throws and scored 15 points to hold off the Utes and advance to the Elite Eight.

Tyus Jones earned six assists with zero turnovers and scored 15 points as Duke dismantled #2 Gonzaga. The Bulldogs shot just 20% from three (2-of-10). Okafor, Winslow, and Jones combined for 38 points. Jones was named South Regional MOP. Duke reached the Final Four for the 16th time.

Michigan State jumped to a 14-6 lead before Duke erupted on a 14-2 run with Okafor scoring seven points. The Spartans missed 17 of their final 20 first-half shots and trailed 36-25 at the break. Duke's most dominant Final Four performance in decades. The expected championship opponent — undefeated 38-0 Kentucky — was upset by Wisconsin in the other semifinal.

Down nine in the second half with Okafor in foul trouble. Grayson Allen scored 16 off the bench to spark the comeback. Tyus Jones scored 19 of his 23 points in the second half, including two threes in the final five minutes. Okafor re-entered with Duke up 59-58 and scored back-to-back. Jones: Final Four MOP. K's fifth championship. Duke's fifth title. The promise fulfilled.

milestones: NATIONAL CHAMPIONS — K's 5th title, Duke's 5th overall; beat Wisconsin 68-63
Tyus Jones: Final Four MOP + South Regional MOP — first Duke player to earn both in the same tournament
K's 1,000th career victory (vs St. John's, January 25, 2015, MSG) — first D-I men's coach to reach the milestone
Dean Smith passes away February 7, 2015; Duke-UNC game played in his memory February 18 (Duke wins 92-90 OT)
Rasheed Sulaimon dismissed January 29 — 'Eight is enough'; team goes 17-1 after his departure
Duke allowed just 56.3 ppg across six NCAA Tournament games
Grayson Allen: 16 points off the bench in the championship game; K: 'Grayson just put us on his back'
Three lottery picks: Okafor (#3 Philly), Winslow (#10 Miami), Jones (#24 Cleveland)
Quinn Cook: 4,000+ career minutes; finally hangs a banner; goes undrafted, wins 2018 NBA title with Warriors
Started 14-0 (7th such start in school history); finished 35-4
Completed the Resurgence Era: two championships in six years (2010, 2015)

unc.story: Eleven days after the death of legendary UNC coach Dean Smith, both teams gathered at center court for a moment of silence before tip-off. The emotion carried into the game itself. UNC stormed back from a halftime deficit to build a 10-point lead with four minutes left, but Duke answered with a furious 9-2 run to force overtime. In the extra period, Tyus Jones and Jahlil Okafor made just enough plays to escape with a two-point victory. The win continued Duke's dominance in the rivalry, but the night belonged to the memory of Dean Smith.

Tyus Jones delivered a huge second half as Duke shot 59% after the break to complete the season sweep. Jones orchestrated the offense with precision, finding Okafor and Winslow for baskets while hitting timely jumpers of his own. Justise Winslow's athleticism overwhelmed UNC's wings on both ends. The sweep — Duke's second in three years — sent the Blue Devils into the postseason as a legitimate national title contender. They would prove it, winning it all three weeks later in Indianapolis.

seasons.accTournament: Quarterfinal loss to Notre Dame (beat NC State in second round)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #4 seed West; beat UNC Wilmington 93-85, Yale 71-64; lost to #1 Oregon 82-68 Sweet 16
seasons.tagline: The Last Plumlee, The First Superteam Freshman, and Eight Who Were Enough. The defending champions lose four starters to the NBA and only 23% of their scoring returns — the most devastating roster turnover of the K era. Then Amile Jefferson, the senior captain and the team’s most experienced player, goes down with a foot injury after nine games. Duke is left with a six-man rotation, a sophomore star in Grayson Allen who’d never been a lead player, and a lanky freshman from Kinston named Brandon Ingram who starts the year looking like he’s still in high school. Marshall Plumlee — the third Plumlee brother, a career reserve, the happy kid nobody expected much from — transforms into a leader and logs 30 minutes a night. Allen makes the leap from role player to All-American, averaging 21.6 points. Ingram becomes ACC Freshman of the Year. They win 25 games and reach the Sweet 16 with the youngest, thinnest roster K has ever coached. Oregon ends it 82-68, and K says: “They were the better team. That was pretty obvious.” But what this team accomplished with what it had was anything but obvious. The end of the Plumlee era. The dawn of the Superteam age.
seasons.overview:
The 2015-16 Duke Blue Devils were the youngest and least experienced team Mike Krzyzewski had ever coached. They lost four starters from the national championship team: Jahlil Okafor (#3 pick), Justise Winslow (#10), Tyus Jones (#24), and senior captain Quinn Cook. Only 23.2% of the previous season’s scoring and 30.5% of its minutes returned. The cupboard wasn’t bare — it was nearly empty.

What remained was a core of three: sophomore Grayson Allen, the kid who had saved the championship game off the bench; senior Amile Jefferson, the emotional leader and best interior defender who had waited four years for his moment as the face of the team; and junior Matt Jones, the quiet wing defender from DeSoto, Texas. Behind them stood Marshall Plumlee, the third Plumlee brother, a 7-0 redshirt junior who had logged fewer than 700 career minutes across four years. And arriving was a freshman class headlined by Brandon Ingram — a 6-9, 190-pound stick figure from Kinston, North Carolina whose NBA ceiling was obvious but whose college readiness was a genuine question mark — along with point guard Derryck Thornton (a reclassified junior-to-be from LA), shooting guard Luke Kennard from Ohio, and center Chase Jeter from Las Vegas.

The season’s trajectory was defined by a single injury. On December 14, after just nine games, Amile Jefferson suffered a right foot injury that ended his season. In those nine games, Jefferson had averaged 7.6 points and 8.9 rebounds — he was the team’s best rebounder, best interior defender, most experienced player, and defensive voice. His 2,032 career minutes coming into the season were roughly equal to the combined total of every other player on the roster. His loss was devastating.

What followed was one of the most remarkable survival stories in program history.

Marshall Plumlee became a different person. The player K would later say had improved more in one year than any player in his 36 years at Duke stepped into the starting center role and averaged 8.7 points, 8.4 rebounds, and 2.0 blocks in over 30 minutes per game. He led the team vocally and emotionally, dunking 60 times on the season (fourth-best single-season total in program history) and shooting .688 effective field goal percentage (third nationally). “No kid has improved more in one year in my 36 years here at Duke than Marshall,” K said. “He was as important a player as we could have, especially after Amile got hurt.” When Plumlee graduated, it marked the end of an era — Duke would be without a Plumlee on its roster for the first time since Miles’ arrival in 2008.

Grayson Allen’s transformation was even more dramatic. The sophomore went from 4.4 points per game as a freshman to 21.6 as a sophomore — the largest scoring jump in ACC history. He led the team in points, assists, and steals, becoming only the ninth Duke player to lead in all three categories. His 789 points were the seventh-highest single-season total in program history. He was named first-team All-ACC, ACC Co-Most Improved Player, and earned All-America recognition from multiple outlets. But the season was also marked by two tripping incidents that made Allen a national lightning rod — ESPN had already run an article calling him “the next hated Duke player” before the first trip.

Brandon Ingram’s freshman arc was slower but equally transformative. He started tentatively — early in the season, the scouting reports questioned whether he’d been overrated. But by February, the 6-9 wing was averaging 17.3 points and 6.8 rebounds per game, shooting with a smooth stroke that projected beautifully to the NBA. He was named ACC Freshman of the Year with 48 of 51 media votes. Allen and Ingram combined to average 38.9 points per game — the highest-scoring duo in the ACC. Luke Kennard added 12.0 points as a freshman and shot .889 from the free throw line.

The team made 328 three-pointers on the season — the program’s second-best total. Allen (90), Ingram (80), and Matt Jones (76) became the first trio in Duke history to each make 70+ threes in a season.

There were stretches of real crisis. In late January, with Jefferson gone and the team floundering, Duke lost four of five games — including home losses to Notre Dame and Syracuse. K later admitted he wasn’t sure the team would make the tournament. In mid-February, Matt Jones sprained his ankle badly and was hobbled for weeks. Then Plumlee broke his nose at the ACC Tournament. At various points, Duke played with as few as five healthy scholarship players.

The UNC rivalry split as expected. At Cameron Indoor, Duke won behind Allen’s fearless scoring. At Chapel Hill, UNC pulled away for a comfortable victory.

As a #4 seed in the West Region, Duke survived UNC Wilmington 93-85 and Yale 71-64 to reach the Sweet Sixteen. There, top-seeded Oregon — older, deeper, more physical — controlled the game from start to finish and won 82-68. K was gracious afterward: “They were the better team. That was pretty obvious tonight. They were an old, extremely well-coached team. Great athletes playing together and they knocked us back.”

The season ended at 25-11 — Duke’s second-worst record in the previous 19 seasons. But context matters. This was a team that lost its most important player nine games in, operated with a six-man rotation for months, was led by a sophomore who’d never been a primary option, and featured a starting lineup whose combined previous experience was barely one season’s worth. They won 25 games and reached the Sweet 16. K called it one of the seasons he was most proud of.

Ingram declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #2 overall by the Los Angeles Lakers. Plumlee graduated and was briefly signed by the New York Knicks before joining the U.S. Army. Thornton transferred to USC. The Superteam Era was dawning — but this first chapter belonged to a team that had almost nothing and gave almost everything.

uncGames.story: Duke pulls off the upset against #5 UNC at Cameron Indoor, part of a three-game stretch of wins over ranked opponents that included a Grayson Allen buzzer-beater against Virginia. Allen and Ingram carry the scoring load. A season-defining moment for a team fighting to prove it belongs in the NCAA Tournament.

UNC avenges the Cameron loss at Chapel Hill. The Tar Heels control the game, exploiting Duke's thin frontcourt. UNC would go on to reach the national championship game, losing to Villanova on a buzzer-beating three.

theGame.title: Marshall Plumlee's Farewell: Oregon Ends the Run
theGame.headline: Top-seeded Oregon controls the game from start to finish against a depleted Duke squad playing with seven healthy scholarship players. K says afterward: 'They were the better team. That was pretty obvious.' The end of the Plumlee era at Duke.
theGame.story: The Sweet 16 matchup against Oregon was never really in doubt. The Ducks were older, deeper, and more physical. Duke was playing with seven healthy scholarship players — a six-man core rotation plus Chase Jeter — and had been operating on fumes since December. Oregon won 82-68. Marshall Plumlee, playing his final game, embodied the team's spirit to the end. K was gracious in defeat, acknowledging Oregon's superiority while expressing immense pride in what his depleted roster had accomplished. 'I think I appreciate it more than anybody,' K said, 'because I've said it since Amile went out that we might not make the tournament. We're very proud of winning our 25th game and being a Sweet 16 team. That's a heck of a thing for this group.' With Plumlee's graduation, Duke was without a Plumlee on its roster for the first time since Miles arrived in 2008 — an eight-year dynasty of brotherly toughness that defined an era.

accTourney.story: Duke handled NC State to advance, but Marshall Plumlee broke his nose in the game — yet another injury to a team that had been battered all season.
Duke fell to Notre Dame in the ACC Tournament quarterfinals, ending conference tournament play. The team headed into Selection Sunday unsure of its seeding.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke survived a spirited UNC Wilmington effort, winning 93-85 behind Allen and Ingram's combined firepower. The Seahawks wouldn't go away, but Duke's scoring depth proved sufficient.
Duke dispatched Yale to advance to the Sweet 16 — the program's 25th trip to the regional semifinals and second-most in NCAA history at the time.

Top-seeded Oregon was too deep and too physical for Duke's depleted roster. The Ducks controlled the game throughout, winning 82-68. K: 'They were the better team. That was pretty obvious.' The end of the Plumlee era at Duke and the beginning of the roster turnover that would define the Superteam years.
