unc.story: One of the great road upsets of the K era. Grayson Allen scored 23 points and freshman Derryck Thornton came up with the clinching block on UNC's final possession — swatting Joel Berry II's shot in the lane as time expired. Brice Johnson was magnificent for UNC with 29 points and 19 rebounds, but got little help. UNC led by eight with seven minutes left and had a 6.4% Duke win probability according to KenPom, but the Tar Heels sputtered down the stretch, missing 12 of 13 three-point attempts for the game. Allen's two free throws with 1:09 left provided the winning margin.

UNC clinched the outright ACC regular-season title with the win. Grayson Allen scored a game-high 29 points, including 17 in the second half and four second-half three-pointers, but it wasn't enough. Brandon Ingram battled foul trouble and Duke couldn't overcome UNC's superior depth and size. The loss dropped Duke to the No. 5 seed in the ACC Tournament, costing them a double-bye.

seasons.accTournament: Champions (20th ACC Tournament title; first team in ACC history to win four games in four days; beat Clemson, Louisville, UNC, Notre Dame in Brooklyn)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #2 seed South; beat Troy 110-44; lost to #7 South Carolina 88-81 in Round of 32
seasons.tagline: The Unluckiest Season in Duke History. Preseason #1. Jayson Tatum arrives as the Gatorade National Player of the Year. Harry Giles — the consensus #1 recruit — arrives on two surgically repaired knees and is never himself. Marques Bolden breaks his foot before the season starts. K has back surgery in January and misses a month. Grayson Allen trips another player and is stripped of his captaincy. Amile Jefferson — the fifth-year senior who’d waited his entire career for this — gets hurt just as the team is peaking. And yet. Four wins in four days in Brooklyn: Clemson, Louisville, UNC, Notre Dame. The 20th ACC Tournament championship. The first team in conference history to do it in four days. They’ll always have Brooklyn. Then South Carolina shoots 70% in the second half of the Round of 32 and it’s over 88-81. The most talent-rich roster K had ever assembled, undone by injuries, bad luck, and one opponent who couldn’t miss. UNC wins the national championship. Tatum goes #3. The what-ifs pile up.
seasons.overview:
The 2016-17 Duke Blue Devils were preseason #1 in the AP poll and possessed the most raw talent of any roster Mike Krzyzewski had assembled to that point. Sophomore Luke Kennard returned as the team’s leading scorer from the previous season. Junior Grayson Allen was back after his All-American sophomore campaign. Senior Matt Jones provided toughness. Fifth-year senior Amile Jefferson — granted a medical hardship year after his 2015-16 foot injury — finally got the chance to be the team’s anchor. And the incoming freshman class was headlined by Jayson Tatum (the #4 overall recruit, Gatorade National Player of the Year from St. Louis), Harry Giles (the #1 overall recruit in many services, a 6-10 forward from North Carolina who’d torn his ACL twice in high school), point guard Frank Jackson, and center Marques Bolden.

The injuries started before the season did. Bolden broke his foot and was limited to 18 games. Giles’ damaged knees meant he never moved like the player who’d been ranked #1 in his class — he appeared in 26 games but averaged just 3.9 points and 3.8 rebounds, a ghost of his projected self. Then, on January 6, K underwent back surgery and was sidelined for roughly a month. Assistant Jeff Capel took over coaching duties. The team went 5-3 under Capel, including a stretch where Duke lost four of five games.

Grayson Allen’s season was defined by controversy. After two tripping incidents the previous year, Allen tripped an Elon player in December and was stripped of his captaincy by K. The incident overshadowed what was still a highly productive season: Allen averaged 14.5 points and 3.5 assists while battling three separate injuries. But the national media narrative had hardened around him, and it colored the perception of the entire team.

Through it all, the players who could play were exceptional. Luke Kennard blossomed into one of the best shooters in Duke history, averaging 19.5 points per game while shooting 43.8% from three. He was named second-team All-ACC and would have been a candidate for ACC Player of the Year in a less competitive field. Jayson Tatum, despite missing time with injuries, averaged 16.8 points, 7.3 rebounds, 2.1 assists, 1.3 steals, and 1.1 blocks per game — a complete offensive game with defensive versatility that screamed future All-Star. He earned third-team All-ACC honors. Amile Jefferson, in his fifth and final year, averaged 12.5 points and 8.4 rebounds and was the team’s emotional heartbeat — until he, too, got hurt late in the season.

Duke finished 11-7 in ACC play, tied for fifth. On paper, it looked like a disappointing season for a preseason #1 team. Then came Brooklyn.

The 2017 ACC Tournament, held at the Barclays Center in Brooklyn, New York, produced one of the great tournament runs in conference history. Duke became the first ACC team ever to win four games in four days, beating Clemson, Louisville, North Carolina, and Notre Dame in succession to claim the program’s 20th ACC Tournament championship. It was a virtuoso performance: Tatum was brilliant throughout, Kennard shot the lights out, and the team played with a desperation and togetherness that had eluded them during the regular season. The UNC win in the semifinals was particularly sweet — Duke’s veteran-heavy backcourt outplayed the Tar Heels in what amounted to a neutral-court slugfest.

As a #2 seed in the South Region, Duke opened the NCAA Tournament with a 110-44 demolition of Troy — a 66-point victory that was the most lopsided in tournament history under K. But the second round brought #7 South Carolina and one of the most painful losses in program history. The Gamecocks, normally a 41% shooting team, went supernova in the second half — shooting over 70% from the field and 80% from three-point range. A team that normally shot 69.6% from the foul line hit 21 of 23 free throws. Duke fought valiantly, but South Carolina’s 88-81 victory was the product of an opponent having the single best shooting half of its entire season against the tournament’s most talented roster. “Sometimes, you just catch a team on the wrong night,” the Duke Basketball Report wrote.

The loss was devastating because of what it prevented. This team, when healthy, had the talent to win a national championship. But it was never healthy. Three of the four impact freshmen were sidelined at various points. Allen battled injuries and controversy all season. Jefferson got hurt as the team was peaking. K missed a month. The schedule was brutally unbalanced — every top ACC opponent came on the road.

UNC, the team Duke had beaten in the ACC Tournament semifinals, went on to win the national championship — beating Gonzaga on a Marcus Paige-to-Justin Jackson sequence that gave the Tar Heels their sixth title. Duke fans were left to wonder: what if Giles had been healthy? What if Bolden hadn’t broken his foot? What if K hadn’t needed surgery?

Tatum declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #3 overall by the Boston Celtics, beginning one of the greatest careers of any Duke player since Grant Hill. Kennard went #12 to the Detroit Pistons. Giles went #20 to the Sacramento Kings. Frank Jackson went #31 to the New Orleans Pelicans. Four picks from one roster — the Superteam model was working, even when the wins didn’t follow.

Jefferson graduated. Matt Jones graduated. Allen returned for a controversial senior year. The 2017 Devils will always have Brooklyn — four wins in four days, the 20th ACC championship, and the knowledge that on the right night, with everyone on the floor, this team could beat anyone in the country. They just never got that night in March.

uncGames.story: UNC controlled the action at Chapel Hill. The Tar Heels' depth and frontcourt size proved too much for Duke's injury-depleted roster. UNC won comfortably on their home floor.

UNC won a tight game at Cameron Indoor, overcoming a strong Duke effort in the regular-season finale. The Tar Heels clinched the outright ACC regular-season championship. Duke lost both regular-season meetings to UNC for the first time since the 2008-09 season.

Revenge in Brooklyn. Duke's veteran backcourt of Kennard, Allen, and Tatum outplayed the Tar Heels in the ACC Tournament semifinals — part of Duke's historic four-wins-in-four-days run to the 20th ACC Tournament championship. Sweet payback after losing both regular-season meetings.

theGame.title: South Carolina's Eruption
theGame.location: Bon Secours Wellness Arena, Greenville, South Carolina
theGame.headline: #7-seed South Carolina shoots over 70% in the second half and 80% from three to stun #2-seed Duke 88-81. A team that normally shoots 41% has the best offensive half of its entire season against the tournament's most talented roster. The unluckiest season in Duke history ends in the most painful way possible.
theGame.story: Fresh off the euphoria of four wins in four days in Brooklyn, Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as a #2 seed with renewed confidence. After demolishing Troy 110-44 in the first round — a 66-point victory — the Blue Devils faced #7 South Carolina in Greenville, effectively a road game. The Gamecocks, a team that normally shot 41% from the field, went nuclear in the second half. They shot over 70% from the floor, 80% from three, and hit 21 of 23 free throws. Duke fought to the end — Kennard, Tatum, and Allen all scored in double figures — but the shots just kept falling for South Carolina. The final was 88-81. 'Sometimes, you just catch a team on the wrong night,' the Duke Basketball Report wrote. It was the most painful second-round loss of the K era — worse than Lehigh or Mercer because this team, when healthy, had championship-level talent. The Gamecocks advanced to the Final Four for the first time in program history. UNC went on to win the national championship.

accTourney.story: Duke opened its historic ACC Tournament run with a win over Clemson — game one of the unprecedented four-games-in-four-days marathon.
Duke dispatched Louisville in the quarterfinals — game two of four.
Revenge on the Tar Heels. After losing both regular-season meetings, Duke's backcourt outplayed UNC in a neutral-court semifinal — game three of four. Tatum and Kennard led the way.

Duke completed the unprecedented four-games-in-four-days run by beating Notre Dame for the program's 20th ACC Tournament championship. The first team in ACC history to win four tournament games in four days. They'll always have Brooklyn.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke demolished Troy by 66 points — 110-44 — the most lopsided NCAA Tournament victory under K. A dominant performance that briefly made it seem like the tournament would go Duke's way.

South Carolina shot over 70% in the second half and 80% from three in one of the most improbable shooting performances in tournament history. Duke lost 88-81 in what amounted to a road game in Greenville. The Gamecocks went on to the Final Four. UNC won the national championship. The unluckiest Duke season ended on the most unlucky night of all.

Harry Giles: consensus #1 recruit, two ACL tears, never fully healthy — averaged just 3.9/3.8 in limited action; drafted #20
Marques Bolden broke foot before season; limited to 18 games
Amile Jefferson's fifth-year senior season cut short by injury
110-44 victory over Troy — most lopsided NCAA Tournament win under K (66-point margin)
South Carolina upset in Round of 32 — Gamecocks shot 70%+ in second half; went to Final Four
Four NBA Draft picks: Tatum (#3 Celtics), Kennard (#12 Pistons), Giles (#20 Kings), Jackson (#31 Pelicans)
Jayson Tatum: future multi-time NBA All-Star, All-NBA selection; one of the greatest careers of any Duke player since Grant Hill
UNC won the national championship this season — beating Gonzaga in the title game

unc.story: Grayson Allen tied a career high with seven three-pointers and scored a game-high 25 points. Luke Kennard added 20 and Jayson Tatum erupted for 19 points in the second half after going scoreless on 0-of-3 shooting in the first. The freshman also posted a career-best five assists. Justin Jackson led UNC with 21 and Berry added 15, but the Tar Heels couldn't match Duke's perimeter barrage — the Blue Devils hit 14 three-pointers in the game.

unc.location: Dean E. Smith Center (Senior Night, Chapel Hill)
unc.story: Joel Berry II scored 28 points — making all five of his first-half three-point attempts — and Isaiah Hicks added 21 and nine rebounds on Senior Night to give UNC the regular-season title. Kennard matched Berry with 28 points for Duke, and there were 24 lead changes, but missed free throws by Allen and others down the stretch doomed the Blue Devils. Michael Jordan made a rare courtside appearance at the Smith Center. The loss cost Duke its double-bye in the ACC Tournament.

The rubber match, 500 miles from Tobacco Road, in Brooklyn. Jayson Tatum was sensational with 25 points, averaging 23 per game through three ACC Tournament contests. Kennard added 20 and Allen scored 18 off the bench. By winning two of three in the season series, fifth-seeded Duke knocked off the top-seeded Tar Heels in their first ACC Tournament meeting since the 2011 championship game. Tatum: 'It's like night and day from the one stretch when we were 1-4. We're clicking at the right time.'

seasons.accTournament: Quarterfinal exit (beat Notre Dame, lost to North Carolina in semifinal)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #2 seed Midwest — Elite Eight (lost to #1 Kansas in OT)
seasons.tagline: The Reclassification, the Senior, and the Shot That Wouldn't Fall
seasons.overview:
The 2017-18 Duke Blue Devils were built around the most audacious recruiting maneuver of the Mike Krzyzewski era — and perhaps in the history of college basketball. On August 14, 2017, just fourteen days before fall semester classes began, Marvin Bagley III — the consensus #1 recruit in the Class of 2018, the grandson of Olympic basketball player 'Jumpin' Joe Caldwell, a 6-11 left-handed phenom from Sierra Canyon in Los Angeles — announced on SportsCenter that he would reclassify to the Class of 2017 and play at Duke immediately. He pulled a blue Duke #35 jersey out of a bag on the broadcast desk, the number belonging to retired legend Danny Ferry. Vegas moved Duke's national championship odds from 7-1 to 3-1 overnight.

Bagley's reclassification was unprecedented in its timing. No #1 recruit in the modern era had committed this late — Andrew Wiggins' May 2013 Kansas commitment had been the previous record. Bagley's father, Marvin Jr., had been born and raised in Durham and played football at North Carolina A&T. The family connection to the city ran deep. Bagley cited 'the brotherhood' as the deciding factor: 'That's what Coach K preached to me and my family on my visit. I just had a good feeling there.' He graduated from Sierra Canyon on September 1 and arrived at Duke a week later, joining an already loaded freshman class of Wendell Carter Jr. (#5 national recruit, from Atlanta), Gary Trent Jr. (#1 shooting guard nationally, son of NBA veteran Gary Trent, from Duke, Minnesota), Trevon Duval (#1 point guard nationally, from IMG Academy), Marques Bolden (returning sophomore center), and Jordan Tucker.

The lone senior was Grayson Allen — returning for a fourth year after an All-American sophomore campaign, a tumultuous junior year defined by tripping incidents, and a near-departure for the NBA Draft. Allen's decision to come back gave Duke something rare for the one-and-done era: a four-year player with 90 more games of experience than any of his starting teammates. He would start alongside four freshmen, the first time Duke had done so in 35 years.

Duke opened the season ranked #1 in both polls and looked the part immediately. Bagley scored 25 points and grabbed 10 rebounds in his debut against Elon — a Duke freshman record for scoring in a first game. Two nights later, Duval had 15 points and 12 assists in a 99-69 rout of Utah Valley. Then came the Champions Classic in Chicago, the 42nd meeting of the AP's top two teams since 1949: #1 Duke vs. #2 Michigan State.

Bagley suffered an eye injury midway through the first half and didn't return. It didn't matter. Grayson Allen took over, scoring a career-high 37 points on 11-of-20 shooting (7-of-11 from three) — setting a Champions Classic record that broke former MSU player Denzel Valentine's mark. Duval added 17 points and 10 assists. Carter contributed 12 and 12. Allen's five second-half three-pointers powered a closing 15-6 run that buried the Spartans 88-81. 'I've played in 90 more games than the four teammates out there with me,' Allen said afterward. 'I feel a little more comfortable and calm and confident.'

The Blue Devils ran the nonconference table at 12-0. They won the PK80 Invitational (Phil Knight's 80th birthday celebration) in Portland, beating Portland State, Texas (85-78 in overtime, Bagley scoring 34 with a freshman-record 15 rebounds), and Florida (87-84, Bagley going for 30/15 — the first Duke freshman to post back-to-back 30-point games). Bagley's 23/10 against Indiana in the ACC-Big Ten Challenge made it four double-doubles in five games. Then, on December 30 at Cameron, Bagley erupted for 32 points and 21 rebounds against Florida State in a 100-93 win — the 21 boards setting a Duke freshman record.

Bagley was a statistical force unlike anything Durham had seen. He moved in the post with the fluidity of a wing, elevated on second jumps faster than centers six inches taller, and played with a relentless motor that belied his age. He would finish the season averaging 21.0 points and 11.1 rebounds in just 33.9 minutes — becoming only the second player in ACC history (after Jahlil Okafor in 2015) to sweep the ACC Player of the Year and Rookie of the Year awards. He earned consensus first-team All-American honors and the NABC National Freshman of the Year award.

But the season was not without turbulence. On December 2, Duke lost to St. John's 81-77 at Madison Square Garden — a team that had dropped its first 11 Big East games. Sophomore guard Shamorie Ponds torched Duke for 33 points. 'We did not play basketball the first 32 minutes worthy of our program,' K said. 'We had blank faces, we didn't talk, we were like five individuals out there. It was disgusting, really.' Jordan Tucker transferred from Duke at the end of the fall semester, further thinning the bench.

ACC play brought the season into sharper focus. Duke went 13-5, finishing second behind Virginia. The Blue Devils beat Florida State 100-93 (Bagley's 32/21 game), swept Pittsburgh and Wake Forest, and won at Miami (Gary Trent Jr. scoring a game-high 30). But the defense remained a persistent concern — Duke ranked outside the top 50 nationally in defensive efficiency, an unusual vulnerability for a K team. The losses to NC State (85-96, Bagley scoring 31 in a losing effort), Virginia (63-65 at home on a Kyle Guy buzzer-beater), and Virginia Tech (63-64 on the road) all underscored a team that could outscore almost anyone but struggled to get stops consistently.

Wendell Carter Jr. was the season's quiet revelation. The Atlanta freshman averaged 13.5 points, 9.1 rebounds, and 2.1 blocks per game while shooting 56.1% from the field. He provided interior defense that Bagley — for all his brilliance — could not, swatting 76 shots on the season. He earned second-team All-ACC and ACC All-Freshman honors. Carter and Bagley together gave Duke the most talented and productive big-man tandem in program history.

Gary Trent Jr. developed into one of the ACC's most dangerous shooters, averaging 14.5 points while hitting 40.2% from three on 241 attempts — a volume-and-accuracy combination that ranked among the best in the conference. His 30-point explosion at Miami and consistent perimeter threat made Duke's offense nearly impossible to game-plan against. Trevon Duval ran the point with fearless athleticism, averaging 10.3 points and 5.6 assists, though his 29% three-point shooting and .596 free throw percentage remained rough edges.

Allen, in his farewell season, averaged 15.5 points, 4.6 assists, and 1.7 steals per game. He shot 37% from three and 85% from the foul line, and his 1,996 career points made him the 12th-leading scorer in Duke history. He joined Jon Scheyer, Grant Hill, Danny Ferry, and Johnny Dawkins as the only Duke players with 1,900+ points, 400+ rebounds, and 400+ assists. Allen earned third-team All-ACC honors and remained the team's most clutch performer in tight games — a four-year contributor whose legacy at Duke would never be simple but was always significant.

The rivalry with North Carolina produced a classic split. At Chapel Hill on February 8, the #9 Blue Devils built a 12-point first-half lead behind their frontcourt size, seemingly in control. Then UNC opened the second half on a devastating 16-2 run. Joel Berry II, the Tar Heels' senior point guard, scored 21 points with six assists and zero turnovers — and when Duval's flashy first-half dunk fired Berry up, the senior picked him up full-court for the rest of the game. 'That's why I started picking him up full-court,' Berry said, 'letting him know that you're still a freshman and I don't care how good you are, one and done, that doesn't scare me.' Kenny Williams hit six three-pointers — the most in a Duke-UNC game since Hubert Davis in 1992. UNC committed just two turnovers, tying a program record. Theo Pinson's breakaway slam in the final seconds sealed the 82-78 win.

The rematch at Cameron on March 3 — Senior Night for Allen — was a mirror image. UNC built a 35-25 halftime lead as Duke shot 1-of-10 from three and 4-of-14 from the foul line in a miserable first twenty minutes. Bagley scored just three first-half points. Then he erupted: 18 of his 21 points came in the second half as Duke roared back. K told some freshmen before the game to treat this like their Senior Day too, and they responded. Duval had six second-half assists without a turnover. Trent drilled three three-pointers in the final ten minutes. Duke went 8-of-15 from deep in the second half and outrebounded Carolina 24-13 after halftime. The Blue Devils closed on a 17-6 run, holding UNC scoreless for the final 3:18 to win 74-64. 'Marvin went nuts there for a while and just put us on his back,' K said. Roy Williams offered the complementary view: 'Aliens crawled up in my guys' bodies in the second half.'

The ACC Tournament in Brooklyn offered a third meeting. Duke beat Notre Dame 88-70 in the quarterfinals, then fell to UNC 74-69 in the semifinals — Berry scoring 13 with six assists, UNC controlling the paint and denying Duke's zone any easy answers. The loss stung but didn't damage Duke's NCAA Tournament positioning.

As a #2 seed in the Midwest, Duke dispatched Iona 89-67 and Rhode Island 87-62 to reach the Sweet 16, with Bagley scoring exactly 22 points in each game — a metronome of consistency. Against #11 Syracuse in the regional semifinal, Duke survived a scare, winning 69-65 as the Blue Devils' zone-versus-zone matchup produced an ugly, grinding affair. It was the program's 21st Elite Eight — the 15th under K.

The Elite Eight brought #1 seed Kansas, and with it one of the great NCAA Tournament games of the decade. The matchup featured 18 lead changes and 11 ties across 45 minutes of basketball at its best. Neither team ever led by more than seven. Duke started four freshmen against a Kansas squad led by senior Devonte' Graham and the red-hot Malik Newman, a Mississippi State transfer who'd found his game in March.

Kansas doubled Bagley on the catch throughout the first half, holding him to five points on five shots. Duke adjusted, and Bagley finished with 16 points and 10 rebounds. Duval was brilliant — 20 points, his second-highest scoring game of the season. Trent added 17. Five Blue Devils scored in double figures.

But Kansas owned the glass, outrebounding Duke 47-32 — a staggering margin against the nation's best offensive rebounding team. Svi Mykhailiuk played the game of his life, posting 11 points, 10 rebounds, and five assists while helping defend Bagley. And Newman was transcendent. After an inconsistent regular season averaging 12 points on 43.8% shooting, Newman erupted for a career-high 32 points.

The game's final minutes became legend. Allen hit four consecutive free throws to put Duke up 72-69 with under a minute to play. Newman missed. Bagley rebounded. Carter missed with 36 seconds left. Then the 6-8 freshman Silvio De Sousa grabbed the rebound, and the ball found Mykhailiuk at the top of the key with 26 seconds left. His three-pointer — after he'd missed two open threes moments before — knotted the score at 72.

Duke had the final possession. Allen dribbled upcourt for the last shot of regulation. He spun left, spun right, rose near the left side of the lane, and released a floater that touched every inch of the rim. It rolled in. It rolled out. It kissed the back of the cylinder. It spun away. The horn sounded. Overtime.

In the extra period, Carter fouled out — Duke's best defender gone. Newman scored all 13 of Kansas's overtime points, including a devastating corner three with 1:49 left that made it 81-78. When Duval missed a wide-open corner three with 37 seconds remaining and Duke down three, the season was over. Kansas 85, Duke 81.

'It's way different than a regular loss,' Allen said in the silent locker room, 'because with a loss, you know you've got something else. There's two parts in me, one still fighting and the other, Sorry, it's done. That's why it's really hard to grasp.'

Four Blue Devils were selected in the 2018 NBA Draft, tying a Duke single-year record: Bagley went #2 overall to Sacramento (behind former high school teammate Deandre Ayton), Carter went #7 to Chicago, Allen was taken #21 by Utah (traded to Atlanta), and Trent was chosen #37 by Portland. Duval, despite being the #1 point guard recruit, went undrafted — his shooting limitations proved too much to overlook. The entire starting five went pro.

The season will always be remembered for what it almost was. Bagley was a generational talent whose 21.0/11.1 averages were among the best freshman seasons in ACC history. Allen capped one of the most complicated and compelling careers in Duke history. The team averaged 84.4 points per game (ninth nationally), shot 49.2% from the field (16th nationally), and grabbed 41.1 rebounds per game (seventh nationally). They were six points and a fickle basketball from the Final Four.

But the shot that wouldn't fall — Allen's regulation floater that touched every surface of the iron before spinning away — is the image that endures. Like Austin Rivers' buzzer-beater that did go in, like Christian Laettner's turn-and-shoot that changed everything, the cruelty of March is measured in millimeters. Allen's shot was a millimeter from legend. Instead, it was the last image of a tumultuous, brilliant, ultimately heartbreaking four-year career — and a fitting capstone for a season that had everything except the ending it deserved.

rosterHighlights.name: Marvin Bagley III
rosterHighlights.notes: Reclassified from Class of 2018; consensus #1 overall recruit; ACC Player of the Year AND Rookie of the Year (2nd player ever to sweep both, after Okafor); consensus first-team All-American; NABC Freshman of the Year; 32/21 vs Florida State (freshman record 21 rebounds); wore Danny Ferry's retired #35; drafted #2 overall by Sacramento Kings

rosterHighlights.name: Grayson Allen
rosterHighlights.notes: 12th all-time leading scorer in Duke history (1,996 pts); career-high 37 pts vs #2 Michigan State in Champions Classic (7-11 from three); joined Scheyer/Hill/Ferry/Dawkins with 1,900+ pts, 400+ reb, 400+ ast; third-team All-ACC; regulation floater vs Kansas in Elite Eight rattled in and out; drafted #21 by Utah Jazz

rosterHighlights.name: Wendell Carter Jr.
rosterHighlights.notes: Second-team All-ACC; ACC All-Freshman Team; 76 blocks (team leader); 27 pts and 7 reb vs Evansville; fouled out in OT vs Kansas in Elite Eight; drafted #7 overall by Chicago Bulls

rosterHighlights.name: Gary Trent Jr.
rosterHighlights.notes: One of ACC's most dangerous perimeter shooters; game-high 30 pts at Miami; three key threes in 2nd-half comeback vs UNC at Cameron; son of NBA veteran Gary Trent; drafted #37 by Portland Trail Blazers

rosterHighlights.name: Trevon Duval
rosterHighlights.notes: #1 point guard recruit nationally; 17 pts/10 ast in Champions Classic vs Michigan State; 20 pts in Elite Eight vs Kansas; 12-assist game vs Utah Valley; electric athleticism but shooting limitations kept him from being drafted

rosterHighlights.name: Marques Bolden
rosterHighlights.notes: Backup center who provided rim protection behind Carter and Bagley; key contributor in nonconference play; would stay through junior year before declaring for 2019 draft

rosterHighlights.name: Javin DeLaurier
rosterHighlights.notes: Energy big man off the bench; breakaway dunk sealed the Champions Classic win vs Michigan State; 11 rebounds in Duke debut vs Elon; would become a four-year contributor

rosterHighlights.name: Jack White
rosterHighlights.notes: Australian freshman wing; provided defensive versatility and toughness off the bench; would develop into a starting role by junior year

rosterHighlights.name: Alex O'Connell
rosterHighlights.notes: Sharpshooting guard from Charlottesville, VA; key corner three vs UNC at Cameron; would become a rotation player in later years

uncGames.story: Duke built a 12-point first-half lead behind dominant frontcourt play from Bagley and Carter, seemingly in total control at Chapel Hill. Then UNC opened the second half on a devastating 16-2 run that flipped the game entirely. Joel Berry II scored 21 points with six assists and zero turnovers, picking Trevon Duval up full-court after Duval's flashy first-half dunk — 'letting him know that you're still a freshman and I don't care how good you are, one and done, that doesn't scare me.' Kenny Williams hit six three-pointers, the most in a Duke-UNC game since Hubert Davis in 1992. UNC committed just two turnovers, tying a program record, and grabbed 15 offensive rebounds in the second half. Theo Pinson's breakaway slam in the final seconds sealed it. Duke had now lost back-to-back games (after the St. John's upset) for the first time all season.

Senior Night for Grayson Allen — and what K privately told some freshmen would be their Senior Day too. Duke came out flat: 1-of-10 from three, 4-of-14 from the foul line, and just three assists in a dreadful first half. Bagley scored three points. UNC led 35-25 at the break. Then Bagley erupted — 18 of his 21 points in the second half, going 9-of-14 from the field. Duval had six second-half assists without a turnover. Gary Trent Jr. drilled three three-pointers in the final ten minutes. Duke went 8-of-15 from deep in the second half and outrebounded Carolina 24-13, closing on a 17-6 run and holding UNC scoreless for the final 3:18. Roy Williams: 'Aliens crawled up in my guys' bodies in the second half.' Berry shot 0-of-7 from three and finished with just six points. Allen had 15 in his Cameron farewell.

The rubber match in Brooklyn. UNC controlled the paint and denied Duke's zone any easy answers. Berry scored 13 with six assists, Luke Maye was steady, and the Tar Heels won 74-69 to advance to the ACC Tournament final. Duke's season-long defensive issues resurfaced against a Carolina team playing with senior confidence. The loss ended Duke's ACC Tournament run but didn't damage their NCAA Tournament seeding.

theGame.title: The Shot That Wouldn't Fall: Allen's Last Dance in Omaha
theGame.location: CenturyLink Center (Omaha, NE) — NCAA Midwest Regional Final
theGame.headline: Allen's regulation floater touches every inch of iron, spins away; Newman scores all 13 of Kansas's OT points as Jayhawks end Duke's season
theGame.story: This was college basketball at its best — two blue bloods trading blows for 45 minutes in what was arguably the greatest game of the 2018 NCAA Tournament. Eighteen lead changes. Eleven ties. Neither team ever led by more than seven points. Kansas doubled Bagley on the catch throughout the first half, holding him to five points on five shots. Duke adjusted, and Bagley finished with 16 points and 10 rebounds. Duval was a revelation with 20 points. Trent added 17. Five Blue Devils scored in double figures. But Kansas owned the glass, outrebounding Duke 47-32 — a staggering margin against the nation's best offensive rebounding team. Svi Mykhailiuk posted 11/10/5 while helping defend Bagley. And Malik Newman — a Mississippi State transfer who'd averaged 12 points in an inconsistent regular season — erupted for a career-high 32. The final minutes became instant legend. Allen hit four consecutive free throws to put Duke up 72-69 with under a minute left. Newman missed. Bagley rebounded. Carter missed with 36 seconds left. De Sousa grabbed the board. Mykhailiuk — who'd just missed two open threes — drilled a three from the top of the key with 25.7 seconds left to tie it at 72. Duke had the final possession. Allen dribbled upcourt, spun each way near the left side of the lane, and released a floater that kissed seemingly every inch of the cylinder — rolling in, rolling out, touching the back of the iron, spinning away. The horn sounded. In overtime, Carter fouled out — Duke's best defender gone. Newman scored all 13 of Kansas's points, including a devastating corner three with 1:49 remaining. Duval missed a wide-open corner three with 37 seconds left. Kansas 85, Duke 81. 'It's way different than a regular loss,' Allen said in the silent locker room, 'because with a loss, you know you've got something else. There's two parts in me, one still fighting and the other, Sorry, it's done.'

accTourney.story: Duke handled Notre Dame 88-70 in the ACC Tournament quarterfinals at the Barclays Center. A balanced effort with no real drama — exactly what Duke needed after the emotional Senior Night win over UNC five days earlier.

The rubber match with UNC. North Carolina controlled the paint, Berry led with 13 and six assists, and Duke's defensive vulnerabilities resurfaced. The 74-69 loss ended Duke's ACC Tournament run but preserved their NCAA positioning as a #2 seed.

ncaaTourney.story: Bagley posted 22 points and 7 rebounds as Duke pulled away from Iona after a competitive first half. The Blue Devils' talent advantage was too much for the MAAC champions.

Another 22-point, 9-rebound performance from Bagley as Duke rolled past the #7 seed. The Blue Devils looked like the most dangerous team in the Midwest bracket, with their big-man duo dominating inside.

An ugly, grinding zone-vs-zone affair. Bagley scored 22 for the third straight tournament game and Duke survived the Cinderella Orange, 69-65. It was the program's 21st Elite Eight — the 15th under K.

An instant classic. 18 lead changes, 11 ties, 45 minutes of the best basketball the 2018 tournament produced. Malik Newman scored all 13 of Kansas's overtime points (32 total). Allen's regulation floater rattled every surface of the rim before spinning away. Carter fouled out in OT. Duval missed an open corner three with 37 seconds left. Kansas to the Final Four; Duke's season ended one game short.

milestones: Marvin Bagley III reclassifies from Class of 2018 to 2017 on August 14, committing to Duke on SportsCenter — the latest-ever commitment by a consensus #1 recruit in the modern era
Duke opens season ranked #1 with four freshmen in the starting lineup for the first time in 35 years
Allen scores career-high 37 points (7-11 from three) to beat #2 Michigan State 88-81 in Champions Classic — setting the event's scoring record
Bagley records 32 points and 21 rebounds (freshman record) vs Florida State on December 30
Entire starting five goes pro after the season — all five leave for NBA/professional basketball
Four players selected in 2018 NBA Draft: Bagley (#2 SAC), Carter (#7 CHI), Allen (#21 UTA), Trent (#37 POR) — tying Duke single-year record
Team averaged 84.4 ppg (9th nationally), 49.2% FG (16th), 41.1 rpg (7th nationally)
Grayson Allen finishes career as Duke's 12th all-time leading scorer with 1,996 points
Allen's regulation buzzer-beater attempt vs Kansas in Elite Eight rattles around the rim and spins out — one of the most agonizing near-misses in Duke tournament history
Bagley becomes second player in ACC history to sweep Player of the Year and Rookie of the Year (after Okafor, 2015)

unc.story: Duke built a 12-point first-half lead behind its touted freshman frontcourt, but UNC roared out of halftime with an aggressive edge that had been missing for weeks. Joel Berry II scored 21 points and Cameron Johnson posted an 18-point, 13-rebound double-double. Marvin Bagley III had 15 points and 16 rebounds but Duke committed eight turnovers and couldn't match UNC's second-half energy. The Tar Heels outrebounded Duke 44-38 and held their composure down the stretch after emerging from a three-game losing streak.

Grayson Allen's Senior Night was a microcosm of his Duke career — emotional, turbulent, and ultimately triumphant. Allen admitted he cried three or four times before the game and couldn't sleep. Duke trailed 42-29 early in the second half after shooting 1-of-10 from three and 4-of-14 from the line in a miserable first twenty minutes. Then Trevon Duval buried an open three — 'That's a shot I'm confident in,' he said — and everything changed. Duke went 8-of-15 from deep in the second half. Bagley erupted for 18 of his 21 points after halftime with 15 rebounds. Allen finished with 15 points. K told his freshmen to treat it as their Senior Night too. They listened.

seasons.accTournament: Champions (21st title — beat Syracuse, North Carolina, Florida State; Zion Williamson named MVP)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #1 overall seed (East) — Elite Eight (lost to #2 Michigan State 67-68)
seasons.tagline: The Shoe, the Phenom, and the One That Got Away
seasons.overview:
The 2018-19 Duke Blue Devils were the most famous college basketball team of the 21st century. Not necessarily the best — that debate will rage forever — but the most watched, most discussed, most culturally significant. And it was all because of one player: Zion Lateef Williamson, a 6-7, 285-pound force of nature from Spartanburg, South Carolina, who played basketball like no human being who had ever lived.

The recruiting class was staggering even by Duke standards. Williamson, the consensus #1 recruit in the country, committed alongside RJ Barrett (#2 nationally, a Canadian from Mississauga, Ontario who'd played at Montverde Academy), Cam Reddish (#3 nationally, a silky 6-8 wing from Norristown, Pennsylvania via Westtown School), and Tre Jones (#4 point guard nationally, from Apple Valley, Minnesota — the younger brother of former Duke star and NBA lottery pick Tyus Jones). Four top-10 recruits in one class. ESPN's recruiting analysts called it the greatest freshman class in college basketball history. CBS dedicated a camera to follow Zion's every move throughout the NCAA Tournament.

The returning core included sophomore Marques Bolden, junior Javin DeLaurier, junior Jack White (the Australian), and sophomore Alex O'Connell. Jordan Goldwire and Antonio Vrankovic would play crucial bench roles. There was no senior in the regular rotation — this was a team built almost entirely around freshmen, with juniors providing the glue.

Duke opened the season on November 6 at the Champions Classic in Indianapolis and announced itself with one of the most jaw-dropping performances in the event's history. Barrett scored 33 points, Williamson added 28, and Reddish chipped in 22 as the #4 Blue Devils annihilated #2 Kentucky 118-84 — a 34-point demolition of a John Calipari roster loaded with its own five-star freshmen. Duke scored 59 points in each half. The three freshmen combined for 83 of Duke's 118 points. It was the most dominant opening-night display in modern college basketball history.

The Blue Devils rose to #1 in the AP poll and largely stayed there. They lost at Gonzaga 89-87 on November 21 — a game where Rui Hachimura outdueled Williamson in a battle of future lottery picks — but won 21 of their first 23 games. Williamson was a nightly SportsCenter feature: windmill dunks that bent the rim, chase-down blocks that defied physics, and a smile that made him impossible not to root for. He averaged 22.6 points and 8.9 rebounds while shooting 68% from the field — a shooting percentage so absurd for a player with his usage rate that it ranked among the highest in NCAA history. Barrett matched him at 22.6 points per game while adding 7.6 rebounds and 4.3 assists, making them the first freshman teammates in NCAA history to each average 20+ points in the same season.

Then came February 20, 2019 — the night that transcended basketball and became a global news event.

Duke vs. North Carolina at Cameron Indoor Stadium. #1 vs. #8. Tickets went for as much as $3,000. The crowd included former President Barack Obama (courtside), director Spike Lee, Hall of Fame outfielder Ken Griffey Jr., and recently retired NFL star Julius Peppers. Every eye in the sports world was on Williamson.

Thirty-six seconds into the game, Williamson planted his left foot near the free-throw line and began to drive. His Nike PG 2.5 sneaker — Paul George's signature shoe in a Duke-exclusive colorway — exploded. The blue rubber sole ripped completely from the white upper, from heel to toe, and Williamson's foot burst through the gap. He crumpled to the floor, clutching his right knee. Obama pointed from his courtside seat and said what everyone was thinking: 'His shoe broke.'

Williamson walked off under his own power but did not return. Duke, looking lost without its supernova, was routed 88-72. Luke Maye scored 30 points and grabbed 15 rebounds for UNC. Barrett scored 33 and Reddish 27 for Duke, but the rest of the team combined for just 12 points. Nike's stock dropped $1.1 billion in market value the next morning. LeBron James, Donovan Mitchell, and dozens of current and former players tweeted their concern. The debate over whether Williamson should 'shut it down' and protect his NBA future consumed sports media for weeks.

Williamson's answer, delivered to walk-on teammate Mike Buckmire weeks before the injury, became the defining quote of the season. When asked whether he'd sit out, Zion turned to Buckmire: 'Buck, would you hate me if I shut down the season?' Before Buckmire could answer, Williamson continued: 'I couldn't do that to my teammates. I love college too much to stop playing.'

He missed the next five-and-a-half games. Duke went 3-3 without him, including a 79-70 loss at Chapel Hill in which UNC completed the regular-season sweep. The Blue Devils' defense and rebounding cratered without Williamson's presence. The national championship favorite suddenly looked vulnerable.

Williamson returned for the ACC Tournament in Charlotte and produced one of the great postseason performances in conference history. In the quarterfinal against Syracuse, he went 13-of-13 from the field — setting both the Duke and ACC Tournament records, and tying the overall ACC record, for single-game field goal accuracy — with 29 points, 14 rebounds, and five assists. It was the most dominant 40 minutes anyone could remember from any player in the tournament's 66-year history.

The semifinal brought North Carolina. UNC led by 13 in the first half, and it looked like a sweep was inevitable. Then Williamson took over. He scored 12 of Duke's final 24 first-half points to tie the game at 44. In the second half, the teams traded blows, neither leading by more than a few possessions. With 48 seconds left, UNC's Nassir Little dunked to put Carolina up 73-72. With 30.3 seconds remaining, Williamson missed a shot near the rim — then elevated above everyone for a tip-in that put Duke ahead 74-73. Cam Johnson missed a stepback three for the lead. Barrett missed two free throws with 12.4 seconds left. Coby White's contested runner at the buzzer rattled out. Duke 74, North Carolina 73. Williamson: 31 points, 11 rebounds. The rivalry game he'd been denied all season.

Duke beat Florida State 73-63 in the championship game for the program's 21st ACC Tournament title. Williamson was named tournament MVP — the first freshman in ACC history to win both ACC Player of the Year and ACC Tournament MVP in the same season. Over three tournament games, he averaged 27.0 points on 77% shooting and 10.0 rebounds.

As the tournament's #1 overall seed, Duke opened the NCAA Tournament with an 85-62 win over North Dakota State, Williamson scoring 25 in his tournament debut. Then came #9 UCF — and the closest Duke came to elimination before the Elite Eight. Aubrey Dawkins scored 32 for the Knights and UCF led late, but B.J. Taylor's wide-open three-pointer at the buzzer rimmed out, and Duke survived 77-76. Against #4 Virginia Tech in the Sweet 16, Zion scored 23 and Duke held on 75-73 when Ahmed Hill's potential game-tying shot in the final seconds missed. Three games, three nail-biters, three escapes.

The Elite Eight brought #2 Michigan State at Capital One Arena in Washington, D.C. Tom Izzo's veteran-laden Spartans had the experience and cohesion that Duke's freshmen could not match. Cassius Winston was magnificent — 20 points and 10 assists with just one turnover. Xavier Tillman frustrated Williamson early, and Duke trailed by as many as nine in the first half. Williamson was held to seven points in the first 20 minutes.

He erupted in the second half — 17 of his 24 points came after halftime — and Duke took a 66-65 lead. But in the final six minutes, as the Blue Devils needed their best player most, Barrett took control of the offense and Williamson stood in the corner or the post, waiting for entry passes that never came. Kenny Goins — a fifth-year walk-on senior who had missed his first four three-point attempts — drained the go-ahead three with 34.3 seconds left to put Michigan State up 68-66.

Barrett missed a contested three-pointer. The rebound bounced out of bounds off Michigan State, giving Duke one more chance. Barrett drove and was fouled with 5.2 seconds left. He made the first free throw: 68-67. He tried to intentionally miss the second to set up a tip-in. Instead, it went in: 68-67, still. Winston dribbled out the clock.

'We're very upset, obviously,' Williamson said, 'but congrats to Michigan State. Just looking around the locker room and seeing your teammates, your brothers, and you just think this group will probably never play together again.'

He was right. Three Duke freshmen were selected in the top three of the 2019 NBA Draft: Williamson went #1 overall to the New Orleans Pelicans, Barrett went #3 to the New York Knicks, and Reddish went #10 to the Atlanta Hawks. Tre Jones returned for his sophomore year — the only member of the core four to come back.

The numbers were historic. The freshman class scored 2,432 combined points — the most by any freshman class in Duke history. Williamson and Barrett became the first freshman teammates to both earn consensus first-team All-American honors. Williamson won every major National Player of the Year award (AP, Wooden, Naismith, NABC, Oscar Robertson, Sporting News, Basketball Times). Barrett won the USA Today award. Jones set the overall Duke single-season record with a 3.62 assist-to-turnover ratio. The team averaged 84.4 points per game.

But the season ended one win short. For the second consecutive year, Duke lost in the Elite Eight — this time by a single point. Virginia, the team Duke had beaten twice, went on to win the national championship. The what-ifs multiplied: What if Williamson hadn't been injured for six games? What if Barrett had made that first free throw? What if the ball had found Zion in the final minutes instead of Barrett?

The 2018-19 Blue Devils were a cultural phenomenon more than a basketball team. Zion's shoe explosion became the most-discussed moment in college sports that year. His dunks went viral on a weekly basis. His decision to return after the knee sprain — 'I love college too much to stop playing' — became the rallying cry for everyone who believed the college experience still mattered. And his tip-in to beat UNC with 30 seconds left in the ACC Tournament, in the only full rivalry game he ever got to play, remains one of the singular moments in the Duke-Carolina rivalry's storied history.

They didn't win the championship. They probably should have. But they gave us a season that no one who watched it will ever forget.

uncGames.story: The most famous shoe malfunction in sports history. Thirty-six seconds in, Zion Williamson planted his left foot near the free-throw line and his Nike PG 2.5 exploded — the sole ripping completely from the upper, his foot bursting through the gap. He crumpled to the floor with a right knee sprain as Barack Obama pointed from his courtside seat: 'His shoe broke.' Williamson did not return. Duke, visibly stunned, was routed. UNC scored 32 of its first 34 points in the paint, exploiting the Zion-sized hole in the lane. Luke Maye had 30 points and 15 rebounds; Cam Johnson added 26. Barrett (33) and Reddish (27) scored 60 combined for Duke, but the rest of the team managed just 12 points. Nike's stock dropped $1.1 billion in market value the next morning. The debate over whether Williamson should protect his NBA future by sitting out consumed sports media for weeks. His answer: 'I couldn't do that to my teammates. I love college too much to stop playing.'

With Williamson still sidelined by the knee sprain, Duke visited Chapel Hill and lost 79-70. UNC completed the regular-season sweep — both victories coming without Zion in the lineup. Coby White and Cameron Johnson led the Tar Heels. Duke went 3-3 in the games Williamson missed, and the consensus was clear: this team's championship ceiling required its best player healthy.

The game Zion had been denied all season — his first full rivalry game against North Carolina. A record crowd of 20,116 packed the Spectrum Center. UNC led by 13 in the first half behind Cam Johnson's hot shooting (14 of UNC's first 22 points). Then Williamson took over, scoring 12 of Duke's final 24 first-half points to tie it at 44. In the second half, the teams traded blows. Nassir Little's dunk put UNC up 73-72 with 48 seconds left. With 30.3 seconds remaining, Williamson missed near the rim — then elevated above everyone for a tip-in to make it 74-73. Johnson missed a stepback three. Barrett missed two free throws with 12.4 seconds left. Coby White's contested runner at the buzzer rattled out. Zion: 31 points, 11 rebounds. The rivalry game that justified his decision to keep playing.

theGame.title: The Goins Three: Michigan State Ends the Zion Era in Washington
theGame.location: Capital One Arena (Washington, D.C.) — NCAA East Regional Final
theGame.headline: Kenny Goins' go-ahead three with 34.3 seconds left ends Zion Williamson's college career as Barrett's missed free throw seals Duke's fate
theGame.story: The tournament's #1 overall seed vs. Tom Izzo's battle-tested Spartans. Cassius Winston was magnificent — 20 points, 10 assists, one turnover — and Xavier Tillman frustrated Williamson early, holding him to seven first-half points as Michigan State built a nine-point lead. Williamson erupted in the second half (17 of his 24 points), and Duke took a 66-65 lead. But in the final six minutes, Williamson stood in the corner or the post, waiting for entry passes that never came, as Barrett took control of the offense. Kenny Goins — a fifth-year walk-on senior who had missed his first four three-point attempts — drained the go-ahead three with 34.3 seconds left: 68-66. Barrett missed a three. The rebound went out of bounds off Michigan State, giving Duke life. Barrett drove and was fouled with 5.2 seconds left. He made the first free throw: 68-67. He tried to intentionally miss the second, but it went in. Winston dribbled out the clock. Williamson finished with 24 points, 14 rebounds, three blocks, and three steals. 'Just looking around the locker room and seeing your teammates, your brothers,' he said, 'and you just think this group will probably never play together again.' Izzo beat K for just the second time in 13 meetings.

accTourney.story: Zion Williamson's triumphant return from the knee sprain that had sidelined him for nearly six full games. He went 13-of-13 from the field — setting the Duke and ACC Tournament record, and tying the overall ACC record, for single-game field goal accuracy — with 29 points, 14 rebounds, and five assists. The most dominant individual performance in the tournament's 66-year history.

Williamson's first full game against UNC. Down 13, he scored 12 of Duke's final 24 first-half points to tie it. His tip-in with 30.3 seconds left — putting back his own miss — proved to be the game-winner. Zion: 31/11. Record crowd of 20,116 at Spectrum Center.

Duke claimed its 21st ACC Tournament championship. Williamson led with 21 points, Tre Jones added 18 and six assists, and Barrett had 17/9/4 steals. Williamson was named tournament MVP — first freshman in ACC history to win both regular-season Player of the Year and tournament MVP.

ncaaTourney.story: Williamson's NCAA Tournament debut: 25 points in an explosive second half after Duke led just 31-27 at the break. Barrett added 26 and 14 rebounds. CBS had dedicated a camera to Williamson's every move.

The closest Duke came to elimination before the Elite Eight. Aubrey Dawkins scored 32 for UCF and the Knights led late. B.J. Taylor's wide-open three-pointer at the buzzer rimmed out — a millimeter from ending Duke's season. The narrowest of escapes.

Another nail-biter. Williamson scored 23 and Duke held on when Ahmed Hill's potential game-tying shot in the final seconds missed. Cam Reddish missed the game with a left knee injury. Three straight tournament games decided by a combined five points.

Cassius Winston: 20 points, 10 assists, one turnover. Kenny Goins: go-ahead three with 34.3 seconds left. Barrett: missed the first of two free throws with 5.2 seconds left. Williamson: 24/14/3 blocks/3 steals, but underutilized in the final minutes. The Zion era ended one point short of the Final Four.

milestones: Duke opens with a 118-84 demolition of #2 Kentucky in the Champions Classic — Barrett (33), Williamson (28), and Reddish (22) combine for 83 points in the most dominant opening-night performance in modern college basketball history

Zion Williamson's Nike PG 2.5 shoe explodes 36 seconds into the #1 Duke vs. #8 UNC game at Cameron (February 20), spraining his knee and causing Nike's stock to drop $1.1 billion; Barack Obama, Spike Lee, and Ken Griffey Jr. are in attendance
Williamson goes 13-of-13 from the field vs. Syracuse in ACC Tournament quarterfinal — setting Duke and ACC Tournament records for single-game field goal accuracy
Williamson's tip-in with 30.3 seconds left beats UNC 74-73 in ACC Tournament semifinal — his only full rivalry game against North Carolina
Duke wins 21st ACC Tournament championship; Williamson becomes first freshman in ACC history to win both regular-season Player of the Year and tournament MVP
Williamson and Barrett become first freshman teammates in NCAA history to both earn consensus first-team All-American honors
Williamson and Barrett co-lead ACC in scoring at 22.6 ppg — first freshman teammates in NCAA history to each average 20+ points
Freshman class scores 2,432 combined points — highest-scoring freshman class in Duke history
Three top-10 picks in 2019 NBA Draft: Williamson (#1 NOP), Barrett (#3 NYK), Reddish (#10 ATL)
CBS dedicates a camera exclusively to Zion Williamson throughout the NCAA Tournament
Duke survives three consecutive single-possession tournament games (UCF by 1, Virginia Tech by 2, then loses to Michigan State by 1)
Williamson named AP, Wooden, Naismith, NABC, Oscar Robertson, Sporting News, and Basketball Times National Player of the Year — sweeping every major award

unc.story: The Shoe Game. Thirty-six seconds in, Zion Williamson planted his left foot and his Nike PG 2.5 exploded — the sole ripping away from the upper as his foot burst through the side. He fell awkwardly, grabbing his right knee. Barack Obama was courtside. Spike Lee was there. The Cameron crowd went silent. Zion walked off under his own power but did not return. Duke, looking lost without its supernova, was routed. Luke Maye scored 30 points and 15 rebounds for UNC. RJ Barrett scored 33 in a losing cause. Nike's stock dropped $1.1 billion the next trading day.

UNC completed the regular-season sweep as Duke continued to struggle without Zion, who had missed five and a half games. The Blue Devils went 3-3 without their supernova. Duke's defense and rebounding cratered without Williamson's rim protection and second-chance athleticism. Cameron Johnson and Nassir Little powered UNC's attack. The loss dropped Duke to the No. 3 seed in the ACC Tournament.

Zion's revenge. UNC led by 13 in the first half and a sweep looked inevitable. Then Williamson took over, scoring 12 of Duke's final 24 first-half points to tie it. In the second half, he was unstoppable — finishing with 31 points on 13-of-16 shooting. With under a minute left and Duke clinging to a one-point lead, Williamson tipped in a missed RJ Barrett shot to make it 74-71. UNC got a layup but couldn't complete the comeback. Zion was named ACC Tournament MVP — the first freshman in conference history to win both ACC Player of the Year and Tournament MVP.

seasons.tagline: The Streak, the Steal, and the Season That March Forgot
seasons.overview:
The 2019-20 Duke Blue Devils were built around the most important returning player in the program's recent history: Tre Jones, who came back for his sophomore year when every other member of the Zion-era core four departed for the NBA. Jones' decision to stay gave Duke something it hadn't had since Quinn Cook's senior year — a point guard who'd already played at the highest level of college basketball, who understood the system, and who wanted to lead.

The freshman class featured Vernon Carey Jr. (#5 nationally, a 6-10, 270-pound center from Fort Lauderdale who'd played at University School in nearby Plantation, Florida), Cassius Stanley (#24 nationally, an explosive 6-6 wing from Sierra Canyon — Marvin Bagley's alma mater — who had once been teammates with LeBron James' son Bronny), Matthew Hurt (#10 nationally, a 6-9 stretch-four from Rochester, Minnesota, and a McDonald's All-American), and Wendell Moore Jr. (#21 nationally, a 6-5 wing from Cox Mill High School in Concord, North Carolina). The returning core included juniors Jack White and Javin DeLaurier (both named captains), sophomore Alex O'Connell, and sophomore Joey Baker. Senior Justin Robinson, a walk-on who had spent his career as a practice player and locker-room glue guy, was also on the roster.

Duke opened the season ranked #4 and started 6-0, including a convincing 68-66 Champions Classic win over #3 Kansas on November 5 — a statement that this post-Zion team could still compete with the sport's elite. Jones was magnificent, Carey announced himself with 20 points and 11 rebounds, and the Blue Devils climbed to #1 in the AP poll for the second consecutive season.

Then came November 26, 2019 — the night the streaks ended.

Stephen F. Austin, from the Southland Conference, arrived at Cameron Indoor Stadium as 27.5-point underdogs. The Lumberjacks were 4-1 and had lost by 12 at Rutgers. Their coach, Kyle Keller, told his players before the game: 'Banners can't beat us tonight. The players have to beat us.' What followed was one of the most stunning upsets in the history of college basketball.

SFA's relentless full-court pressure generated 22 Duke turnovers. They scored 64 of their 85 points in the paint — more than Duke allowed to Kansas in the Champions Classic. Kevon Harris scored 26 points on 11-of-16 shooting. The game went to overtime — the first overtime in the 150-game nonconference home winning streak — and Duke scored just one basket in the extra period while committing six turnovers. With seconds left, Tre Jones missed a jumper, Wendell Moore rebounded, and Jones' bounce pass to Matthew Hurt was broken up. Nathan Bain — a fifth-year senior from the Bahamas whose family had lost nearly everything in Hurricane Dorian that summer — picked up the loose ball and raced the length of the floor for a buzzer-beating layup. SFA 85, Duke 83.

The 150-game nonconference home winning streak was over. It had lasted nearly 20 years — since a loss to St. John's on February 26, 2000, when LeBron James was a high school freshman, Tiger Woods had won only two majors, and Tom Brady was about to be drafted in the sixth round. Duke was favored by 27.5 points, making it the largest upset by point spread in Division I basketball in at least 15 years. 'If we would have won, we would not have been deserving of winning,' K said afterward.

The Blue Devils regrouped. They won their first eight ACC games, climbing back into the top 5. Jones was emerging as the best point guard in the conference — his shooting had improved dramatically, from 26.2% from three as a freshman to 36.1% as a sophomore, and his scoring jumped from 9.4 to 16.2 points per game. Vernon Carey Jr. dominated inside, averaging 17.8 points and 8.8 rebounds while shooting 57.7% from the field, racking up 15 double-doubles that ranked second nationally among freshmen.

But road losses continued to plague this team. They fell at Clemson 79-72 on January 14 — their first road loss since the previous season. A loss at NC State followed. Then Louisville beat them at the KFC Yum! Center. Then Wake Forest stunned them in Winston-Salem 113-101 in double overtime, with the Demon Deacons scoring an absurd number against a K team. The Blue Devils' defense, particularly in hostile road environments, was a consistent liability.

The rivalry with North Carolina produced one of the great chapters in the series' 100-year history — fitting, since the 2020 season marked the centennial of Duke-UNC basketball.

At Chapel Hill on February 8, UNC — struggling through a 10-12 season that would have been Roy Williams' worst — led by 13 points with four minutes left in regulation. Vernon Carey Jr. fouled out after scoring 18 first-half points and zero in the second half. Duke was dead. Then Tre Jones took over. He scored 15 consecutive Duke points — a run that stretched from the final minutes of regulation through the opening of overtime. With the game tied at 84 and seconds left in regulation, Jones intentionally missed a free throw, got his own rebound in the scramble, and hit a jumper at the buzzer to send the game to overtime.

Overtime was equally chaotic. UNC led 96-91 with 21 seconds left. Jones hit a layup with 16 seconds remaining, was fouled, and made the first free throw to cut it to 96-94. He missed the second — this time unintentionally — but Moore tipped the rebound out to Jordan Goldwire, who gave it back to Jones. Jones drove, got fouled again, made the first to tie it at 96, then missed the second. Moore tipped the rebound again. Jones gathered and shot — an airball. But Moore was exactly where a smart player should be: at the basket. He grabbed the airball and laid it in as the buzzer sounded. Duke 98, North Carolina 96.

The dog pile at center court was almost as amazing as the play itself. At the end, Roy Williams and Mike Krzyzewski embraced and smiled — two legends acknowledging they'd been part of something extraordinary. Exactly eight years earlier, #0 Austin Rivers had hit a buzzer-beater to beat UNC. Now #0 Wendell Moore had done the same. Jones finished with 28 points, six assists, and three steals — one of the defining performances in the history of the rivalry.

The rematch at Cameron on March 7 — Senior Night for DeLaurier, White, Robinson, and Goldwire — was less dramatic but still satisfying: Duke 89, UNC 76. Carey and Jones combined for 48 points. Justin Robinson hit a three-pointer in his final home game, sending Cameron into a roar for a player who embodied everything the brotherhood was supposed to mean.

Duke was 25-6 and the #3 seed in the ACC Tournament, scheduled to play NC State in the quarterfinals on March 12 at the Greensboro Coliseum. The NCAA Tournament was expected to award Duke a #3 or #4 seed. Both tournaments would never be played.

On March 11, 2020, the NBA suspended its season after Utah Jazz center Rudy Gobert tested positive for COVID-19. Within hours, the ACC Tournament was cancelled. Within days, the NCAA Tournament — the entire event, all 67 games — was cancelled for the first time in its history. College basketball's 2019-20 season simply stopped. No conference tournament. No Selection Sunday. No March Madness. No One Shining Moment.

For seniors like Jack White, Javin DeLaurier, and Justin Robinson, there would be no final tournament run. For Tre Jones, who had come back specifically to lead a team through March, there would be no vindication. For Vernon Carey Jr., there would be no NCAA Tournament debut. The season just... ended.

Jones was named ACC Player of the Year and ACC Defensive Player of the Year — becoming the fourth Duke player in five years to win Player of the Year (following Williamson, Bagley, and Allen's conference). Carey was named ACC Rookie of the Year. Both earned first-team All-ACC honors.

Jones declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #41 overall by the San Antonio Spurs. Carey went #32 to the Charlotte Hornets. Cassius Stanley went #54 to the Indiana Pacers. The seniors graduated without ever playing a postseason game.

The 2019-20 Blue Devils will always carry an asterisk — not because their accomplishments were diminished, but because their season was amputated. This was a team that had just swept North Carolina, had won 11 of its last 14 games, and was playing its best basketball of the season when the world shut down. Whether they would have made a deep tournament run or been upset early remains college basketball's greatest unanswerable question of 2020.

What we do know is this: Tre Jones came back, led, and gave Duke a season that — for one February night in Chapel Hill — produced a moment as miraculous as anything in the rivalry's century of history. That alone justified the decision to stay.

rosterHighlights.name: Tre Jones
rosterHighlights.notes: ACC Player of the Year AND ACC Defensive Player of the Year (only 3rd Duke player to win DPOY); first-team All-ACC; scoring jumped from 9.4 to 16.2 ppg; 3PT improved from 26.2% to 36.1%; 28 pts, 6 ast at UNC in buzzer-beater OT win; scored 15 consecutive Duke points across end of regulation and start of OT; drafted #41 by San Antonio Spurs

rosterHighlights.name: Vernon Carey Jr.
rosterHighlights.notes: ACC Rookie of the Year (runaway winner — 70 of 75 votes); first-team All-ACC; 15 double-doubles (2nd nationally among freshmen); Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Award finalist; 20/11 vs Stephen F. Austin; led team in scoring and rebounding; drafted #32 by Charlotte Hornets

rosterHighlights.name: Cassius Stanley
rosterHighlights.notes: Explosive athlete from Sierra Canyon (same school as Bagley); ACC All-Freshman Team; 22 pts at UNC in OT win; fouled out in overtime of the epic Chapel Hill rivalry game; drafted #54 by Indiana Pacers

rosterHighlights.name: Matthew Hurt
rosterHighlights.notes: McDonald's All-American from Rochester, MN; #10 national recruit; 25 pts vs Georgetown; provided floor spacing as stretch-four; would develop into a star as a sophomore

rosterHighlights.name: Wendell Moore Jr.
rosterHighlights.notes: Local product from Concord, NC; hit buzzer-beating putback to beat UNC 98-96 in OT — wearing #0, same number as Austin Rivers' buzzer-beater exactly 8 years earlier; 17/10 (first career double-double) in that game; missed over a month with hand injury

rosterHighlights.name: Javin DeLaurier
rosterHighlights.notes: Team captain; four-year contributor; started 31 games; provided interior toughness and energy; career ended abruptly when COVID cancelled the postseason

rosterHighlights.name: Jack White
rosterHighlights.notes: Australian senior captain; Academic All-ACC; defensive versatility and toughness; career ended without a postseason game due to COVID

rosterHighlights.name: Jordan Goldwire
rosterHighlights.notes: Defensive specialist; grabbed Tre Jones' missed FT tip-out and returned it to Jones in the miraculous UNC sequence; steadily growing into a reliable backcourt presence

rosterHighlights.name: Justin Robinson
rosterHighlights.notes: Walk-on senior who hit a three-pointer on Senior Night vs UNC to bring down the house at Cameron; embodied the Brotherhood ideal; career ended by COVID cancellation

uncGames.story: One of the greatest games in the 100-year history of the rivalry — fitting, as 2020 marked the centennial of Duke-UNC basketball. UNC led by 13 with 4:16 left and Vernon Carey had fouled out after scoring zero second-half points. Duke was dead. Then Tre Jones scored 15 consecutive Duke points. With seconds left in regulation, Jones intentionally missed a free throw, scrambled for his own rebound, and hit a jumper at the buzzer to force overtime. In OT, UNC led 96-91 with 21 seconds left. Jones scored, was fouled, made the first free throw, missed the second. Wendell Moore tipped the rebound out. Jones gathered, shot — an airball. Moore was at the basket, caught it, laid it in as the buzzer sounded. Duke 98, UNC 96. Wearing #0 — the same number Austin Rivers wore for his buzzer-beater exactly eight years earlier. Jones: 28 pts, 6 ast, 3 stl. K and Roy embraced at midcourt, smiling — two legends who'd just witnessed something beyond basketball.

Senior Night for DeLaurier, White, Robinson, and Goldwire. Duke completed the season sweep with a comfortable 89-76 victory. Carey and Jones combined for 48 points. Justin Robinson hit a three-pointer in his final home game — the kind of moment that makes Cameron roar for a walk-on who embodied the brotherhood. It would be the last Duke basketball game before COVID-19 shut down the sports world four days later.

theGame.title: The Lumberjack Miracle: Nathan Bain Ends 150 at Cameron
theGame.headline: Nathan Bain — a Bahamian senior whose family lost everything in Hurricane Dorian — steals the ball and races coast-to-coast for a buzzer-beating layup to end Duke's 150-game nonconference home winning streak
theGame.story: The most stunning upset in Cameron Indoor Stadium history. Stephen F. Austin, 27.5-point underdogs from the Southland Conference, deployed relentless full-court pressure that generated 22 Duke turnovers and scored 64 of their 85 points in the paint. Kevon Harris had 26 points on 11-of-16 shooting. The game went to overtime — the first OT in the 150-game streak — and Duke scored just one basket in five extra minutes while committing six turnovers. With seconds left in OT and the score tied at 83, Tre Jones missed a jumper. Moore rebounded and kicked to Jones, who tried a bounce pass to Matthew Hurt. It was broken up. Nathan Bain — a fifth-year senior from Nassau who'd played his freshman year on the SFA team that upset #8 West Virginia in the 2016 NCAA Tournament, and whose family had lost nearly everything in Hurricane Dorian that past summer — picked up the loose ball and went the length of the floor for a buzzer-beating layup. The nonconference home winning streak, dating to February 26, 2000, was over at 150 games. 'Banners can't beat us tonight,' SFA coach Kyle Keller had told his team. He was right.

accTourney.story: Duke was the #3 seed and scheduled to play NC State in the ACC Tournament quarterfinals at the Greensboro Coliseum on March 12. The tournament was cancelled before Duke's game was played, as the COVID-19 pandemic shut down all college sports. The ACC Tournament had actually begun — the first game (between NC State and Syracuse) was played — before the plug was pulled mid-event.

ncaaTourney.story: The 2020 NCAA Tournament was cancelled entirely — the first time in the event's history. No Selection Sunday. No brackets. No March Madness. Duke was projected as a #3 or #4 seed. For seniors DeLaurier, White, and Robinson, there would be no final tournament run. For Tre Jones, who had returned specifically to lead a team through March, there would be no vindication.

milestones: Stephen F. Austin beats #1 Duke 85-83 in OT at Cameron (November 26) — Nathan Bain's buzzer-beating coast-to-coast layup ends the 150-game nonconference home winning streak that dated to February 26, 2000
Duke was a 27.5-point favorite vs. SFA — the largest upset by point spread in Division I men's basketball in at least 15 seasons

Tre Jones' epic at UNC (February 8): Intentionally misses free throw, gets own rebound, hits game-tying jumper at buzzer to force OT; Wendell Moore's putback at OT buzzer wins it 98-96 — one of the greatest Duke-UNC games ever, played in the rivalry's centennial year
COVID-19 cancels both the ACC Tournament and the NCAA Tournament — first time in history the NCAA Tournament was not held
Tre Jones named ACC Player of the Year and ACC Defensive Player of the Year — Duke's fourth consecutive ACC POY (Allen 2016, Bagley 2018, Williamson 2019, Jones 2020)
Vernon Carey Jr. named ACC Rookie of the Year — Duke's fourth straight (Bagley, Williamson, Barrett, Carey)
Jones improves 3PT shooting from 26.2% (freshman) to 36.1% (sophomore) and scoring from 9.4 to 16.2 ppg
Duke sweeps North Carolina for the first time since 2017-18
Three players drafted: Jones (#41 SAS), Carey (#32 CHO), Stanley (#54 IND)
Season ended abruptly at 25-6 on March 11 — the last Duke basketball game was the March 7 Senior Night win over UNC

unc.story: One of the greatest games in the rivalry's 100-year history — fitting for the centennial season of Duke-UNC basketball. UNC led by 13 with four minutes left in regulation. Vernon Carey Jr. had fouled out. It was over. Then Tre Jones scored 15 straight Duke points. With the game tied and the clock expiring in regulation, Jones intentionally missed his second free throw, got his own rebound, and banked in a prayer that forced overtime. In OT, UNC led 96-91 with 21 seconds left. Jones hit a layup, was fouled, and made the free throw: 96-94. He intentionally missed again. Wendell Moore Jr. — wearing #0, exactly eight years after Austin Rivers' buzzer-beater from the same arena while also wearing #0 — grabbed the rebound and put it back at the buzzer. Duke 98, UNC 96. The dog pile at center court was epic. Roy Williams and K embraced and smiled.

Senior Night for Javin DeLaurier, Jack White, Justin Robinson, and Jordan Goldwire. Less dramatic than the Chapel Hill classic but still satisfying. Tre Jones and Vernon Carey Jr. combined for 48 points. Duke controlled the game throughout, completing the season sweep of UNC for the first time since 2015. It would be the last game played before COVID shut everything down — the ACC Tournament was canceled the following week, and March Madness never happened.
