seasons.tagline: The Bubble, the Quitter, and the Streak That Died
seasons.overview:
The 2020-21 Duke Blue Devils played college basketball in an alternate dimension. Cameron Indoor Stadium — the most electric home court in America — sat empty. The players lived in a hotel, isolated from the campus and the student body. Classes were on Zoom. Games were constantly cancelled, postponed, or rescheduled. COVID-19 testing protocols meant that a single positive result could shut down the entire program overnight. And for the first time in a quarter century, Duke was not going to the NCAA Tournament.

The season was K's 41st, and by every measure it was his worst. Duke finished 13-11 overall and 9-9 in ACC play — tenth in the conference, the lowest finish of the Krzyzewski era. The Blue Devils were never ranked after the first two weeks. They lost to Michigan State at home in December, lost to Illinois, and then voluntarily cancelled the rest of their nonconference schedule to allow players to go home for the holidays. When ACC play began, the inconsistency was staggering: a seven-game stretch from late January through mid-February saw Duke go 2-5, including losses to Miami, Notre Dame, and Louisville — teams that would finish in the middle or bottom of the conference.

The roster had talent but never cohesion. Sophomore Matthew Hurt was the team's best player, emerging as one of the ACC's most dangerous stretch-fours. His shooting improved dramatically from freshman year — he averaged 18.3 points per game on 44.4% from three, the highest scoring average by a Duke player since Grayson Allen in 2015-16. He led the entire ACC in scoring and won the conference's Most Improved Player award, the first Duke player ever to receive the honor. His 37-point game against Louisville was Duke's highest individual output since JJ Redick in 2006.

Freshman Jalen Johnson had been the class's highest-rated recruit — a 6-9 forward from Nicolet High School in Wisconsin with guard skills and projected lottery-pick potential. He opened the season with 19 points and 19 rebounds against Coppin State, a debut that suggested a Zion-level impact. Then it unraveled. Johnson suffered a foot injury in December, missed three games, and was never the same after returning. His minutes decreased. He moved from the starting lineup to the bench. Off-court tension between his camp and the coaching staff simmered. On February 15, Johnson announced he was opting out of the remainder of the season to prepare for the NBA Draft, leaving the team at 8-8 and in the middle of a losing streak.

The reaction was severe. Roy Williams, when asked about the concept of players opting out, said what many were thinking — that opting out meant quitting. Duke fans were even less charitable. Johnson's departure exposed the fragility of the one-and-done model when things aren't going well: the brotherhood only holds when winning is easy. K, to his credit, was publicly supportive. 'I'm a coach because of players,' he said. 'These kids should have the choice to do whatever they want. I'm 100% behind him.'

Johnson would be drafted #20 by the Atlanta Hawks and has since developed into an All-Star caliber player — vindicating, in some sense, the talent evaluation while leaving the questions about his Duke tenure unanswered.

The freshmen who stayed — DJ Steward (a dynamic scoring guard from Chicago), Jeremy Roach (a steady point guard from Virginia who would become a four-year starter), and Mark Williams (a 7-1 center from Norfolk, Virginia who barely played early but emerged late as a rim protector) — showed growth in the second half. Sophomore Wendell Moore Jr. was inconsistent but gutsy, and Jordan Goldwire provided his usual defensive energy as a senior.

The rivalry with North Carolina produced a split. At Cameron on February 6, Duke fell 91-73 in an empty arena — one of the most lopsided losses in the rivalry's recent history, made surreal by the silence where the Cameron Crazies should have been. UNC's Garrison Brooks and Caleb Love torched Duke, and the game was never competitive after halftime. The rematch at Chapel Hill on March 6 was closer — UNC won 91-87 in a game Duke very nearly stole late, with Steward and Moore making it a one-possession game in the final seconds before UNC closed it out.

Duke entered the ACC Tournament needing a run to have any chance at an at-large bid. They beat Boston College 86-51 in the first round, with Steward, Roach, and Goldwire all playing well simultaneously for perhaps the first time all season. They beat Louisville 70-56 the next day — impressive, given that the Cardinals had beaten them twice in the regular season. For a few hours, hope flickered.

Then, on Thursday morning — hours before Duke's quarterfinal against Florida State — a member of the program's Tier 1 personnel tested positive for COVID-19. Contact tracing protocols kicked in. Duke withdrew from the tournament. Athletic director Kevin White released a statement: 'Since last March when the pandemic started, we have listened to our medical experts and always put safety at the forefront of any determinations regarding competition. As a result, this will end our 2020-21 season.'

Selection Sunday came and went. Duke's name was not called. The 24-year consecutive NCAA Tournament appearance streak — the second-longest active streak in college basketball, the third-longest in history — was over. Duke declined an NIT invitation. The season was done.

For Krzyzewski, it was a humbling coda to a stretch of years that had produced Zion, Barrett, and the most culturally significant team in college basketball history. For the program, it was a reminder that even the most successful machine can break down when the circumstances are hostile enough. COVID took away the fans, the energy, the normalcy — and with it, the connective tissue that makes a Duke season feel like a Duke season.

Hurt declared for the NBA Draft (selected #37 by the Memphis Grizzlies, traded to the Dallas Mavericks). Steward declared (#undrafted, signed a two-way deal). Johnson went #20 to Atlanta. Goldwire transferred to Oklahoma for a fifth year. Moore, Roach, Baker, and Williams all returned — and the returning core would form the foundation of something much bigger the following season, when K would announce his retirement and Duke would play for a national championship.

uncGames.story: One of the most surreal games in rivalry history — played in an empty Cameron Indoor Stadium, with no Cameron Crazies, no noise, no energy. UNC led wire-to-wire, building a 20-point second-half lead as Garrison Brooks and the Tar Heels dominated inside. Duke had no answer without crowd energy or defensive intensity. An 18-point loss at home to UNC would have been unthinkable in any normal year. This was not a normal year.

uncGames.location: Dean E. Smith Center (Chapel Hill — limited fans)

But UNC closed it out for the season sweep — the Tar Heels' first sweep of Duke since 2017-18. For Duke's seniors, it was the last regular-season game of a strange, painful year.

theGame.title: The COVID Withdrawal: A Season Ends in a Test Result
theGame.headline: A positive COVID-19 test within Tier 1 personnel forces Duke to withdraw from the ACC Tournament hours before its quarterfinal, ending the season and a 24-year NCAA Tournament appearance streak
theGame.story: Duke had beaten Boston College and Louisville on consecutive days — the best the Blue Devils had looked all season, with Hurt scoring efficiently and the freshman guards finally clicking. For a few hours, hope flickered that Duke might make a tournament run. Then, on Thursday morning, hours before the quarterfinal against Florida State, a Tier 1 member tested positive. Contact tracing kicked in. Duke withdrew. Kevin White's statement was clinical: 'This will end our 2020-21 season.' Selection Sunday came and went without Duke's name. The 24-year consecutive NCAA Tournament appearance streak — dating to 1996 — was over. Duke declined the NIT. For the first time since 1995, there would be no March Madness for the Blue Devils. 'After going an entire season with no positive COVID-19 tests among our student-athletes and coaching staff,' White said, 'one member of our program tested positive following Wednesday's ACC Tournament game.' One positive test. One season-ending result.

accTourney.story: Duke's best performance of the season. Steward led with 17 points, Goldwire hit a career-high 4 three-pointers, and the guards finally clicked simultaneously. A 35-point blowout that gave the Blue Devils life.

Duke handled a Louisville team that had beaten them twice in the regular season. Hurt and Williams led the way as Duke's tournament momentum continued to build. For a few hours, the bubble conversation was real.

Hours before tipoff against Florida State, a Tier 1 member tested positive. Duke withdrew from the tournament and announced the end of its season. The 24-year NCAA Tournament streak died not on the court, but in a testing facility.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke missed the NCAA Tournament for the first time since 1995, ending the second-longest consecutive appearance streak in college basketball history at 24 years. The Blue Devils declined an NIT invitation.

unc.story: One of the most surreal games in rivalry history. An empty Cameron Indoor Stadium — no students, no Crazies, no atmosphere. Just sneaker squeaks echoing through 9,314 empty seats. And UNC took full advantage, delivering one of the most lopsided rivalry wins in recent memory. The 18-point defeat was a microcosm of Duke's worst season under K: the COVID bubble had drained the team's identity. Without the Cameron crowd, Duke had no sixth man. UNC controlled the paint and never let Duke get comfortable.

unc.location: Dean E. Smith Center (Chapel Hill, limited fans)
unc.story: UNC completed the sweep in a season where both arenas felt like ghost towns. The Tar Heels were the better team from start to finish, and the nine-point margin actually flattered Duke. The loss dropped the Blue Devils to 11-11 on the season. It was the first UNC sweep since 2016 and a fitting indignity for a season that would end without a tournament appearance for the first time since 1995.

seasons.tagline: The Last Dance: Coach K's Farewell, Paolo's Arrival, and the Rivalry's Ultimate Chapter
seasons.overview:
On June 2, 2021, Duke University announced that Mike Krzyzewski would retire following the 2021-22 season. After 42 years, five national championships, 13 Final Fours, 1,196 victories, 101 NBA Draft picks, and a transformation of Duke from a good program into arguably the greatest dynasty in college basketball history, Coach K would coach his final game. Associate head coach Jon Scheyer was named head coach in waiting.

The farewell season became the most emotionally charged year in Duke basketball history. Every road game was a goodbye. Every opponent wanted to be the team that beat K one last time. The pressure on a roster built around freshmen — as Duke rosters had been for a decade — was immense. And the freshman who shouldered more of that weight than anyone was Paolo Napoleon Banchero.

Banchero arrived from O'Dea High School in Seattle as the #2 recruit in the country — a 6-10, 250-pound forward whose combination of perimeter skill, post footwork, and passing vision drew comparisons to a young Carmelo Anthony. He was joined by AJ Griffin (#7 nationally, a 6-6 wing from Ossining, New York whose father Adrian had played in the NBA), Trevor Keels (#22 nationally, a physical guard from the Bronx via Paul VI Catholic in Virginia), and Jaylen Blakes. The returning core included junior Wendell Moore Jr. (now the team's emotional leader and best all-around player), sophomore Mark Williams (the 7-1 rim protector who'd emerged late the previous season), sophomore Jeremy Roach, and senior Joey Baker. Graduate transfer Theo John (a center from Marquette) added physicality. Senior walk-on Michael Savarino, K's grandson, was also on the roster.

Duke opened the season at the Champions Classic and immediately announced this team was different from the 13-11 disaster that preceded it. Banchero scored 22 points on 7-of-11 shooting in an eight-point win over Kentucky. Two weeks later, Duke beat #1 Gonzaga 84-81 behind Banchero's 21 points and three three-pointers — a victory over Chet Holmgren, the only player ranked higher than Paolo in his class. The Blue Devils climbed to #1 in the AP poll.

A loss at Ohio State in late November dropped them from the top spot, but Duke regrouped and won its next eight. By February, the team had found its identity: Banchero's scoring and playmaking, Williams' rim protection (he would average 2.2 blocks per game and shoot 72.7% from the field), Moore's all-around contributions (he led the team in assists with 4.4 per game), and Roach's steady floor generalship. Griffin provided instant offense off the bench (or in the starting lineup), shooting 44.7% from three on the season.

Duke won the ACC regular season championship outright — the program's first since 2006, ending a 16-year drought. But late-season turbulence foreshadowed what was to come. A home loss to Virginia on a buzzer-beating three-pointer by Reece Beekman ended a 10-game winning streak. And then came March 5, 2022.

Coach K's final home game at Cameron Indoor Stadium.

More than 90 former Blue Devils players returned for the pregame ceremony. NBA Commissioner Adam Silver sat courtside. Jerry Seinfeld was in attendance. Tickets reached five-figure prices. The Cameron Crazies were operating at a decibel level that threatened to lift the roof. K fought back tears during the national anthem. Jon Scheyer patted his mentor's shin before the opening tip.

None of it mattered. North Carolina — unranked, a month removed from a 20-point loss to Duke in Chapel Hill — walked into Cameron and played fearlessly. UNC shot 59% after halftime. Armando Bacot scored 23. R.J. Davis added 21. Brady Manek hit 20. Caleb Love overcame an 0-for-8 start to score 15 of his 22 after halftime. It was the first time in UNC program history that four players scored 20 or more in the same game. By the final minutes, UNC had stretched the lead to 13 and the Cameron Crazies sat in stunned silence.

Final: North Carolina 94, Duke 81.

K grabbed the microphone for the postgame ceremony. 'I'm sorry about this afternoon,' he began, waving off the crowd's cheers. 'Today was unacceptable, but the season has been very acceptable. And the season isn't over.'

He was right. Duke entered the ACC Tournament as the #1 seed, beat Syracuse and Miami before losing to Virginia Tech 82-67 in the championship game. Then came the NCAA Tournament.

As a #2 seed in the West Region, Duke dispatched Cal State Fullerton 78-61 (Banchero: 17/10 in his tournament debut) and then survived Michigan State 85-76 in a game that was tighter than the final score suggested. In the Sweet 16, Banchero put up 22 points on 7-of-12 shooting against the nation's #1 defense (Texas Tech) as Duke advanced 78-73. In the Elite Eight, Duke handled Arkansas 78-69 behind a balanced effort — six players scored at least nine points. K was going to his 13th Final Four, breaking his tie with John Wooden for the most in men's college basketball history. He was the first coach to reach a Final Four in five different decades.

And waiting in New Orleans was North Carolina.

The rivals had played 257 times in 102 years. They had never met in the NCAA Tournament. The brackets, the rivalry, and K's retirement had conspired to produce the most anticipated college basketball game in modern history. Duke-Carolina in the Final Four, with K's career on the line.

The game lived up to every impossible expectation. The first half was a slugfest — Duke led 37-34 at the break, with both teams playing with a desperation and intensity that transcended a normal basketball game. In the second half, the teams traded three-pointers and leads. Duke led by as many as five. UNC answered every run.

With 1:47 left, Duke trailed 75-74. Roach drove and scored to put Duke up 76-75. Then Caleb Love — the same guard who'd torched Duke in Cameron — pulled up from 30 feet and buried a three-pointer that pushed UNC ahead 78-76. The Superdome erupted. Duke couldn't answer. Love scored 28 points for the game. Bacot had 11 points and 21 rebounds. Banchero finished with 20 points and 10 rebounds in his final college game.

Final: North Carolina 81, Duke 77.

In the locker room afterward, K's players wept. 'I had a locker room full of guys crying,' K said. 'It's not about me, especially right now. As a coach, I'm just concerned about these guys.'

Banchero, fighting tears: 'He was so committed to us, never made it about him. He made me a better player and a better man.'

Mike Krzyzewski walked off the floor of the Caesars Superdome for the last time with a career record of 1,202-361 — the most victories in men's Division I basketball history. Five national championships. Thirteen Final Fours. Ninety-seven NCAA Tournament victories. Three Olympic gold medals. A legacy that will never be replicated.

The 2022 NBA Draft was historic for Duke. Five Blue Devils were selected — a program record: Banchero went #1 overall to the Orlando Magic (the fifth Duke player taken first, after Brand, Irving, Williamson, and no one else had that distinction with four), Mark Williams went #15 to the Charlotte Hornets, AJ Griffin went #16 to the Atlanta Hawks, Wendell Moore Jr. went #26 to the Dallas Mavericks (traded to the Minnesota Timberwolves), and Trevor Keels went #42 to the New York Knicks.

The season ended with heartbreak, but it was the only ending that could have matched the magnitude of the story. Duke and North Carolina, in the Final Four, for the first time ever, with the greatest coach in college basketball history walking off the court for the last time. K had said all year that he didn't want the farewell to be about him. In the end, it was about exactly what it should have been about — the rivalry, the players, and the game itself.

North Carolina went on to lose to Kansas in the national championship game, 72-69. Duke fans were left with a question that will echo through decades: What if Love's three had been an inch to the left? What if Roach's drive had put Duke up three instead of one? What if the greatest coach had gotten the Hollywood ending?

But March doesn't deal in Hollywood endings. It deals in reality. And the reality is that K's final season gave us the greatest Duke-Carolina game ever played — not at Cameron, not at the Dean Dome, but on the biggest stage in the sport, with everything at stake. That it ended in heartbreak doesn't diminish it. It elevates it to mythology.

Coach K's final record: 1,202-361. Five titles. Thirteen Final Fours. Forty-two years at Duke. The Brotherhood he built will outlast him. It already has.

uncGames.story: Duke dominated from start to finish in Chapel Hill, winning by 20 in what appeared to be a thorough dismantling of UNC. The Blue Devils' pressure defense suffocated Caleb Love and R.J. Davis, who struggled mightily. Banchero was efficient and Williams controlled the paint. It felt like a statement — Duke was on another level. One month later, the Tar Heels would prove that impression spectacularly wrong.

Adam Silver, Jerry Seinfeld, and thousands of alumni packed Cameron for tickets reaching five figures. K fought back tears during the anthem. None of it mattered. UNC shot 59% after halftime. Bacot scored 23, R.J. Davis 21, Manek 20, Love 22 — the first time in UNC history four players scored 20+. By the final minutes, the Crazies sat in stunned silence as the Tar Heels stretched the lead to 13. K grabbed the microphone afterward: 'I'm sorry about this afternoon. Today was unacceptable, but the season has been very acceptable. And the season isn't over.' First unranked UNC team to beat a top-5 Duke team in Cameron since 1990.

Now they would meet in the Final Four, with K's career in the balance. The game matched every impossible expectation. Duke led 37-34 at halftime. The second half was a masterpiece of rivalry basketball — traded threes, traded leads, traded punches. Duke led by five. UNC answered. With 1:47 left, Roach drove and scored to put Duke up 76-75. Then Caleb Love pulled up from 30 feet and buried the three that broke Duke's heart: 78-76 UNC. Duke couldn't answer. Love: 28 points. Bacot: 11/21. Banchero: 20/10 in his final college game. 'He made me a better player and a better man.'

theGame.title: The Last Game: Love's Three Ends the K Era in New Orleans
theGame.location: Caesars Superdome (New Orleans, LA) — NCAA Final Four Semifinal
theGame.headline: Caleb Love's 30-foot three-pointer with 1:26 left puts UNC ahead for good as North Carolina ends Coach K's career in the first-ever Duke-UNC NCAA Tournament meeting
theGame.story: Two hundred and fifty-seven previous meetings across 102 years, and never once in the NCAA Tournament. Until now. The Final Four. K's farewell. The Superdome in New Orleans. The game of all games. Duke led 37-34 at halftime behind Banchero's 12 first-half points and Williams' paint dominance. The second half was a war — traded threes, traded leads, a combined 81 second-half points between the two teams. With 1:47 left, Roach drove past his defender and scored to put Duke ahead 76-75. The Superdome tilted toward Durham. Then Caleb Love — the same junior guard who'd torched Duke in Cameron a month earlier — took a dribble past half-court, rose from 30 feet, and buried the three-pointer that ended an era. UNC 78, Duke 76. The building erupted. Duke never led again. Love hit 28 points. Bacot had 11 points and 21 rebounds. Banchero finished with 20 and 10 in his final college game. After the buzzer, K walked off the floor of the Superdome. In the locker room, his players wept. 'I had a locker room full of guys crying,' K said. 'It's not about me, especially right now. As a coach, I'm just concerned about these guys.' Banchero, fighting tears: 'He was so committed to us, never made it about him. He made me a better player and a better man.' K's final record: 1,202-361. Five national championships. Thirteen Final Fours. The most victories in men's Division I history. Forty-two years at Duke. The greatest college basketball coaching career ever.

accTourney.story: Banchero had 10/10/7 in the quarterfinal. Duke's offense clicked early and the Blue Devils pulled away in the second half against Jim Boeheim's zone.
Banchero shot 8-of-10 from the field for 18 points and 11 rebounds. Duke survived a late Miami push to reach the ACC championship game.

Virginia Tech, led by Hunter Cattoor's hot shooting, overwhelmed Duke 82-67 in the ACC championship game. A concerning loss — the Blue Devils looked flat and disjointed, raising questions about their ability to sustain tournament intensity heading into March Madness.

ncaaTourney.story: Banchero's NCAA Tournament debut: 17 points and 10 rebounds. Duke handled the #15 seed without drama, though the first half was tighter than expected.
Duke survived Michigan State and Tom Izzo in a game that was closer than the final score suggested. Banchero had 19/7/4. A career-ending matchup for Izzo vs. K added extra weight to the contest.

Against the nation's #1 defense, Banchero scored 22 on 7-of-12 shooting with 3-of-4 from three. Duke's offense carved up Texas Tech's vaunted defensive system. The most impressive win of the tournament run.

K's 13th Final Four — breaking his tie with John Wooden for the most in men's college basketball history. Six Duke players scored at least nine points in a balanced effort. Banchero had 16/7/3. After the game, Banchero called K 'the GOAT' during the broadcast interview. K told him to shut up. Both were smiling.
The first Duke-UNC NCAA Tournament game in 257 meetings across 102 years. K's final game. Love's three from 30 feet. The end of an era. The greatest rivalry game ever played.

milestones: Mike Krzyzewski announces retirement on June 2, 2021 — 42nd and final season at Duke; Jon Scheyer named head coach in waiting
Duke beats #1 Gonzaga 84-81 behind Banchero's 21 points — establishing the farewell team as a legitimate contender
Duke wins first outright ACC regular-season championship since 2006 (16-4 ACC, first in conference)
UNC spoils Coach K's final home game at Cameron, 94-81 — first unranked UNC team to beat a top-5 Duke in Cameron since 1990; 90+ former players, Adam Silver, and Jerry Seinfeld in attendance
K reaches 13th Final Four, breaking tie with John Wooden for most in men's college basketball history; first coach to reach a Final Four in five different decades
Duke and North Carolina meet in the NCAA Tournament for the first time in 257 games across 102 years — in the Final Four with K's career at stake
Caleb Love's 30-foot three-pointer with 1:26 left in the Final Four semifinal puts UNC ahead for good — ending K's career with a 77-81 loss
K's final career record: 1,202-361 — most victories in men's Division I basketball history
Program-record five players selected in 2022 NBA Draft: Banchero (#1 ORL), Williams (#15 CHA), Griffin (#16 ATL), Moore (#26 DAL→MIN), Keels (#42 NYK)
Paolo Banchero becomes fifth Duke player taken #1 overall (after Brand, Irving, Williamson) — and the first since Zion in 2019
UNC goes on to lose the national championship game to Kansas, 72-69 — Duke fans left with the ultimate what-if

unc.story: K's final trip to Chapel Hill was a demolition. Paolo Banchero led with 18 points and the Blue Devils hit 12 three-pointers — their most ever against UNC — in a 20-point rout. Duke shot 53% and dominated the glass 35-24. The Blue Devils built a 47-24 halftime lead that rendered the second half meaningless. It was a vintage K performance: disciplined, relentless, and utterly dominant on the road. UNC coach Hubert Davis, in his first season, watched helplessly.

unc.location: Cameron Indoor Stadium (K's Last Home Game)
unc.story: The most emotional game in Cameron history turned into a nightmare. More than 90 former Duke players returned. NBA Commissioner Adam Silver was there. Jerry Seinfeld was there. The arena crackled with 42 years of gratitude — and then unranked UNC spoiled everything. The Tar Heels shot 59% after halftime. Armando Bacot had 11 points and 21 rebounds. Caleb Love scored 22. UNC dominated 38-20 in the final 10:30. K told the crowd: 'I'm sorry about this afternoon. Today was unacceptable, but the season isn't over.' He was right — but the sting of this loss never fully healed.

unc.location: Caesars Superdome (New Orleans, NCAA Final Four)
unc.story: The greatest game in the greatest rivalry, played on the greatest stage. Duke and UNC had met 257 times in 102 years but never in the NCAA Tournament. K's retirement and the brackets had conspired to produce the most anticipated college basketball game ever played. The first half was a slugfest — Duke led 37-34 at the break. In the second half, both teams played with an intensity that transcended normal basketball. With 1:47 left, Jeremy Roach drove for a layup to put Duke up 76-75. Then Caleb Love — the same guard who'd torched Duke at Cameron — pulled up from 30 feet and buried it: 78-76 UNC. Duke never led again. Final: North Carolina 81, Duke 77. K walked off the floor for the last time. UNC lost to Kansas in the championship two days later, leaving Duke fans with a question that will echo through decades: What if Love's three had been an inch to the left?

seasons.accTournament: Champions (22nd title — beat Pittsburgh, Miami, Virginia; first person in ACC history to win tournament as both player and coach)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #5 seed East — Round of 32 (beat Oral Roberts 74-51; lost to #4 Tennessee 52-65)
seasons.tagline: New Sheriff, Same Standards: Scheyer's Baptism by Fire and ACC Tournament Redemption
seasons.overview:
For the first time since 1980, someone other than Mike Krzyzewski coached Duke basketball. Jon Scheyer — the Northbrook, Illinois native who'd captained the 2010 national championship team, joined K's staff in 2013, and been named head coach in waiting in June 2021 — took the reins of a program that had just played for a national championship with 42 years of institutional gravity behind it. He inherited a roster that bore almost no resemblance to the one that had reached the Final Four: Jeremy Roach was the lone returning starter. Seven freshmen joined the team. The expectations, impossibly, remained the same.

The freshman class was ranked #1 nationally. Dereck Lively II (the consensus #1 recruit, a 7-1 center from Westtown School in Pennsylvania) was the headliner — a rim-protecting, alley-oop-finishing defensive anchor whose blocked shots would become the team's signature. Kyle Filipowski (#3 nationally, a 6-11 stretch center from Wilbraham, Massachusetts) provided the offensive counterpart — skilled in the post with an expanding perimeter game. Dariq Whitehead (#4 nationally, a 6-6 wing from Newark, New Jersey) was supposed to be the team's best perimeter scorer, but suffered a right foot fracture in August that kept him out until late December. Mark Mitchell (#19 nationally, a 6-8 forward from Kansas City) brought athleticism and versatility. Tyrese Proctor, an Australian guard who reclassified from the 2023 class, joined Roach in the backcourt. Graduate transfer Jacob Grandison (from Illinois) and senior Ryan Young provided depth.

The season was a rollercoaster that tested Scheyer's resolve at every turn. Duke opened ranked #7, beat #1-ranked Oregon State-bound Gonzaga — wait, no. Let me correct: Duke opened with a loss to Kansas 69-64 at the Champions Classic, with both Whitehead and Lively still injured. They beat Purdue 75-56 without those two as well, showing defensive promise. By early December, they'd lost to Illinois too. The early results without key players suggested a team still finding itself.

When Lively returned in late December, the defense transformed. By February, Duke was 16-0 at Cameron Indoor Stadium — including a signature performance against North Carolina on February 4 that crystallized the team's identity. Lively had 8 blocks and 14 rebounds, Roach scored 20, and Duke's suffocating defense held UNC to 57 points in a 63-57 victory that felt like a statement of program continuity under the new coach.

But the road was a different story. Duke lost at Wake Forest, NC State, Clemson, Virginia Tech, and Miami (an 81-59 blowout). The most painful road loss came at Virginia, where Duke appeared to have a go-ahead dunk from Filipowski that was controversially disallowed, leading to a heartbreaking overtime defeat. The Blue Devils simply could not replicate their Cameron dominance away from home.

On February 15, Jalen Johnson — wait, that was 2020-21. In this season, the controversy was different: road inconsistency and the pressure of following a legend. Scheyer navigated it with a maturity that surprised skeptics. His defensive coaching was elite — Duke led the ACC in blocked shots during conference play (4.9 per game), with Lively leading all freshmen nationally at 2.4 blocks per game despite averaging just 20.6 minutes. His emphasis on ball movement created stretches of offense that were, at times, breathtaking — a clear departure from K's more iso-heavy late-era approach.

The regular season ended with a sweep of UNC — Duke beat the Tar Heels 62-57 in Chapel Hill on March 4 behind a 22-point effort from Filipowski, with Proctor and Mitchell both scoring in double digits. Not a single player from the previous year's team returned for this rematch, and yet Duke swept the rivalry. It was Scheyer's first signature accomplishment: proving that the program's identity transcended any individual roster.

The ACC Tournament in Greensboro became the crowning achievement of year one. Duke entered as the #4 seed and demolished Pittsburgh 96-69 in the quarterfinal — a 27-point blowout where Scheyer emptied the bench. In the semifinal against #1-seed Miami — the same team that had beaten Duke 81-59 during the regular season — the Blue Devils shot 50% from three and 84% from the foul line. Dariq Whitehead, finally healthy after his August foot fracture, scored key late first-half points. Duke won 85-78.

The championship game against Virginia was a defensive masterpiece. After the gut-wrenching overtime loss at Charlottesville that could have broken the team's spirit, Duke held the Cavaliers to 49 points — Virginia's lowest output of the season — and won 59-49. The Blue Devils claimed the program's 22nd ACC Tournament championship, and Scheyer became the first person in conference history to win the tournament as both a player (2010) and a head coach.

Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as a #5 seed in the East Region, riding a 10-game winning streak — the longest by a power-conference team entering March Madness. They handled #12 Oral Roberts 74-51, with Roach scoring a career-high tying 23 points and Lively pulling down 12 rebounds with 6 blocks.

Then came Tennessee.

The fourth-seeded Volunteers brought a level of physicality that Duke's freshmen simply weren't ready for. Within the first two minutes, Uros Plavsic had committed two offensive fouls against Filipowski — and within six minutes, Filipowski was on the bench receiving medical attention for a bleeding wound under his eye. Tennessee's Olivier Nkamhoua scored a career-high 27 points, including 13 straight during a decisive second-half run. Duke shot 6-of-22 from three and managed just 52 points. Mark Mitchell, Duke's starting small forward, missed the game with a knee injury suffered in practice the day before. The Vols won 65-52.

'We ran out of gas down the stretch,' Scheyer said. 'You have to really work for everything in a game like this.'

The loss stung, but the season told a larger story. Scheyer had taken a team with seven freshmen, three significant injuries (Whitehead's foot, Roach's toe, Lively's early-season conditioning), and the impossible weight of following the most successful coach in college basketball history — and produced a 27-9 record, an ACC Tournament championship, and an NCAA Tournament appearance. Not a single player from the Final Four roster transferred. The culture held.

Lively declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #12 by the Dallas Mavericks, where he immediately became a key rotation player. Whitehead went #18 to the Chicago Bulls (traded to the Atlanta Hawks). Mitchell considered the draft but returned. And in a development that would define Scheyer's early tenure, every other significant player — Filipowski, Roach, Proctor, Baker, Mitchell — came back. Zero players entered the transfer portal. For a program that had spent a decade cycling through one-and-done rosters, the retention was unprecedented.

The returning core would form the foundation of the 2023-24 team that reached the Elite Eight. The Scheyer era had begun — imperfect, physical, and absolutely alive.

rosterHighlights.notes: Lone returning starter and team captain; career-high tying 23 pts vs Oral Roberts in NCAA Tournament; 20 pts vs UNC at Cameron; steadiest player on a young roster; All-ACC Honorable Mention

rosterHighlights.notes: Australian guard who reclassified from 2023 class; 92nd percentile defensively nationally; 16 pts vs Tennessee in NCAA Tournament (team high); steadily improved as season progressed; would become a multi-year starter

rosterHighlights.notes: #4 national recruit; missed first 12 games with August foot fracture; when healthy, shot 68.7% from three over one electric stretch in February; 6 key points in ACC Tournament semifinal vs Miami; drafted #18 by Chicago (traded to Atlanta)

uncGames.story: The game that defined Scheyer's defensive identity. Dereck Lively II had 8 blocks and 14 rebounds — a performance that screamed future NBA lottery pick. Roach scored 20 points. Proctor contributed on both ends. Duke held UNC to 57 points, suffocating the Tar Heels' offense with length and rim protection. It was the clearest evidence that Duke's defensive culture had survived the coaching transition. Lively's highlight blocks brought Cameron to its feet and announced the Scheyer era's signature: defense first.

Duke completed the sweep of UNC — at Chapel Hill, no less — behind Filipowski's 22-point effort. Proctor and Mitchell both scored in double figures. The defense was again elite, holding UNC to 57 points for the second time. Not a single player from the previous year's Final Four appeared on either roster, and yet Duke dominated the rivalry. It was Scheyer's first major statement: the program transcends personnel.

theGame.title: Scheyer's Coronation: Duke Claims the ACC Championship in Year One
theGame.headline: Scheyer becomes the first person in ACC history to win the tournament as both a player and a head coach, holding Virginia to its lowest output of the season
theGame.story: After a devastating overtime loss at Virginia earlier in the season — where a controversial disallowed dunk could have changed the outcome — Duke got its revenge in the most important game. The Blue Devils held the Cavaliers to 49 points, Virginia's lowest output of the season, in a defensive masterpiece that validated everything Scheyer had built in year one. Duke claimed its 22nd ACC Tournament championship, and Scheyer made history: the first person to win the ACC Tournament as both a player (2010, when he captained K's title team) and a head coach. The win capped a 10-game winning streak that included demolishing Pittsburgh, beating a Miami team that had embarrassed Duke during the regular season, and now shutting down Virginia. 'You guys were winners,' K would tell this team at the season-ending banquet — high praise from the man whose shadow Scheyer was supposed to be trapped in.

accTourney.story: Duke blew out Pittsburgh 96-69, jumping to a 12-0 lead before the Panthers blinked. Four Blue Devils hit double figures. Scheyer emptied the bench. An emphatic start to the tournament run.

Revenge for the 81-59 regular-season blowout. Duke shot 50% from three and 84% from the foul line. Whitehead scored 6 key points late in the first half. Duke's shooting, which had been inconsistent all year, peaked at exactly the right time.

A defensive masterpiece. Duke held Virginia to 49 points — the Cavaliers' worst offensive output of the season. Scheyer became the first person in ACC history to win the tournament as both a player and a head coach. Duke's 22nd ACC Tournament title.

ncaaTourney.story: Roach scored a career-high tying 23 points. Lively had 12 rebounds and 6 blocks. Duke snapped Oral Roberts' 17-game winning streak — the longest in Division I at the time — in a resounding 23-point victory in Orlando.

Tennessee brought a physicality that Duke's freshmen weren't ready for. Filipowski was bloodied within six minutes — a cut under his eye from a rebounding scuffle. Mitchell missed the game with a knee injury. Nkamhoua scored 27 for the Vols, including 13 straight during a decisive second-half run. Duke shot 6-of-22 from three and scored just 52 points. The Scheyer era's first NCAA Tournament ended with a lesson: freshmen, no matter how talented, need experience to survive March's most physical moments.

unc.story: Scheyer's first Duke-UNC game at Cameron was a defensive slugfest. Dereck Lively II was the story — the 7-1 freshman delivered 8 blocks and 14 rebounds, swatting everything UNC sent toward the rim. Kyle Filipowski added 16 points and the Blue Devils held UNC to 35% shooting. Neither team shot well, but Duke's length and rim protection were the difference. The win kept Cameron unbeaten on the season at 16-0.

Filipowski led the way with 22 points as Duke completed its first sweep of UNC since Scheyer had been a player on the 2010 national championship team. Tyrese Proctor and Mark Mitchell both scored in double digits. The five-point margin was deceptive — Duke controlled the game's tempo throughout, never trailing in the second half. The sweep was a statement for Scheyer's program: the rivalry still tilted blue.

seasons.accTournament: Quarterfinal loss to NC State 69-74 (Filipowski 28/14 in defeat; lost to the eventual Cinderella)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #4 seed South — Elite Eight (beat Vermont, James Madison, #1 Houston; lost to #11 NC State 64-76)
seasons.tagline: McCain's Breakout, Houston's Fall, and the Exodus That Followed
seasons.overview:
Jon Scheyer's second season was built on the foundation his first had laid — and this time, the pieces fit from the start. The unprecedented roster retention from 2022-23 gave Duke something it hadn't had in over a decade: continuity. Sophomore Kyle Filipowski returned as a preseason All-American, the first Duke player to come back after earning first-team All-ACC honors since Grayson Allen in 2017. Junior Jeremy Roach was the team's steadying veteran, entering his fourth year as a rotation player and his third as a starter. Sophomore Tyrese Proctor and sophomore Mark Mitchell both returned with refined games. And the incoming freshman class brought Jared McCain (#14 nationally, a 6-3 shooting guard from Centennial High School in Corona, California with an enormous social media following and an even bigger shot), Caleb Foster (#23 nationally, a point guard from Notre Dame High School in Chattanooga via the same Paul VI Catholic pipeline as Roach and Keels), TJ Power, and Sean Stewart.

The season began ranked #8 and Duke quickly established itself as a contender, beating Kentucky in the Champions Classic yet again and climbing into the top 10. But the team's rhythm took time. McCain averaged just 7.6 points over his first eight games, adjusting to the speed and physicality of college basketball. Then something clicked. From mid-December forward, McCain became one of the most dangerous perimeter shooters in the country — averaging 15.5 points the rest of the way, hitting 41.4% of his three-pointers on 5.8 attempts per game, and showing a fearless shot-making ability that would eventually produce historic results in March.

Filipowski was the constant. The 7-footer improved his efficiency across the board — shooting 50.5% from the field and 34.8% from three, nearly doubling his assists, and establishing himself as a consensus second-team All-American. He scored his 1,000th career point at Wake Forest, becoming just the 12th Blue Devil to reach that milestone in two seasons. His combination of scoring versatility, passing, and shot-blocking made him the most complete player in the ACC.

The rivalry with UNC produced two painful losses. At Chapel Hill on February 3, #3 North Carolina beat #7 Duke 93-84 — Harrison Ingram posted a 21-point, 13-rebound double-double with four steals, and the Tar Heels' physical defense forced 11 Duke turnovers. At Cameron on March 9 — Senior Night for the program — UNC returned and won again, 84-79, clinching the outright ACC regular-season title on Duke's home floor. Cormac Ryan scored a career-high 31 points, shooting 6-of-8 from three. After the final buzzer, UNC players gathered near the Cameron Crazies section and waved goodbye in a taunting farewell — prompting fans to throw water bottles and plastic cups. Scheyer apologized to the front row of Crazies afterward: 'I'm sorry. We're going to keep going, we're going to get this right.'

Retired Coach K, making a rare courtside appearance for the Senior Night game, watched from the stands as his former rival's program celebrated on his court.

The ACC Tournament brought a rematch with NC State in the quarterfinals, and it foreshadowed what was to come. Filipowski was brilliant — 28 points on 13-of-20 shooting with 14 rebounds — but Duke lost 74-69 as DJ Horne hit a late basket and the Wolfpack's defensive pressure overwhelmed Duke's guards. It was the start of NC State's remarkable eight-game winning streak that would carry them all the way to the Final Four.

Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as a #4 seed in the South Region, carrying some uncertainty after back-to-back losses. What followed was a March run that validated the entire season. Against Vermont in the Round of 64, Filipowski grabbed 12 rebounds despite attempting just one shot, and Duke advanced comfortably. Against James Madison in the Round of 32, Jared McCain erupted — shooting 8-of-14 from three-point range (breaking Duke's single-game NCAA Tournament three-point record) en route to 30 points in a 93-55 demolition. It was the first of two historic tournament performances.

The Sweet 16 brought #1-seed Houston at the American Airlines Center in Dallas. The Cougars were the regional favorites and one of the best defensive teams in the country. Duke matched their physicality. Graduate center Ryan Young — a player who had been an afterthought for much of the season — posted a remarkable +18 plus/minus and provided interior toughness that changed the game. Filipowski had 16 points. Duke won 54-51 in an absolute grind — the kind of defensive slugfest that Duke teams of the one-and-done era rarely won. It was the program's biggest tournament upset since beating #1 overall seed Gonzaga would have been — actually, it was just a phenomenal win against the tournament's #1 seed.

The Elite Eight brought NC State. Again. And this time, the Wolfpack were playing with destiny's hand on their shoulder. Duke led 27-21 at halftime behind another hot start from McCain (11 straight points early). But NC State's DJ Burns — the 275-pound senior who'd become March's most unlikely star — erupted for 29 points on 13-of-19 shooting. The Wolfpack outscored Duke 55-37 in the second half. Filipowski fouled out with 4:52 remaining. Proctor went scoreless on 0-of-9 shooting. The only Duke player who showed fight was McCain, who finished with 32 points — tying Zion Williamson's Duke freshman NCAA Tournament scoring record. He became the first Duke freshman to score 30+ twice in a single NCAA Tournament.

Final: NC State 76, Duke 64. The Wolfpack — an 11-seed that had been 17-14 before their magical run began — went on to the Final Four.

'This is not easy,' Scheyer said afterward. 'We never had a rhythm on offense.'

What followed in April was seismic. McCain and Filipowski declared for the NBA Draft (McCain went #16 to the Philadelphia 76ers; Filipowski went #32 to the Utah Jazz). Then came the transfer portal exodus: a program-record seven players entered the portal — Jeremy Roach (to Baylor), Mark Mitchell (to Missouri), Sean Stewart (to Ohio State), TJ Power (to Virginia), Jaylen Blakes (to Stanford), Christian Reeves (to Clemson), and Jaden Schutt (to Virginia Tech). Combined with Ryan Young's eligibility expiring, Duke was left with just two returning scholarship players who had scored a single point: Tyrese Proctor and Caleb Foster.

The exodus was unprecedented in Duke history and raised fundamental questions about the transfer portal era's impact on program continuity. The zero-portal-departure retention of 2022-23 — the story that had defined Scheyer's first year — was obliterated in his second. The roster that had reached the Elite Eight was scattered across nine different programs and two NBA teams within a month of the season ending.

But Scheyer had already planned for this. His 2024 recruiting class, headlined by the consensus #1 overall recruit Cooper Flagg, was ready. The Scheyer era was about to enter a new phase entirely.

rosterHighlights.name: Kyle Filipowski
rosterHighlights.notes: Consensus second-team All-American (AP, USBWA, NABC); first-team All-ACC for second straight year; 1,000th career point at Wake Forest (12th Duke player to reach milestone in two seasons); 28/14 vs NC State in ACC Tournament; South Regional All-Tournament Team; fouled out in Elite Eight; drafted #32 by Utah Jazz

rosterHighlights.name: Jared McCain
rosterHighlights.notes: ACC All-Freshman Team; All-ACC Honorable Mention; first Duke freshman to score 30+ twice in a single NCAA Tournament (30 vs James Madison, 32 vs NC State — tying Zion's tournament freshman scoring record); broke Duke's single-game NCAA Tournament 3PT record (8 vs JMU); massive social media following; drafted #16 by Philadelphia 76ers (became immediate NBA impact player)

rosterHighlights.name: Jeremy Roach
rosterHighlights.notes: Four-year starter and team captain; 100th career start vs Louisville; steadying veteran presence; 13 pts in Elite Eight; transferred to Baylor after the season — ending the longest Duke tenure of the one-and-done era

rosterHighlights.name: Tyrese Proctor
rosterHighlights.notes: Australian guard; led team in assists (3.7); improved defender; scoreless on 0-of-9 shooting in Elite Eight vs NC State — worst game of his career at the worst possible time; one of only two players to return for 2024-25

rosterHighlights.name: Mark Mitchell
rosterHighlights.notes: Breakout sophomore season; shooting 58.1% from field and 50% from three after January 1; career-highs of 23 pts and 14 reb vs Notre Dame; transferred to Missouri after the season

rosterHighlights.name: Ryan Young
rosterHighlights.notes: Graduate center who became an unlikely NCAA Tournament hero; +18 plus/minus in Sweet 16 upset of #1 Houston — Duke's most impactful player in that game; provided interior toughness the freshmen couldn't; eligibility expired

rosterHighlights.name: Caleb Foster
rosterHighlights.notes: Point guard from Paul VI Catholic (same pipeline as Roach/Keels); showed flashes but missed NCAA Tournament with ankle stress fracture; one of only two players to return for 2024-25

rosterHighlights.name: Sean Stewart
rosterHighlights.notes: Athletic freshman forward who provided energy off the bench; significant minutes in Elite Eight; transferred to Ohio State after the season

rosterHighlights.name: TJ Power
rosterHighlights.notes: Freshman forward who saw limited minutes; transferred to Virginia after the season

uncGames.story: UNC dominated from start to finish. Harrison Ingram posted a 21-point, 13-rebound double-double with four steals, and Armando Bacot added a team-high 25 points. Duke's guards struggled against the Tar Heels' physical defense, committing 11 turnovers. The Blue Devils fought back in spurts but never led. The first of two painful rivalry losses that would define Duke's regular-season narrative.

UNC clinched the outright ACC regular-season title — its first since 2016-17 — on Duke's Senior Night. Cormac Ryan scored a career-high 31 points (6-of-8 from three), RJ Davis added 19. Filipowski had 23 in a losing cause. After the buzzer, UNC players waved goodbye to the Cameron Crazies, prompting fans to throw water bottles and plastic cups. theGame.title: McCain's 32 Aren't Enough: NC State's Cinderella Ends Duke's Season in Dallas
theGame.location: American Airlines Center (Dallas, TX) — NCAA South Regional Final
theGame.headline: DJ Burns scores 29 as the #11-seed Wolfpack — who'd also beaten Duke in the ACC Tournament — rout the Blue Devils in the second half, 55-37, to reach the Final Four
theGame.story: Duke led 27-21 at halftime, holding NC State to its second-lowest first-half output of the season. McCain had 11 straight early points and the defense looked championship-caliber. Then DJ Burns happened. The 275-pound senior, who'd become March's most unlikely folk hero, scored 29 points on 13-of-19 shooting in a second-half demolition. NC State outscored Duke 55-37 after the break. Filipowski fouled out with 4:52 remaining. Proctor went scoreless on 0-of-9 shooting — the worst game of his Duke career, at the worst possible time. Only McCain fought: his 32 points tied Zion Williamson's Duke freshman NCAA Tournament scoring record. He became the first Duke freshman to post two 30-point games in a single tournament. But it wasn't enough. The Wolfpack — 17-14 before their magical run began — were headed to the Final Four. Duke was headed into the most dramatic offseason in program history.

accTourney.story: A foreshadowing. Filipowski was sensational — 28 points on 13-of-20 shooting with 14 rebounds — but NC State's pressure overwhelmed Duke's guards. DJ Horne hit a key late basket, and the Wolfpack's Cinderella run began. Duke lost 74-69 despite Filipowski's best game of the season.

ncaaTourney.story: A grinding defensive victory. Filipowski grabbed 12 rebounds despite attempting just one shot. Duke held Vermont to 47 points in a controlled, physical performance that set the tone for the tournament run.

Jared McCain's coming-out party. The freshman shot 8-of-14 from three — breaking Duke's single-game NCAA Tournament three-point record — en route to 30 points in a 38-point demolition. Duke's most dominant tournament performance in years.

The signature win of Scheyer's tenure to date. Duke upset #1-seed Houston in a defensive masterpiece. Graduate center Ryan Young was the unlikely hero, posting a +18 plus/minus while providing interior toughness. Filipowski had 16 points. A 54-51 grind that proved these Blue Devils could win the kind of physical, low-scoring game that one-and-done teams historically cannot.

Duke led 27-21 at halftime but NC State's DJ Burns (29 pts, 13-19 FG) took over in the second half. The Wolfpack outscored Duke 55-37 after the break. Filipowski fouled out. Proctor went 0-of-9. McCain scored 32 (tying Zion's freshman tournament record) but Duke shot 32.2% in the second half. NC State to the Final Four; Duke to the most dramatic offseason in program history.

milestones: Unprecedented roster retention from 2022-23 gives Duke its most continuity in over a decade — Filipowski, Roach, Proctor, Mitchell all return
Filipowski earns consensus second-team All-American honors — first Duke sophomore to achieve the distinction since 2016
Filipowski scores 1,000th career point at Wake Forest — 12th Duke player to reach the milestone in two seasons
UNC sweeps Duke and clinches outright ACC regular-season title on Duke's Senior Night; UNC players wave goodbye to Cameron Crazies, prompting fans to throw water bottles
McCain breaks Duke's single-game NCAA Tournament three-point record (8) in 30-point effort vs James Madison
McCain becomes first Duke freshman to score 30+ twice in a single NCAA Tournament (30 vs JMU, 32 vs NC State), tying Zion Williamson's freshman tournament scoring record
Duke upsets #1-seed Houston 54-51 in Sweet 16 — Ryan Young posts +18 plus/minus as unlikely hero
NC State beats Duke twice in March (ACC Tournament quarterfinal, NCAA Elite Eight) en route to the Final Four as an 11-seed

Program-record seven players enter the transfer portal after the season: Roach (Baylor), Mitchell (Missouri), Stewart (Ohio State), Power (Virginia), Blakes (Stanford), Reeves (Clemson), Schutt (Virginia Tech)
McCain (#16 76ers) and Filipowski (#32 Jazz) declare for NBA Draft — McCain becomes immediate NBA impact player
Only two scholarship players who scored a point return for 2024-25: Proctor and Foster — setting the stage for the Cooper Flagg rebuild

unc.story: Harrison Ingram posted a 21-point, 13-rebound double-double with four steals, and RJ Davis scored 23 as UNC overwhelmed Duke with physicality and depth. Filipowski had 20 points but Duke's defense couldn't contain UNC's multi-pronged attack. Armando Bacot and Cormac Ryan combined for 25 more. The nine-point loss snapped Scheyer's unbeaten run against UNC and signaled the Tar Heels were legitimate ACC title contenders.

Cormac Ryan was unstoppable — the senior poured in 31 points on 6-of-8 three-point shooting as UNC clinched at least a share of the ACC regular-season title on Duke's home floor. Filipowski had 23 but couldn't match Ryan's brilliance. As the final buzzer sounded, UNC players waved goodbye to the Cameron Crazies. Enraged fans threw water bottles. Scheyer: 'I'm sorry. We're going to get this right.' Retired Coach K, making a rare courtside appearance for the Senior Night game, watched from the stands.

seasons.accTournament: Champions (23rd title — most in ACC history — beat Georgia Tech, North Carolina, Louisville; won semifinal and final without Cooper Flagg)
seasons.ncaaTournament: #1 seed East — Final Four (beat Mount St. Mary's, Baylor, Arizona, Alabama; lost to #1 Houston 67-70)
seasons.tagline: Flagg's Masterpiece, the 33-Second Nightmare, and 97.1%
seasons.overview:
The 2024-25 Duke Blue Devils were the best team in college basketball for 39 minutes and 27 seconds of their final game. Then they became the sport's most devastating cautionary tale about how March doesn't care about talent, rankings, or win probability.

The season began with a near-complete roster overhaul. After the program-record transfer portal exodus of spring 2024, only Tyrese Proctor and Caleb Foster returned as scholarship players who had scored a point. Scheyer rebuilt through the transfer portal (Maliq Brown from Syracuse, Sion James from Tulane, Mason Gillis from Purdue, Cameron Sheffield from Rice) and a recruiting class headlined by the most hyped freshman prospect since Zion Williamson: Cooper Flagg.

Flagg, a 6-9 forward from Newport, Maine, via Montverde Academy, had reclassified from the Class of 2025 and was the unanimous #1 recruit in the country. He'd been named to the 2024 USA Basketball Men's Select Team to train alongside NBA players preparing for the Paris Olympics — the only teenager and first collegian since 2013. At 17, he was already being discussed as the probable #1 pick in the 2025 NBA Draft. He arrived at Duke carrying expectations that rivaled Zion's — and met them.

Flagg scored 18 in his debut against Maine, dropped 26 and 11 in a close loss to Kentucky at the Champions Classic, and put up 24 and three blocks in a 14-point win over Arizona. A loss at Kansas — where Flagg had five turnovers including one on a potential go-ahead possession with 48 seconds left — was Duke's second and final regular-season loss away from Cameron. From there, Duke went on a tear. They finished 17-0 at Cameron Indoor Stadium and 19-1 in ACC play, winning the regular-season championship by a wide margin.

Kon Knueppel, a 6-7 freshman from Milwaukee via the same Wisconsin basketball circles that produced Jalen Johnson, emerged as Duke's second-most important player — a floor-spacer, playmaker, and relentless competitor who shot 37.9% from three and routinely dove for loose balls in games already decided. Khaman Maluach, the 7-2 South Sudanese center, provided elite rim protection and finished at 71.2% from the field. Proctor, in his third year, matured into a reliable scorer and defender. Brown, the Syracuse transfer, was the ACC Defensive Player of the Year and ACC Sixth Man of the Year.

The rivalry with North Carolina was one-sided for the first time in years. Duke beat UNC 87-70 at Cameron on February 1 — Flagg had 21 points, eight rebounds, and seven assists, becoming just the third Duke player to post a 20/7/7 line against the Tar Heels. On Senior Night at Cameron on March 8, Duke won 76-61, with Flagg overcoming three first-half fouls to post a season-high four blocks. The Blue Devils had now won five straight in the rivalry.

Flagg won every major award: ACC Player of the Year, ACC Rookie of the Year (the fifth Duke freshman to sweep both, after Okafor, Bagley, Williamson, and now Flagg), the Wooden Award, the Naismith Award, and consensus first-team All-American. His 709 points ranked third among Duke freshmen all-time. He set Duke's freshman record for free throws made (179). His 12 ACC Rookie of the Week selections set a conference record. He was a 12-time weekly award winner and the only player in ACC history to sweep both weekly awards five times in one season.

Duke entered the ACC Tournament as the #1 seed and immediately lost Flagg to an ankle sprain in the quarterfinal against Georgia Tech. The Blue Devils won that game 78-70 anyway, then faced UNC in the semifinals without Flagg or Maliq Brown. Duke led by 24 in the first half before UNC mounted a furious 23-point comeback, pulling within one at 72-71 with 32 seconds left. UNC's Ven-Allen Lubin went to the free-throw line with a chance to give the Tar Heels the lead — but missed the first, and Jae'Lyn Withers committed a lane violation on the second, wiping it off. Knueppel hit two free throws. Lubin's desperation three at the buzzer missed. Duke 74, UNC 71. First 3-0 sweep of UNC since 2001-02.

Duke beat Louisville 73-62 in the championship game — again without Flagg — behind Proctor's 19 points on six three-pointers and Knueppel's 18. The program's 23rd ACC Tournament title, the most in conference history.

As the #1 seed in the East Region, Duke rolled through the NCAA Tournament. Mount St. Mary's fell 93-49 (Flagg: 18 pts, two-handed dunk highlight). Baylor fell 89-66 (Flagg: 18/9/6). Arizona fell 100-93 in a thrilling Sweet 16 rematch (Flagg: 22/4/4/3 steals, including 3-of-4 from three against the nation's #1 defense). Alabama fell 85-65 in the Elite Eight (Flagg: 16/9 with suffocating defense that held the country's best offense to 65 points). Duke was averaging 91.8 points per tournament game. They were the #1 overall seed, the #1 adjusted offense in the country, and the #1 adjusted defense. They were headed to the Final Four.

On April 5, 2025, at the Alamodome in San Antonio, Duke faced #1-seed Houston with a national championship game berth at stake. Flagg had won the Wooden Award hours earlier. The coronation was supposed to be Monday night.

For 30 minutes, it looked exactly like it should. Duke led 34-28 at halftime behind Flagg and Knueppel. In the second half, Duke extended the lead to 14 — 56-42 with 11:54 remaining. Flagg was everywhere: scoring, passing, blocking shots, rebounding. He was having the kind of game that ends with confetti and net-cutting.

Then Duke stopped scoring.

One field goal in the final 10 minutes and 31 seconds. One. The nation's #1 offense, the team averaging 83.7 points per game, produced a single basket in over ten minutes of basketball. Houston chipped away. Still, with 2:30 remaining, Duke's win probability was 97.1%. They led 64-55.

With 1:14 left, Duke led 67-61 and had the ball. A Knueppel free throw on a technical foul pushed it to 67-61. Then everything collapsed — in exactly 33 seconds.

Emanuel Sharp hit a three-pointer from the right wing after Flagg and Sion James miscommunicated on a ball-screen switch: 67-64. Duke's inbound pass was knocked away by Mylik Wilson. JoJo Tugler dunked: 67-66. Proctor missed the front end of a one-and-one. Flagg was called for a controversial over-the-back foul on the rebound, sending J'Wan Roberts — a career 58.9% free-throw shooter who'd been practicing 150 foul shots a night — to the line. Roberts made both: Houston 68, Duke 67.

Duke had one last chance. Scheyer drew up the play he wanted: Flagg, with the ball, in space, near the basket. Flagg drove to his spot — a mid-range pull-up jumper about six feet from the rim, the shot he'd hit dozens of times all season. J'Wan Roberts contested it. The ball hit the front of the rim. LJ Cryer made two free throws. Houston 70, Duke 67.

'It's the play coach drew up,' Flagg said afterward, tears in his eyes. 'Took it into the paint. Thought I got my feet set, rose up. Left it short obviously. A shot I'm willing to live with in that scenario.'

Scheyer: 'For the rest of my life, I'll have no regrets with #2 with the ball 6 feet from the basket.'

Flagg finished with 27 points, seven rebounds, four assists, three blocks, and two steals — the first player since defensive stats became official in 1986 to lead his team in all five categories in a Final Four game. He was named to the NCAA All-Tournament Team despite not playing in the championship game.

Houston went on to beat Florida for the national championship. Duke finished 35-4 — one of six teams in program history to win 35+ games, the first since the 2014-15 national champions. They were the first team in ACC history to lead the conference in both scoring offense (83.2 ppg) and scoring defense (62.8 ppg allowed) in the same season. Their opponents shot 38.4% from the field, the best defensive percentage by a Duke team since 1960.

Flagg declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #1 overall by the Dallas Mavericks — the second-youngest #1 pick in draft history behind only LeBron James. Maluach went #10 to the Houston Rockets (traded to Phoenix). Knueppel went in the first round. The Scheyer era had produced its masterpiece — 35 wins, an ACC championship won without its best player, a Final Four, and a freshman who will be remembered as one of the greatest single-season players in college basketball history.

But 33 seconds. 97.1% win probability. One field goal in 10:31. The shot that hit the front of the rim. These are the numbers that will haunt Duke fans the way Allen's floater in 2018 and Love's three in 2022 do — the millimeters between legend and heartbreak, measured in the cruelest sport on earth.

rosterHighlights.name: Cooper Flagg
rosterHighlights.notes: Consensus #1 recruit; unanimous first-team All-American; Wooden Award and Naismith Award winner; ACC Player of the Year AND Rookie of the Year (5th Duke freshman to sweep both); 709 pts (3rd among Duke freshmen all-time); broke Duke freshman record for FT made (179); 42 pts vs Pittsburgh (3rd Duke player to score 42+ at Cameron); 27/7/4/3 blk/2 stl in Final Four vs Houston — first player since 1986 to lead team in all 5 categories in a Final Four game; NCAA All-Tournament Team; drafted #1 overall by Dallas Mavericks (2nd-youngest #1 pick in draft history behind LeBron)

rosterHighlights.name: Kon Knueppel
rosterHighlights.notes: ACC All-Freshman Team; clutch free throw shooter (made both with 2.8 seconds left to seal UNC ACC semifinal win); 22 pts vs UNC at Cameron; 18 pts in ACC championship game vs Louisville; 16 pts in Final Four vs Houston; relentless competitor who dove for every loose ball; first-round NBA Draft pick

rosterHighlights.name: Khaman Maluach
rosterHighlights.notes: 7-2 center from South Sudan; ACC All-Freshman Team; 84% FG in NCAA Tournament (21-of-25); 13/9 in ACC semifinal vs UNC without Flagg; elite rim protector; infectious personality that fueled team chemistry; drafted #10 by Houston Rockets (traded to Phoenix Suns)

rosterHighlights.name: Tyrese Proctor
rosterHighlights.notes: Three-year starter; 19 pts on six three-pointers in ACC championship game vs Louisville; matured into one of ACC's most complete guards; missed front end of 1-and-1 in final minute of Houston game — a devastating miss in the context of the collapse; 31 steals (up from 22 in each of first two years)

rosterHighlights.name: Maliq Brown
rosterHighlights.notes: ACC Defensive Player of the Year; ACC Sixth Man of the Year; led ACC in steals per game; scored 26 vs Duke as a Syracuse player the previous year, now anchored Duke's defense; missed ACC semifinal and final with injury

rosterHighlights.name: Sion James
rosterHighlights.notes: Athletic wing who provided scoring and defense; 15 pts in ACC championship game; miscommunication with Flagg on the Sharp three-pointer in the 33-second Houston collapse; valuable rotation piece all season

rosterHighlights.name: Caleb Foster
rosterHighlights.notes: Returned from injury; provided steady backcourt minutes and floor spacing; one of only two returning players from 2023-24

rosterHighlights.name: Mason Gillis
rosterHighlights.notes: Big Ten Sixth Man of the Year at Purdue the previous season; elite three-point shooter; provided veteran spacing and physicality off the bench

rosterHighlights.name: Isaiah Evans
rosterHighlights.notes: Sharpshooting freshman wing; provided instant offense off the bench; key contributor when Flagg drew defensive attention

uncGames.story: Duke's most dominant rivalry performance in years. Flagg was responsible for scoring or assisting on the first 18 Duke points, finishing with 21 points, eight rebounds, and seven assists — becoming just the third Duke player to post a 20/7/7 line against UNC. Knueppel added 22. Duke led by as many as 30 and controlled every phase of the game. The Blue Devils were simply on another level.

Duke completed the regular-season sweep at Chapel Hill. Flagg overcame three first-half fouls to play all 20 second-half minutes, posting 15 points, nine rebounds, six assists, and a career-high four blocks. The Blue Devils led by as many as 15 in the first half, weathered a UNC surge in the second, then put the game away with a 20-2 run. Duke's fifth straight victory over UNC.

Duke played without Flagg (ankle sprain) and Brown (injury) and led by 24 in the first half — closing on a 15-0 run. Then UNC mounted a 23-point comeback, pulling within one at 72-71 with 32 seconds left. Lubin had two free throws to give UNC the lead. He missed the first. Withers committed a lane violation on the second, wiping it off. Knueppel made two free throws with 2.8 seconds left. Lubin's desperation three at the buzzer missed. Duke 74, UNC 71. First 3-0 sweep of UNC since 2001-02 — and they did it without their best player.

theGame.title: 33 Seconds: Houston's Impossible Comeback Ends Duke's Championship Dream
theGame.location: Alamodome (San Antonio, TX) — NCAA Final Four Semifinal
theGame.headline: Duke leads 67-61 with 1:14 left — 97.1% win probability at the 2:30 mark — but Houston scores 9 unanswered in the final 33 seconds to end Flagg's college career
theGame.story: For 30 minutes, this was a coronation. Flagg had won the Wooden Award hours earlier. Duke led by 14 with 11:54 remaining. The nation's #1 offense and #1 defense was cruising to a championship game berth. Then Duke stopped scoring. One field goal in the final 10:31. One. With 2:30 left, Duke's win probability was 97.1%. They led 64-55. With 1:14 left, they led 67-61. Then 33 seconds happened. Emanuel Sharp hit a three after Flagg and James miscommunicated on a switch: 67-64. Wilson knocked away the inbound pass. Tugler dunked: 67-66. Proctor missed the front end of a 1-and-1. Flagg was called for a controversial over-the-back foul on the rebound. Roberts — a career 58.9% free-throw shooter who'd been practicing 150 a night — made both: Houston 68, Duke 67. Flagg drove to his spot on the final possession. Roberts contested. The pull-up jumper hit the front of the rim. Cryer made two free throws. Houston 70, Duke 67. Flagg: 27/7/4/3 blocks/2 steals — the first player since 1986 to lead his team in all five statistical categories in a Final Four game. 'Left it short obviously,' Flagg said, tears in his eyes. 'A shot I'm willing to live with.' Scheyer: 'For the rest of my life, I'll have no regrets with #2 with the ball 6 feet from the basket.'

accTourney.story: Duke beat Georgia Tech but lost something far more valuable: Cooper Flagg twisted his ankle on a rebound and was diagnosed with a severe sprain. He would miss the remainder of the ACC Tournament.

Without Flagg or Brown, Duke led by 24 in the first half before UNC staged a 23-point comeback. Lubin's potential go-ahead free throws were negated — one missed, one wiped by a lane violation. Knueppel's clutch free throws and Lubin's miss at the buzzer sealed it. First 3-0 sweep of UNC since 2001-02.

Still without Flagg, Duke won its 23rd ACC Tournament title — the most in conference history. Proctor scored 19 on six three-pointers. Knueppel added 18. Scheyer had now won two ACC Tournament titles in three years, both times without his best player available for multiple games.

ncaaTourney.story: Flagg returned from his ankle sprain and Duke rolled, winning by 44 points. Flagg had 18 and a highlight two-handed dunk that confirmed he was fully healthy.
Another blowout. Flagg had 18 points, nine rebounds, and six assists. Duke's defense was suffocating, and the Blue Devils looked like the best team in the country.

A thrilling rematch with Arizona. Flagg scored 22 with four assists, three steals, and three blocks — going 3-of-4 from three against the nation's top defense. Duke hit 100 points in the NCAA Tournament for the first time under Scheyer. The most entertaining game of the tournament's first two weekends.

Duke held the country's best offense to 65 points. Flagg had 16 and 9 alongside suffocating defense. The Blue Devils looked invincible — four tournament wins by an average of 24.5 points, scoring 91.8 per game. A Final Four berth for the first time since 2022.

97.1% win probability with 2:30 left. Led by 14 with 11:54 remaining. One field goal in the final 10:31. Houston's 9-0 run in the final 33 seconds — Sharp's three, Wilson's steal, Tugler's dunk, Proctor's missed free throw, Flagg's controversial foul, Roberts' two free throws — turned a coronation into a collapse. Flagg's final shot hit the front of the rim. Houston 70, Duke 67. The most devastating final minute in Duke history since Love's three in 2022.

unc.story: Cooper Flagg announced himself as the rivalry's newest protagonist with 21 points, eight rebounds, and seven assists — becoming just the third Duke freshman to record a 20-7-7 line against UNC. Duke's defense was suffocating, holding UNC to 37% shooting. Kon Knueppel added 15 and Khaman Maluach controlled the paint with 12 points on near-perfect shooting. The 17-point win was Duke's most decisive home victory over UNC since the 2010 season.

Flagg collected 15 points, nine rebounds, six assists, and four blocks despite playing with three first-half fouls. Duke's defense held UNC to just 27 second-half points, fueled by a stretch where the Tar Heels went nearly 11 minutes without a made field goal. Maliq Brown chipped in 15 points, nine rebounds, and five steals. The 15-point margin in Chapel Hill completed the first Duke sweep of UNC in the regular season since 2022-23.

Duke completed the 3-0 sweep of UNC — the first since 2001-02 — and they did it without Cooper Flagg, who had sprained his ankle in the quarterfinal against Georgia Tech. Kon Knueppel made two clutch free throws with 2.8 seconds left to seal the three-point win. On UNC's final possession, a lane violation on a free throw negated what would have been the tying point. Tyrese Proctor led Duke with 19 points on six three-pointers. The win proved Duke could survive and win without its best player.
